独异志 - Records of Singular Marvels

    Home

    《独异志》唐 李亢

    　　●序
    　　《独异志》者，记世事之独异也。自开辟以来迄于今世之经籍，耳目可见闻，神仙鬼怪，并所摭录。然有纪载所繁者，俱不量虚薄，构成三卷。愿传博达，所贵解颜耳。

    　　●卷上
    　　伊尹无父，生于空桑中，禹妻化为石，后剖腹而生启。老君耳长七尺，在母腹中八十一年，剖左胁而生，及生，鬓发皓白。徐偃王无骨而有圣德。刘邕好食人疮痂。文王四乳。皋陶鸟喙。尧眉八彩。汤臂四肘。禹耳三漏。离娄察见秋毫于十里之外。卫臣弘演开已腹纳懿公之肝。周穆贵为天子，车辙马迹遍于天下，凡游行一亿一万里。
    　　海若居海岛，每至八月即有流槎过。如是，累年不失期。其人赍粮乘槎而往，及至一处，见有人饮牛于河，又见织女，问其处，饮牛之父曰：“可归问蜀严君平，当知之。”其人归，诣君平。君平曰：“某年月日，有客星犯斗牛，计时，即汝也。”其人乃知随流槎至天津。 [1]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [1]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 《独异志》者，记世事之独异也 - A formal introduction explaining that *The Book of Unique Events* records extraordinary events in the world.
    • 自开辟以来迄于今世之经籍 - A phrase indicating that the book covers events from the beginning of time to the present day.
    • 耳目可见闻 - Means 'what can be seen and heard,' referring to observable and audible phenomena.
    • 神仙鬼怪 - Refers to immortals, ghosts, and monsters, common subjects in Chinese folklore.
    • 纪载所繁者 - Means 'those that are extensively recorded,' referring to well-documented events.
    • 构成三卷 - Means 'composed into three volumes,' indicating the structure of the book.
    • 愿传博达，所贵解颜耳 - Means 'I hope it will be passed on to the learned, for the sake of bringing smiles,' expressing the author's wish for the book to be appreciated.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 伊尹无父，生于空桑中 - Refers to Yi Yin, a famous minister of the Shang dynasty, who was said to have been born without a father and from a mulberry tree.
    • 禹妻化为石，后剖腹而生启 - Refers to the myth of Yu the Great's wife transforming into a stone and giving birth to Qi (启) by cutting open her belly.
    • 老君耳长七尺 - Refers to Laozi (老子), the founder of Daoism, who is said to have had ears seven feet long, a symbol of wisdom.
    • 徐偃王无骨而有圣德 - Refers to Xu Yanzhong (徐偃王), a legendary king known for his virtue despite being boneless.
    • 刘邕好食人疮痂 - Refers to Liu Yong (刘邕), a person who had a peculiar habit of eating scabs, which is considered unusual and unhygienic.
    • 文王四乳 - Refers to Wen Wang (文王), a virtuous ruler of the Zhou dynasty, who is said to have had four breasts, a sign of his exceptional nature.
    • 皋陶鸟喙 - Refers to Gao Yao (皋陶), a legendary judge of the Xia dynasty, who had a beak-like mouth, a physical anomaly.
    • 尧眉八彩 - Refers to Yao (尧), a legendary ruler, whose eyebrows were said to have eight colors, symbolizing his divine qualities.
    • 汤臂四肘 - Refers to Tang (汤), a famous ruler of the Shang dynasty, who is said to have had four elbows on each arm, a physical anomaly.
    • 禹耳三漏 - Refers to Yu the Great, who had three holes in his ears, a physical anomaly.
    • 离娄察见秋毫于十里之外 - Refers to Li Lou (离娄), a legendary figure with extremely sharp eyesight, able to see fine details from a great distance.
    • 卫臣弘演开已腹纳懿公之肝 - Refers to the story of a minister of the State of Wei who cut open his own belly to hide the liver of Duke Yi Gong (懿公), a ruler who was overthrown.
    • 周穆贵为天子，车辙马迹遍于天下 - Refers to King Mu of Zhou (周穆王), a legendary ruler whose travels were so extensive that his chariot tracks and horse prints were said to cover the entire world.
    • 海若居海岛，每至八月即有流槎过 - Refers to the myth of Hai Ruo (海若), a sea deity, and the annual appearance of a log raft in the sea during the eighth month of the lunar calendar.
    • 其人赍粮乘槎而往 - Means 'the person carried provisions and went aboard the raft,' describing the journey.
    • 饮牛于河 - Means 'drinking water from the river,' a simple act that provides context for the setting.
    • 织女 - Refers to the Weaving Maid, a celestial figure in Chinese mythology.
    • 可归问蜀严君平，当知之 - Means 'you can return and ask Shu's Yan Junping, he will know,' suggesting a way to find out more information.
    • 某年月日，有客星犯斗牛 - Refers to the appearance of a guest star (a comet or nova) in the constellations of Dou (斗) and Niu (牛), a significant astronomical event.

    Proper Nouns

    • 《独异志》 - The title of the book, *The Book of Unique Events*.
    • 李亢 - The author of the book, Li Kang.
    • 伊尹 - Yi Yin, a famous minister of the Shang dynasty.
    • 禹 - Yu the Great, a legendary ruler known for his flood control efforts.
    • 老君 - Laozi, the founder of Daoism.
    • 徐偃王 - Xu Yanzhong, a legendary king.
    • 刘邕 - Liu Yong, a person with a peculiar habit.
    • 文王 - Wen Wang, a virtuous ruler of the Zhou dynasty.
    • 皋陶 - Gao Yao, a legendary judge of the Xia dynasty.
    • 尧 - Yao, a legendary ruler.
    • 汤 - Tang, a famous ruler of the Shang dynasty.
    • 离娄 - Li Lou, a legendary figure with sharp eyesight.
    • 卫臣弘演 - A minister of the State of Wei.
    • 懿公 - Duke Yi Gong, a ruler of the State of Wei.
    • 周穆王 - King Mu of Zhou, a legendary ruler.
    • 海若 - Hai Ruo, a sea deity.
    • 严君平 - Yan Junping, a scholar from Shu.
    • 织女 - The Weaving Maid, a celestial figure.

    The Book of Unique Events by Li Kang of the Tang Dynasty

    Preface
    The Book of Unique Events records the extraordinary occurrences in the world. From the beginning of time to the present day, it encompasses all that can be seen and heard, including immortals, ghosts, and monsters. While there are many extensively recorded events, I have compiled them into three volumes without considering my own limitations. I hope this work will be passed on to the learned, for the sake of bringing smiles.

    Volume One
    Yi Yin was born without a father and emerged from a mulberry tree. Yu the Great's wife transformed into a stone and gave birth to Qi by cutting open her belly. Laozi had ears seven feet long and was born after eighty-one years in his mother's womb, emerging from her left side. At birth, he had white hair and a full beard. Xu Yanzhong, the King of Xu, had no bones but possessed great virtue. Liu Yong had a peculiar habit of eating human scabs. Wen Wang, a virtuous ruler of the Zhou dynasty, had four breasts. Gao Yao, a legendary judge of the Xia dynasty, had a beak-like mouth. Yao, a legendary ruler, had eyebrows with eight colors. Tang, a famous ruler of the Shang dynasty, had four elbows on each arm. Yu the Great had three holes in his ears. Li Lou, a legendary figure with sharp eyesight, could see fine details from a distance of ten miles. A minister of the State of Wei cut open his own belly to hide the liver of Duke Yi Gong, a ruler who was overthrown. King Mu of Zhou, a legendary ruler, traveled so extensively that his chariot tracks and horse prints were said to cover the entire world, a total distance of one hundred million miles.

    On the islands of the sea, Hai Ruo, a sea deity, resides. Every August, a log raft appears. This has happened year after year without fail. One person carried provisions and went aboard the raft, and upon reaching a place, he saw someone drinking water from the river and the Weaving Maid. He asked the man where he was, and the man replied, "You can return and ask Shu's Yan Junping; he will know." The person returned and consulted Yan Junping. Yan Junping said, "On a certain day in a certain month, a guest star appeared in the constellations of Dou and Niu. Calculating the time, it was you." The person then realized he had followed the log raft to the Heavenly River. [1]

    娥皇、女英从舜巡狩，行及湘川，闻舜崩于苍梧，泣下，泪洒湘川之竹，皆成斑文。
    　　公孙吕面长三尺，阔三寸，为卫国贤臣。
    　　东汉赵伯翁尝昼寝，群孙戏其腹上，内七李于脐中。李至烂，流汁出，其家谓其将死。后李核出而无患。
    　　刘曜字永明，须百茎，皆长五尺。
    　　汉中山靖王胜，有男女一百人，其后子孙流衍于今，问之，皆刘裔。
    　　后周独孤信三女为后，各生周、隋、唐一朝天子。长生周武帝，次生隋炀帝，次生唐高祖。
    　　汉昌邑王贺即位二十七日，积恶凡一千四百二十七条，为霍光所废。
    　　项羽每叱咤，万人手足皆废。
    　　晋宣王司马懿，自顾见背。
    　　隋有麦铁杖，一夕行一千百里。夕发洛阳往宋州为盗，及明却返。宋人因见其所盗之物者，执麦告之，为吏所劫，乃承愆。 [2]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [2]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 娥皇、女英从舜巡狩 - Refers to the legendary consorts of Emperor Shun, who accompanied him on his travels.
    • 行及湘川 - Means 'when they reached the Xiang River.'
    • 闻舜崩于苍梧 - Means 'they heard that Shun had died in Cangwu (a place in present-day Guangxi).'
    • 泣下，泪洒湘川之竹，皆成斑文 - Means 'they wept, and their tears fell on the bamboo of the Xiang River, which turned into spotted patterns.' This is a metaphor for the weeping of the bamboo.
    • 公孙吕面长三尺，阔三寸 - Describes the unusual appearance of Gong Sun Lü, a minister of the State of Wei during the Spring and Autumn period.
    • 为卫国贤臣 - Means 'he was a virtuous minister of the State of Wei.'
    • 东汉赵伯翁尝昼寝 - Means 'during the Eastern Han Dynasty, Zhao Bo翁 once took a nap during the day.'
    • 群孙戏其腹上 - Means 'his grandchildren played on his stomach.'
    • 内七李于脐中 - Means 'they put seven plums into his navel.'
    • 李至烂，流汁出 - Means 'the plums rotted, and the juice flowed out.'
    • 其家谓其将死 - Means 'his family thought he was going to die.'
    • 后李核出而无患 - Means 'later, the plum pits came out, and he had no problem.'
    • 刘曜字永明，须百茎，皆长五尺 - Describes Liu Yao, a Western Jin Dynasty official, whose beard had a hundred strands, each five feet long.
    • 汉中山靖王胜，有男女一百人 - Means 'Emperor Jing of Zhongshan during the Han Dynasty had one hundred sons and daughters.'
    • 其后子孙流衍于今 - Means 'his descendants have spread out until today.'
    • 问之，皆刘裔 - Means 'when asked, they are all descendants of Liu.'
    • 后周独孤信三女为后 - Means 'in the Northern Zhou Dynasty, three daughters of Dugu Xin became empresses.'
    • 各生周、隋、唐一朝天子 - Means 'each gave birth to an emperor of the Zhou, Sui, and Tang Dynasties.'
    • 长生周武帝，次生隋炀帝，次生唐高祖 - Means 'the eldest gave birth to Emperor Wu of Zhou, the second to Emperor Yang of Sui, and the third to Emperor Gaozu of Tang.'
    • 汉昌邑王贺即位二十七日，积恶凡一千四百二十七条 - Means 'Emperor Changyi of Han reigned for twenty-seven days and accumulated a total of one thousand four hundred and twenty-seven evils.'
    • 为霍光所废 - Means 'he was deposed by Huo Guang.'
    • 项羽每叱咤，万人手足皆废 - Means 'every time Xiang Yu shouted, ten thousand people's hands and feet would fall off.' This is a hyperbolic description of his intimidating presence.
    • 晋宣王司马懿，自顾见背 - Means 'King Xuan of Jin, Sima Yi, looked back and saw himself being abandoned.' This is a metaphorical expression indicating his sense of betrayal or loss.
    • 隋有麦铁杖，一夕行一千百里 - Means 'during the Sui Dynasty, Mai Tietang could travel one thousand miles in one night.'
    • 夕发洛阳往宋州为盗，及明却返 - Means 'he left Luoyang at night to go to Song Prefecture to steal, and returned by morning.'
    • 宋人因见其所盗之物者，执麦告之，为吏所劫，乃承愆 - Means 'the people of Song saw the things he stole and arrested Mai, but he was rescued by officials, and he confessed his guilt.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 娥皇、女英 - Legendary consorts of Emperor Shun, known for their loyalty and sorrow after his death.
    • 舜 - A legendary Chinese emperor and the second of the Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors.
    • 苍梧 - A place in present-day Guangxi, where Emperor Shun is said to have died.
    • 公孙吕 - A minister of the State of Wei during the Spring and Autumn period, known for his unusual appearance.
    • 卫国 - One of the major states during the Spring and Autumn period.
    • 赵伯翁 - A figure from the Eastern Han Dynasty, whose story is used to illustrate a miraculous event.
    • 刘曜 - A Western Jin Dynasty official, known for his long beard.
    • 汉中山靖王胜 - Emperor Jing of Zhongshan during the Han Dynasty, known for having many children.
    • 后周 - The Northern Zhou Dynasty, which ruled northern China in the 6th century.
    • 独孤信 - A prominent general and politician during the Northern Zhou Dynasty, whose daughters became empresses.
    • 昌邑王贺 - Emperor Changyi of Han, who was deposed by Huo Guang.
    • 霍光 - A powerful regent during the Western Han Dynasty, who deposed Emperor Changyi.
    • 项羽 - A famous warlord and rival of Liu Bang during the Chu-Han Contention.
    • 晋宣王司马懿 - A key figure in the founding of the Jin Dynasty, known for his political acumen.
    • 麦铁杖 - A legendary figure from the Sui Dynasty, known for his incredible speed and ability to evade capture.
    • 宋州 - A prefecture in present-day Henan, where Mai Tietang is said to have committed his theft.

    Proper Nouns

    • 娥皇、女英 - Legendary consorts of Emperor Shun.
    • 舜 - A legendary Chinese emperor.
    • 苍梧 - A place in present-day Guangxi.
    • 公孙吕 - A minister of the State of Wei.
    • 卫国 - One of the major states during the Spring and Autumn period.
    • 赵伯翁 - A figure from the Eastern Han Dynasty.
    • 刘曜 - A Western Jin Dynasty official.
    • 汉中山靖王胜 - Emperor Jing of Zhongshan during the Han Dynasty.
    • 后周 - The Northern Zhou Dynasty.
    • 独孤信 - A prominent general and politician during the Northern Zhou Dynasty.
    • 昌邑王贺 - Emperor Changyi of Han.
    • 霍光 - A powerful regent during the Western Han Dynasty.
    • 项羽 - A famous warlord and rival of Liu Bang.
    • 晋宣王司马懿 - A key figure in the founding of the Jin Dynasty.
    • 麦铁杖 - A legendary figure from the Sui Dynasty.
    • 宋州 - A prefecture in present-day Henan.

    Ehuang and Nüying accompanied Emperor Shun on his travels. When they reached the Xiang River, they heard that Shun had died in Cangwu. They wept, and their tears fell on the bamboo of the Xiang River, which turned into spotted patterns.

    Gong Sun Lü, a minister of the State of Wei during the Spring and Autumn period, had a face that was three chi long and three cun wide. He was a virtuous minister of the State of Wei.

    During the Eastern Han Dynasty, Zhao Bo翁 once took a nap during the day. His grandchildren played on his stomach and put seven plums into his navel. The plums rotted, and the juice flowed out. His family thought he was going to die. Later, the plum pits came out, and he had no problem.

    Liu Yao, whose courtesy name was Yongming, had a beard with a hundred strands, each five feet long.

    Emperor Jing of Zhongshan during the Han Dynasty had one hundred sons and daughters. His descendants have spread out until today. When asked, they are all descendants of Liu.

    In the Northern Zhou Dynasty, three daughters of Dugu Xin became empresses. Each gave birth to an emperor of the Zhou, Sui, and Tang Dynasties. The eldest gave birth to Emperor Wu of Zhou, the second to Emperor Yang of Sui, and the third to Emperor Gaozu of Tang.

    Emperor Changyi of Han reigned for twenty-seven days and accumulated a total of one thousand four hundred and twenty-seven evils. He was deposed by Huo Guang.

    Every time Xiang Yu shouted, ten thousand people's hands and feet would fall off. This is a hyperbolic description of his intimidating presence.

    King Xuan of Jin, Sima Yi, looked back and saw himself being abandoned. This is a metaphorical expression indicating his sense of betrayal or loss.

    During the Sui Dynasty, Mai Tietang could travel one thousand miles in one night. He left Luoyang at night to go to Song Prefecture to steal, and returned by morning. The people of Song saw the things he stole and arrested Mai, but he was rescued by officials, and he confessed his guilt. [2]

    淮南程干，本富人，三年间为水火焚荡俱尽。妻茅氏，连八年孪生十六子，相持行乞于市。
    　　梁武帝贵为天子，三舍身为同泰寺奴。
    　　唐郭子仪授中书令，考二十四考，月入俸钱二万贯，官供二千人，熟食、马料五百石。
    　　郭太后贵极，绵联八朝帝王：代宗外孙，德宗外甥，顺宗新妇，宪宗皇后，穆宗之母，敬宗、文宗、武宗三宗祖母。
    　　李广利拔佩刀刺山石，泉涌。
    　　东汉孟业身重千斤，故帝疑其自重，乃以大秤悬栋间。业启曰：“陛下秤上秤臣，请秤之。虽肉重千斤，而智无一两。”
    　　干宝母妒，当葬父时，潜推一婢于墓中。十余年后，母亡，与父合葬，开墓，婢伏于棺上，久而乃生。问之，如平昔之时，指使无异。
    　　唐初，僧玄奘至西域取经，入维摩诘方丈室。及归，将书年月于壁，染翰欲书，约行数千百步，终不及墙。 [3]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [3]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 三年間為水火焚蕩俱盡 - Literally 'over three years, all was burned and destroyed by water and fire,' used to describe total destruction.
    • 連八年孿生十六子 - Literally 'for eight consecutive years, twins were born, totaling sixteen children,' an unusual and exaggerated description of fertility.
    • 三舍身 - A Buddhist practice where one renounces worldly life to become a monk, here referring to Emperor Wu of Liang's act of becoming a monk.
    • 考二十四考 - A term referring to a high-ranking official's long service record, with '考' meaning 'term of office.'
    • 月入俸錢二萬貫 - Refers to a monthly salary of 20,000 strings of coins, a very high amount.
    • 官供二千人 - Means 'officially supported by 2,000 people,' likely referring to a large retinue or household.
    • 熟食、馬料五百石 - Refers to 500 shi of cooked food and horse fodder, a large quantity.
    • 貴極 - Means 'reached the highest rank or status.'
    • 綿聯八朝帝王 - Literally 'connected to eight dynasties of emperors,' referring to Dehuang's influence across multiple dynasties.
    • 拔佩刀刺山石 - Literally 'pulled out his sword and stabbed the mountain stone,' a dramatic action.
    • 泉涌 - Means 'springs forth,' describing the result of the action.
    • 身重千斤 - Literally 'weighs a thousand catties,' an exaggeration used to describe extreme obesity.
    • 陛下秤上秤臣 - Literally 'Your Majesty, weigh me on the scale,' a request for a demonstration.
    • 雖肉重千斤，而智無一兩 - A play on words, meaning 'though my flesh weighs a thousand catties, my wisdom weighs not even an ounce.'
    • 潛推一婢於墓中 - Literally 'secretly pushed a maid into the tomb,' a mysterious and unusual action.
    • 開墓，婢伏於棺上，久而乃生 - Literally 'when the tomb was opened, the maid was found lying on the coffin, and after a long time, she revived.'
    • 染翰欲書 - Literally 'dipped the brush to write,' a poetic description of writing.
    • 終不及牆 - Literally 'never reached the wall,' indicating a long walk without finding a place to write.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 淮南程干 - Refers to Cheng Gan, a wealthy man from Huainan.
    • 梁武帝 - Emperor Wu of Liang, a famous emperor known for his promotion of Buddhism.
    • 唐郭子儀 - Guo Ziyi, a prominent general and statesman of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 中書令 - A high-ranking official position in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 郭太后 - Dehuang, a powerful empress who influenced multiple Tang emperors.
    • 李廣利 - Li Guangli, a general of the Han Dynasty known for his military exploits.
    • 東漢孟業 - Meng Ye, a figure from the Eastern Han Dynasty known for his unusual physical condition.
    • 干寶 - Gan Bao, a historian and writer of the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 唐初僧玄奘 - Xuanzang, a famous monk who traveled to India to fetch Buddhist scriptures.
    • 維摩詰方丈室 - Refers to the room of Vimalakirti, a famous figure in Buddhist literature.

    Proper Nouns

    • 淮南 - A region in present-day Anhui Province.
    • 程干 - Cheng Gan, a wealthy man from Huainan.
    • 梁武帝 - Emperor Wu of Liang.
    • 唐郭子儀 - Guo Ziyi.
    • 中書令 - A high-ranking official position.
    • 郭太后 - Dehuang.
    • 李廣利 - Li Guangli.
    • 東漢孟業 - Meng Ye.
    • 干寶 - Gan Bao.
    • 唐初僧玄奘 - Xuanzang.
    • 維摩詰 - Vimalakirti, a famous figure in Buddhist literature.

    Cheng Gan of Huainan, originally a wealthy man, had all his possessions burned and destroyed by water and fire over three years. His wife, Ma Shi, gave birth to twins for eight consecutive years, totaling sixteen children, and they supported themselves by begging in the market.

    Emperor Wu of Liang, despite being a ruler, once renounced his worldly life and became a monk at Tongtai Temple for three years.

    Guo Ziyi of the Tang Dynasty was appointed as the Grand Secretary and served for twenty-four terms. He earned a monthly salary of 20,000 strings of coins, supported by 2,000 people, and received 500 shi of cooked food and horse fodder.

    Dehuang, who reached the highest rank, was connected to eight dynasties of emperors: she was the granddaughter-in-law of Emperor Suzong, the niece of Emperor Dezong, the daughter-in-law of Emperor Shunzong, the wife of Emperor Xianzong, the mother of Emperor Muzong, and the grandmother of Emperors Jingzong, Wenzong, and Wuzong.

    Li Guangli pulled out his sword and stabbed a mountain stone, causing springs to gush forth.

    Meng Ye of the Eastern Han Dynasty weighed a thousand catties, and the emperor suspected his own weight, so he hung him from the rafters with a large scale. Meng Ye said, "Your Majesty, weigh me on the scale, please." Though his flesh weighed a thousand catties, his wisdom weighed not even an ounce.

    Gan Bao's mother was jealous, and when she buried her father, she secretly pushed a maid into the tomb. After more than ten years, when she died and was buried with her husband, the tomb was opened, and the maid was found lying on the coffin. After a long time, she revived. When asked, she answered as usual, obeying commands without hesitation.

    In the early Tang Dynasty, the monk Xuanzang traveled to the West to fetch Buddhist scriptures. Upon returning, he intended to inscribe the date on the wall of Vimalakirti's room. He dipped his brush to write but, after walking several hundred steps, never reached the wall. [3]

    唐柳子升妻郑氏，无疾而终，临卒时告子升曰：“不离君之身，后十八年更与君为亲。”已而，子升年近七十，再娶于崔氏。或多省前生之事，后产一男而卒。
    　　前梁张轨时，抱罕令严根妾产，同夕产一女、一龙、一鸷。
    　　贾弼之夜梦一人，面貌极＜鼻查＞丑，谓弼之曰：“思以易之，可乎”梦中微有所诺。及觉，临镜大惊，一如梦中见者。左右家人见之，皆奔走。其所异者，两手各执一笔，书之于纸，俱有理例。徐说之，亲戚然后乃信。
    　　魏建凌云阁既成，匠人误钉其额。文帝乃令车绳引上韦诞，题三字而下。顷刻之间，头须皓白。
    　　晋赵末，年八岁，一夕异长，身长八尺，髭须满颔，三日而死。
    　　唐惠卿，荆州庭中有橘树，其末有一实甚大，独异之。由是会宾客，摘而将食，乃剖之，有一赤蛇蟠于其中矣。
    　　德宗朝，有戴竿三原妇人王大娘，首戴十八人而行。 [4]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [4]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 無疾而終 - Literally 'died without illness,' often used to describe a peaceful or natural death.
    • 不離君之身 - A phrase suggesting a close, unbreakable bond, possibly implying a spiritual or metaphysical connection.
    • 更與君為親 - Literally 'will become your kin again,' suggesting a future reunion or transformation.
    • 前生之事 - Refers to events or memories from a previous life, invoking the concept of reincarnation.
    • 異長 - Literally 'abnormal growth,' used to describe a sudden and unusual physical change.
    • 頭須皓白 - Describes a sudden graying or whitening of hair, often used to symbolize aging or shock.
    • 戴竿 - Refers to a performance where a person balances on a pole, often seen as a form of acrobatic or folk art.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 唐柳子升 - A historical figure from the Tang Dynasty, though his exact identity or significance is unclear from the text.
    • 前梁張軌 - Refers to Zhang Gui, a military governor during the Western Jin Dynasty, known for his role in the Xiliang region.
    • 抱罕令 - 'Bao Han' is likely a place name, possibly referring to a region in present-day Gansu Province.
    • 魏建凌云阁 - Refers to the construction of the 'Lingyun Pavilion' during the Wei Dynasty, a historical architectural project.
    • 文帝 - Likely refers to Cao Pi, the first emperor of the Wei Dynasty.
    • 贾弼之 - A historical figure, possibly a scholar or official, though his exact role is unclear.
    • 唐惠卿 - A historical figure, possibly a scholar or official, though his exact role is unclear.
    • 德宗朝 - Refers to the reign of Emperor Dezong of the Tang Dynasty (779–805).
    • 三原妇人王大娘 - A historical figure, likely a performer or entertainer, known for her skill in balancing on a pole.

    Proper Nouns

    • 唐柳子升 - A historical figure from the Tang Dynasty.
    • 前梁張軌 - Zhang Gui, a military governor during the Western Jin Dynasty.
    • 抱罕令 - A place name, possibly referring to a region in present-day Gansu Province.
    • 魏建凌云阁 - The 'Lingyun Pavilion' during the Wei Dynasty.
    • 文帝 - Cao Pi, the first emperor of the Wei Dynasty.
    • 贾弼之 - A historical figure, possibly a scholar or official.
    • 唐惠卿 - A historical figure, possibly a scholar or official.
    • 德宗朝 - The reign of Emperor Dezong of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 三原妇人王大娘 - A historical figure, likely a performer or entertainer.

    A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    In the Tang Dynasty, Li Zisheng's wife Zheng died without illness, and on her deathbed, she told Li Zisheng, "I will not leave your side, and after eighteen years, I will become your kin again." Soon after, Li Zisheng, who was nearly seventy, remarried into the Cui family. She often remembered events from her previous life and later gave birth to a son before dying.

    During the Western Jin Dynasty, Zhang Gui was the governor of Bao Han, and his concubine gave birth to a daughter, a dragon, and a bird on the same night.

    One night, Jia Bizi dreamed of a person with an extremely ugly face, who said to him, "Do you want to change it? Is that possible?" In the dream, Jia Bizi seemed to agree slightly. Upon waking, he looked in the mirror and was shocked to see that he looked exactly like the person in his dream. His family members saw him and all ran away. What was strange was that he held a pen in each hand, writing on paper, and both writings had logical structure. When he explained it slowly, his relatives finally believed him.

    During the Wei Dynasty, the Lingyun Pavilion was completed, and a craftsman mistakenly nailed the signboard. Emperor Wen ordered a rope to be used to lift Wei Dun up, who wrote three characters and then descended. In a short moment, his hair and beard turned completely white.

    At the end of the Jin Dynasty, a child named Zhao, who was eight years old, suddenly grew abnormally one night, reaching eight feet tall, with a full beard, and died three days later.

    In the Tang Dynasty, Huizhi had a tangerine tree in his courtyard in Jingzhou. One of the fruits on the tree was exceptionally large, and it was unique. He invited guests to gather and was about to eat it when he cut it open and found a red snake coiled inside.

    During the reign of Emperor Dezong, there was a woman from Sanyuan named Wang Dan'niang, who could balance on a pole while carrying eighteen people on her head. [4]

    韶阳有一人牧牛，一旦，牛舐其臂，而色皎白。此人乐之，即袒其体，令牛遍舐，皆白。其人数日间暴卒。其家恨，杀此牛，召村社同食之。凡食者数十人，一夕同卒。
    　　羿烧仙药，药成。其妻妲娥窃而食之，遂奔入月中。
    　　《山海经》有大耳国，其人寝，常以一耳为席，一耳为衾。
    　　贾耽为滑州节度，酸枣县有俚妇事姑不敬，姑年甚老，无双目，旦食，妇以食裹纳犬粪授姑。姑食之，觉有异气。其子出远还，姑问其子：“此何物向者妇与吾食。”其子仰天大哭。有顷，雷电发，若有人截妇首，以犬续之。耽令牵行于境内，以告不孝者。时人谓之“狗头新妇。”
    　　天后朝，工人安金藏保中宗不反，乃自持刀开腹明之，五脏坠地。后遣医工复内入腹，以桑皮细针缝合，经夕复生。玄宗即位，追封代国公。
    　　有娥简狄氏，吞鸟卵而生后稷。
    　　东汉陆续，岁饥，太守施贫者食，既毕，问之，凡赐六百人，言其姓名，无一参差。 [5]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [5]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 韶阳 - A place name, likely referring to a region or city in ancient China.
    • 一旦 - Means 'one day' or 'suddenly.'
    • 舐其臂 - Literally 'licked his arm,' but in context, it implies a supernatural or unusual event.
    • 色皎白 - Means 'color as white as jade,' suggesting a miraculous or divine transformation.
    • 暴卒 - Means 'sudden death.'
    • 恨 - Means 'to hate' or 'to resent,' indicating the family's anger.
    • 召村社同食之 - Means 'called the village community to eat it together,' suggesting a communal or ritualistic act.
    • 凡食者数十人 - Means 'all who ate it were dozens of people,' indicating a widespread effect.
    • 一夕同卒 - Means 'all died on the same night,' emphasizing the sudden and collective nature of the deaths.
    • 羿烧仙药 - Refers to the mythological figure Yi, who burned elixirs of immortality.
    • 药成 - Means 'the medicine was completed.'
    • 窃而食之 - Means 'stole and ate it,' indicating a secretive or forbidden act.
    • 遂奔入月中 - Means 'then fled into the moon,' suggesting a transformation or escape into the celestial realm.
    • 《山海经》有大耳国 - Refers to the *Shanhaijing* (Classic of Mountains and Seas), an ancient text containing mythological and geographical descriptions.
    • 寝 - Means 'to sleep,' but in this context, it refers to a unique sleeping posture.
    • 常以一耳为席，一耳为衾 - Literally 'often used one ear as a pillow and the other as a quilt,' suggesting a bizarre or mythical practice.
    • 贾耽为滑州节度 - Refers to the historical figure Jia Dan, who served as the military governor of Huazhou.
    • 酸枣县有俚妇事姑不敬 - Refers to a woman in Suzao County who was disrespectful to her mother-in-law.
    • 无双目 - Means 'no eyes,' indicating blindness.
    • 旦食 - Means 'morning meal.'
    • 以食裹纳犬粪授姑 - Means 'wrapped dog feces in food and gave it to her mother-in-law,' indicating a cruel act.
    • 觉有异气 - Means 'felt something strange,' suggesting suspicion or discomfort.
    • 仰天大哭 - Means 'cried to the sky,' indicating a dramatic or emotional reaction.
    • 雷电发 - Means 'thunder and lightning struck,' suggesting divine intervention.
    • 若有人截妇首，以犬续之 - Literally 'as if someone cut off the woman's head and replaced it with a dog's,' indicating a grotesque punishment.
    • 牵行于境内 - Means 'dragged her body through the territory,' suggesting a public display or punishment.
    • 以告不孝者 - Means 'to warn the unrighteous,' indicating a moral lesson.
    • 时人谓之‘狗头新妇’ - Means 'people of the time called her 'Dog-Headed Wife,' indicating a nickname or title.
    • 天后朝 - Refers to the reign of Empress Wu Zetian, a female emperor of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 工人安金藏保中宗不反 - Refers to the historical figure An Jinzang, who protected Emperor Zhongzong from rebellion.
    • 自持刀开腹明之 - Means 'held a knife to his own abdomen to prove it,' indicating a dramatic act of self-sacrifice.
    • 五脏坠地 - Means 'five organs fell to the ground,' indicating severe injury.
    • 遣医工复入腹，以桑皮细针缝合 - Means 'sent doctors to put the organs back and sew them up with fine mulberry thread,' indicating medical intervention.
    • 经夕复生 - Means 'revived after a night,' indicating miraculous recovery.
    • 玄宗即位，追封代国公 - Refers to the reign of Emperor Xuanzong, who posthumously honored An Jinzang as Duke of Dai.
    • 娥简狄氏 - Refers to the mythological figure E Jianti, the mother of Houji (Posterior Ji).
    • 吞鸟卵而生后稷 - Means 'swallowed an egg and gave birth to Houji,' indicating a miraculous conception.
    • 东汉陆续 - Refers to the historical figure Lu Su, a figure from the Eastern Han Dynasty.
    • 岁饥 - Means 'year of famine,' indicating a time of scarcity.
    • 太守施贫者食 - Means 'the governor distributed food to the poor,' indicating charitable action.
    • 既毕 - Means 'after finishing,' indicating the completion of the distribution.
    • 凡赐六百人 - Means 'all who received were six hundred people,' indicating the scale of the distribution.
    • 言其姓名，无一参差 - Means 'named their names, none were wrong,' indicating accuracy in record-keeping.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 韶阳 - A place name, likely referring to a region or city in ancient China.
    • 羿 - A mythological archer and hero from Chinese mythology.
    • 仙药 - Refers to elixirs of immortality, a common theme in Chinese mythology.
    • 嫦娥 - The lunar goddess who, according to legend, stole and ate the elixir of immortality and fled to the moon.
    • 《山海经》 - An ancient Chinese text containing mythological and geographical descriptions.
    • 大耳国 - A mythical country described in the *Shanhaijing*.
    • 贾耽 - A historical figure who served as the military governor of Huazhou during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 酸枣县 - A county in ancient China.
    • 俚妇 - Refers to a woman from a rural or lower-class background.
    • 事姑不敬 - Refers to the Confucian virtue of filial piety, specifically the respect owed to one's mother-in-law.
    • 天后朝 - Refers to the reign of Empress Wu Zetian, a female emperor of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 安金藏 - A historical figure who protected Emperor Zhongzong from rebellion.
    • 中宗 - Emperor Zhongzong of Tang, a ruler of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 玄宗 - Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, a ruler of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 娥简狄氏 - A mythological figure, the mother of Houji (Posterior Ji).
    • 后稷 - A mythological figure, the founder of agriculture in Chinese mythology.
    • 东汉 - The Eastern Han Dynasty, a period in Chinese history.
    • 陆续 - A historical figure from the Eastern Han Dynasty.
    • 岁饥 - Refers to a year of famine, a common theme in historical records.

    In the region of Shaoyang, there was a man who tended cattle. One day, a cow licked his arm, and it turned as white as jade. Delighted by this, he exposed his body to the cow, allowing it to lick him all over, and his entire body turned white. A few days later, the man suddenly died. His family, filled with resentment, killed the cow and called the village community to eat it together. All who ate the cow died on the same night, numbering in the dozens.

    Yi, the legendary archer, burned an elixir of immortality. Once the medicine was completed, his wife, Chang'e, stole and ate it, then fled into the moon.

    The Shanhaijing (Classic of Mountains and Seas) describes a country called the Land of the Large-Eared People, where its inhabitants sleep with one ear as a pillow and the other as a quilt.

    During the reign of Jia Dan, the military governor of Huazhou, there was a woman in Suzao County who was disrespectful to her mother-in-law. The mother-in-law was very old and blind. In the morning, the woman wrapped dog feces in food and gave it to her mother-in-law. When the mother-in-law ate it, she felt something strange. The woman's son returned from a long journey, and the mother-in-law asked him, "What was it that the woman gave me to eat?" The son cried out to the heavens. Soon after, thunder and lightning struck, as if someone had cut off the woman's head and replaced it with a dog's. Jia Dan ordered her body to be dragged through the territory to warn the unrighteous. People of the time referred to her as the "Dog-Headed Wife."

    During the reign of Empress Wu Zetian, the worker An Jinzang protected Emperor Zhongzong from rebellion. To prove his loyalty, he held a knife to his own abdomen, causing his five organs to fall to the ground. Later, doctors were sent to put the organs back and sew them up with fine mulberry thread. After a night, he revived. When Emperor Xuanzong ascended the throne, he posthumously honored An Jinzang as Duke of Dai.

    There was a woman named E Jianti, who swallowed an egg and gave birth to Houji (Posterior Ji).

    During the Eastern Han Dynasty, there was a man named Lu Su. In a year of famine, the governor distributed food to the poor. After the distribution was complete, he asked the recipients to name themselves. A total of six hundred people received food, and all their names were recorded without error. [5]

    吴王阖闾死，埋酵三千，以为殉葬。
    　　汉武钩弋夫人，姓赵氏。手本拳，帝纳后，以后伸之，遂展。
    　　王献之常为写《祭晋元帝庙祝文》版，墨入木，深八分。
    　　王戎视日，睛不眩。
    　　廉颇食，尽米一斗，肉十斤。
    　　陶侃，有相者视其手，策文上指，谓曰：“策文到指上为三公，贵不可说。”侃以针刺之，通指皆出血，洒墙为公字。
    　　唐大历中，河南尹相里造刑洛阳尉苗登，有尾长二尺余。
    　　《神异经》有李子昂，长七寸，日行千里；一旦被海鹄所吞，居鹄腹中，三年不死。
    　　殷纣为糟丘酒池，广可以泛舟。
    　　张少平妻田氏，少平卒后，累年寡居，忽梦一人自天而下，压其腹，因而怀孕。乃曰：“无夫而孕，人闻弃我也。”徙于代，依东方。五月朔旦，生一子。以其居代东方，名之东方朔。或言岁星精，多能，无不该博。
    　　蜀先主刘备，自见其耳。 [6]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [6]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 埋酵三千，以為殉葬 - A euphemistic phrase meaning 'to bury three thousand people as sacrifices for burial,' referring to the practice of burying alive servants and attendants with the deceased.
    • 手本拳，帝納后，以后伸之，遂展 - Describes the story of Empress Zhao, whose hand was originally clenched but uncurled after she was taken as a concubine.
    • 墨入木，深八分 - Literally means 'the ink penetrated the wood to a depth of eight points,' referring to the skillful writing of Wang Xizhi.
    • 睛不眩 - Means 'the eyes did not feel dizzy,' describing Wang Rong's ability to stare directly at the sun.
    • 策文到指上为三公，贵不可说 - A divination phrase meaning 'the writing on the palm reaches the fingertips, indicating the rank of Three Excellencies, a position of great honor.'
    • 广可以泛舟 - Means 'wide enough to sail a boat,' describing the grandeur of King Zhou's wine pool.
    • 无夫而孕 - Means 'conceiving without a husband,' describing the miraculous conception of Dong Fang Shuo.
    • 洒墙为公字 - Means 'spilling blood on the wall to form the character 'public' (公),' referring to the divination method used by Tao Kan.
    • 自见其耳 - Means 'seeing his own ears,' describing Liu Bei's unusual physical characteristic.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 吴王阖闾 - Refers to King Helu of Wu, a famous ruler of the State of Wu during the Spring and Autumn period.
    • 汉武帝 - Refers to Emperor Wu of Han, one of the most powerful emperors of the Han dynasty.
    • 钩弋夫人 - Refers to Empress Zhao, a concubine of Emperor Wu of Han.
    • 王献之 - Refers to Wang Xizhi, a famous calligrapher of the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 王戎 - Refers to Wang Rong, a scholar and official of the Jin dynasty.
    • 廉颇 - Refers to Lian Po, a famous general of the State of Zhao during the Warring States period.
    • 陶侃 - Refers to Tao Kan, a general and politician of the Jin dynasty.
    • 殷纣 - Refers to King Zhou of Shang, the last ruler of the Shang dynasty, known for his tyrannical rule.
    • 张少平 - Refers to Zhang Shaoping, a historical figure whose wife conceived Dong Fang Shuo.
    • 蜀先主刘备 - Refers to Liu Bei, the founder of the Shu Han state during the Three Kingdoms period.

    Proper Nouns

    • 吴王阖闾 - King Helu of Wu
    • 汉武帝 - Emperor Wu of Han
    • 钩弋夫人 - Empress Zhao
    • 王献之 - Wang Xizhi
    • 王戎 - Wang Rong
    • 廉颇 - Lian Po
    • 陶侃 - Tao Kan
    • 殷纣 - King Zhou of Shang
    • 张少平 - Zhang Shaoping
    • 蜀先主刘备 - Liu Bei
    • 大历 - A reign title of Emperor Dezong of Tang
    • 河南尹 - Governor of Henan
    • 相里造 - A historical figure
    • 洛阳尉 - Magistrate of Luoyang
    • 苗登 - A historical figure
    • 《神异经》 - A classical text on mythical creatures
    • 李子昂 - A mythical creature
    • 海鹄 - A mythical bird
    • 代 - A place name
    • 东方 - A place name
    • 岁星 - Jupiter
    King Helu of Wu died, and three thousand people were buried alive as sacrifices for his burial.
Emperor Wu of Han had a concubine named Zhao, whose hand was originally clenched. After she was taken as a concubine, her hand uncurled.
Wang Xizhi often wrote the text "Inscription for the Temple of Emperor Yuan of Jin" on wooden tablets, and the ink penetrated the wood to a depth of eight points.
Wang Rong could look directly at the sun without feeling dizzy.
Lian Po could eat a whole peck of rice and ten pounds of meat in one meal.
Tao Kan had a fortune-teller examine his hand. The fortune-teller said, "The writing on the palm reaches the fingertips, indicating the rank of Three Excellencies, a position of great honor." Tao Kan pricked his fingers with a needle, and blood flowed out, forming the character 'public' (公) on the wall.
During the reign of Emperor Dezong of Tang, the Governor of Henan, Xiang Li Zao, examined the magistrate of Luoyang, Miao Deng, who had a tail two feet long.
The *Shen Yi Jing* (Book of Strange Things) describes a creature named Li Zi Ang, which is seven inches long and can travel a thousand miles in a day. One day, it was swallowed by a sea bird and survived for three years in its stomach.
King Zhou of Shang built a wine pool that was wide enough to sail a boat.
After Zhang Shaoping's death, his wife, Lady Tian, lived as a widow for many years. One day, she dreamed of a man descending from the sky, who pressed on her abdomen and caused her to become pregnant. She said, "I conceived without a husband; people will surely abandon me." She moved to Dai and settled in the east. On the first day of May, she gave birth to a son. Because she lived in the eastern part of Dai, she named him Dong Fang Shuo. Some say he was the essence of Jupiter, skilled in many things, and knowledgeable in all fields.
Liu Bei, the founder of Shu Han, could see his own ears.
    [6]

    邹衍吹律，能变寒谷，生禾黍。
    　　京房吹律易姓，本李氏，因吹律知名，乃改京耳。后弃市。
    　　牛哀病三月，化而为虎，遂食其虎，复化为人。当其为虎时，不知其为人；及其为人，又不知其为虎。
    　　申包胥哭于秦廷，七日七夜不食，乞兵救楚。秦感之，乃假兵救之。
    　　后周有一人，姓陈氏，二十而娶妻，妻亦齐年。至四十，儿女又各生孙，孙复生子，子复生孙，相承百年内，其子孙盈数百人。老少悉为炀帝征辽所杀。
    　　东汉灵帝时，有河内人妇食夫，河南人夫食妇。
    　　灵帝时，洛阳女子生时两头四臂。
    　　魏文帝尝著《典论》云，天下无切玉刀、火浣布。俄而外国进此二物，文帝遂毁《典论》。
    　　古有女木兰者，代其父从征，身备戎装凡十三年，同伙之卒不知其是女儿。
    　　蜀人杨行廉精巧，尝刻木为僧，于益州市引手乞钱。钱满五十于手，则自倾泻下瓶，口〔言“布施”字。〕111111 [7]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [7]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 邹衍吹律，能变寒谷，生禾黍 - Refers to the legendary story of Zou Yan, a philosopher who could change the climate by playing his flute, transforming a cold valley into fertile land.
    • 京房吹律易姓，本李氏，因吹律知名，乃改京耳 - Describes Jing Fang, a scholar who changed his surname from Li to Jing after gaining fame for his musical expertise.
    • 牛哀病三月，化而为虎，遂食其虎，复化为人 - A mythological story about Niu Ai, who transformed into a tiger, ate the tiger, and then transformed back into a human.
    • 申包胥哭于秦廷，七日七夜不食，乞兵救楚 - Refers to Shen Baoxu, who wept for seven days and nights at the Qin court to plead for military aid to save the state of Chu.
    • 后周有一人，姓陈氏，二十而娶妻，妻亦齐年。至四十，儿女又各生孙，孙复生子，子复生孙，相承百年内，其子孙盈数百人 - Describes a person from the Later Zhou dynasty who married at age 20, and his wife was the same age. By the time he was 40, they had children, grandchildren, great-grandchildren, and so on, with over 500 descendants within a century.
    • 东汉灵帝时，有河内人妇食夫，河南人夫食妇 - Refers to a bizarre incident during the reign of Emperor Ling of Han where a woman from Hedong ate her husband, and a man from Henan ate his wife.
    • 灵帝时，洛阳女子生时两头四臂 - Describes a woman from Luoyang who was born with two heads and four arms during the reign of Emperor Ling of Han.
    • 魏文帝尝著《典论》云，天下无切玉刀、火浣布 - Refers to Emperor Wen of Wei, who wrote in his work *Dian Lun* that there were no such things as a knife that can cut jade or cloth that can be washed in fire.
    • 俄而外国进此二物，文帝遂毁《典论》 - Shortly after, foreign envoys presented these two items, and Emperor Wen of Wei then destroyed his work *Dian Lun*.
    • 古有女木兰者，代其父从征，身备戎装凡十三年，同伙之卒不知其是女儿 - Describes the legendary figure Mulan, who disguised herself as a man to take her father's place in the army for 13 years, and her fellow soldiers did not know she was a woman.
    • 蜀人杨行廉精巧，尝刻木为僧，于益州市引手乞钱。钱满五十于手，则自倾泻下瓶，口〔言“布施”字〕 - Describes Yang Xinglian, a skilled craftsman from Shu, who carved a wooden statue of a monk that would beg for money in Yizhou. When the money reached 50 coins, the statue would automatically pour the money into a bottle and say '布施' (alms).

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 邹衍 - A philosopher from the Warring States period known for his theories on cosmology and climate change.
    • 京房 - A scholar from the Western Han dynasty known for his expertise in music and divination.
    • 申包胥 - A loyalist to the state of Chu during the Spring and Autumn period, famous for his plea to the state of Qin for military aid.
    • 灵帝 - Emperor Ling of Han, a ruler of the Eastern Han dynasty known for his eccentric behavior and the decline of the dynasty.
    • 木兰 - A legendary figure from Chinese folklore who disguised herself as a man to take her father's place in the army.
    • 魏文帝 - Emperor Wen of Wei, a ruler of the Cao Wei state during the Three Kingdoms period known for his literary works.
    • 蜀人 - Refers to people from the state of Shu during the Three Kingdoms period, known for their craftsmanship and ingenuity.

    Proper Nouns

    • 邹衍 - A philosopher from the Warring States period.
    • 京房 - A scholar from the Western Han dynasty.
    • 申包胥 - A loyalist to the state of Chu during the Spring and Autumn period.
    • 灵帝 - Emperor Ling of Han.
    • 木兰 - A legendary figure from Chinese folklore.
    • 魏文帝 - Emperor Wen of Wei.
    • 蜀人 - People from the state of Shu during the Three Kingdoms period.

    Zou Yan, by playing his flute, could transform a cold valley into one that grew millet and sorghum.
    Jing Fang, through his mastery of the flute, changed his surname from Li to Jing, having gained fame for his musical skills. Later, he was executed in public.
    Niu Ai fell ill for three months and transformed into a tiger, then ate the tiger and transformed back into a human. When he was a tiger, he did not know he was a human; and when he became a human again, he did not know he had been a tiger.
    Shen Baoxu wept at the Qin court for seven days and seven nights without food, begging for troops to save the state of Chu. Qin was moved by his plea and lent him troops to aid Chu.
    In the Later Zhou, there was a man surnamed Chen who married at age twenty, and his wife was the same age. By the time he was forty, they had children, grandchildren, great-grandchildren, and so on, with over five hundred descendants within a century. All of them, young and old, were killed by Emperor Yang of Sui during his campaign against Goguryeo.
    During the reign of Emperor Ling of Han, a woman from Hedong ate her husband, and a man from Henan ate his wife.
    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.
    Emperor Wen of Wei once wrote in his work Dian Lun that there were no such things as a knife that could cut jade or cloth that could be washed in fire. Shortly after, foreign envoys presented these two items, and Emperor Wen of Wei then destroyed his work Dian Lun.
    There was once a woman named Mulan who disguised herself as a man to take her father's place in the army. She wore military attire for thirteen years, and her fellow soldiers did not know she was a woman.
    Yang Xinglian, a skilled craftsman from Shu, once carved a wooden statue of a monk that would beg for money in Yizhou. When the money reached fifty coins, the statue would automatically pour the money into a bottle and say, "布施" (alms). [7]

    汉有黄安，不知何许人，常坐一龟，畏日光。龟每二千年一出头，安坐来见龟五出头矣。
    　　符坚三年，凤凰集于东阁，坚欲赦国中，时无有知者。忽有一童儿，绯帕幕首言于市，曰：“官家有赦。”坚复验诘，言赦书日有一苍蝇立于笔端，久而飞去，化为童子，以告市人也。
    　　禹治水，渡浙江，风涛甚，有二花蛇龙负舟而过。左右恐惧，惟禹安然无畏。
    　　王伐吴，于蜀江造战舰，长二百四十步，上起走马楼。舟船之盛，自古莫比。
    　　唐贞元中，有乞者解如海，其手自臂而堕，足自胫而脱，善击球、樗蒲戏，又善剑舞、数丹丸，挟二妻，生子数人。至元和末犹在，长安戏场中日集数千人观之。
    　　《敦煌实录》云：王樊卒，有盗开其冢，见王樊与人樗蒲，以酒赐盗者，盗者惶怖饮之，见有人牵铜马出冢者。夜有神至城门，自言是王樊使，今有人发冢，以酒墨其唇，但至，可以验而擒之。盗既入城，城门者乃缚诘之，如神言。 [8]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [8]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 不知何许人 - Indicates that the origin or background of the person is unknown.
    • 常坐一龟，畏日光 - Literally 'often sits on a turtle, fears sunlight,' which likely means the person has a turtle as a companion or a talisman.
    • 二千年一出头 - Means 'appears once every two thousand years,' referring to the rarity of the turtle's appearance.
    • 安坐来见龟五出头矣 - Means 'An has seen the turtle appear five times,' indicating the longevity and rarity of the turtle's appearances.
    • 符坚三年，凤凰集于东阁 - Means 'during the third year of Fu Jian's reign, a phoenix gathered at the east hall,' a symbolic event indicating good fortune or divine favor.
    • 欲赦国中，时无有知者 - Means 'wants to grant amnesty in the country, but no one knows about it,' indicating the secrecy of the plan.
    • 忽有一童儿，绯帕幕首言于市 - Means 'suddenly there was a child, covered with a red scarf, speaking in the market,' a mysterious and symbolic event.
    • 官家有赦 - Means 'the emperor has granted amnesty,' indicating the child's message about the amnesty.
    • 苍蝇立于笔端，久而飞去，化为童子 - Means 'a green fly stood on the tip of the pen, flew away, and transformed into a child,' a supernatural event.
    • 以告市人也 - Means 'to inform the people in the market,' suggesting the child's purpose in appearing.
    • 禹治水，渡浙江，风涛甚 - Means 'Yu the Great controlled the floods, crossed the Qiantang River, and the wind and waves were very strong,' describing the severity of the weather.
    • 二花蛇龙负舟而过 - Means 'two flowered snake dragons carried the boat past,' a fantastical description of the event.
    • 左右恐惧，惟禹安然无畏 - Means 'the attendants were afraid, but only Yu remained calm and fearless,' highlighting Yu's bravery and composure.
    • 王伐吴，于蜀江造战舰 - Means 'Wang Jun campaigned against Wu, built warships on the Shu River,' a historical event during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 长二百四十步，上起走马楼 - Means '240 steps long, with a horse-riding floor on top,' describing a feature of the ship.
    • 舟船之盛，自古莫比 - Means 'the grandeur of the ships was unparalleled in ancient times,' emphasizing the scale and significance of the fleet.
    • 唐贞元中，有乞者解如海 - Means 'in the middle of the reign of Emperor Dezong of Tang, there was a beggar named Jie Ruhai,' indicating a historical period.
    • 手自臂而堕，足自胫而脱 - Means 'his hands fell from his arms, and his feet fell from his shins,' describing a physical anomaly.
    • 善击球、樗蒲戏，又善剑舞、数丹丸 - Means 'skilled at playing ball games and dice games, also skilled at sword dancing and counting pills,' indicating his abilities.
    • 挟二妻，生子数人 - Means 'with two wives and several children,' indicating his personal life.
    • 至元和末犹在，长安戏场中日集数千人观之 - Means 'until the end of the Yuanhe period, he was still alive, and in the theater in Chang'an, thousands of people gathered daily to watch him,' indicating his longevity and popularity.
    • 敦煌实录云 - Refers to the 'Record of Dunhuang,' a historical document.
    • 王樊卒，有盗开其冢 - Means 'King Fan died, and a thief opened his tomb,' indicating a grave robbery.
    • 见王樊与人樗蒲 - Means 'saw King Fan playing dice with someone,' a surprising and symbolic event.
    • 以酒赐盗者，盗者惶怖饮之 - Means 'gave wine to the thief, the thief drank it in fear,' indicating a supernatural interaction.
    • 见有人牵铜马出冢者 - Means 'saw someone pulling a copper horse out of the tomb,' a fantastical event.
    • 夜有神至城门，自言是王樊使 - Means 'at night, a spirit arrived at the city gate, said he was sent by King Fan,' indicating the spirit's identity.
    • 今有人发冢，以酒墨其唇，但至，可以验而擒之 - Means 'now there is someone who opened the tomb, and the thief's lips were marked with wine, just go there, and you can verify and capture him,' giving instructions.
    • 盗既入城，城门者乃缚诘之 - Means 'the thief entered the city, the gatekeeper bound and questioned him,' indicating the capture.
    • 如神言 - Means 'as the spirit had said,' confirming the accuracy of the spirit's instructions.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 黄安 - A person whose background is unknown, possibly a historical or legendary figure.
    • 符坚 - Fu Jian, a ruler of the Former Qin dynasty.
    • 禹 - Yu the Great, a legendary figure who controlled the floods.
    • 王 - Wang Jun, a general during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 唐贞元 - The middle of the reign of Emperor Dezong of Tang.
    • 元和 - A period in the Tang dynasty.
    • 长安 - Chang'an, the capital of the Tang dynasty.
    • 敦煌实录 - The 'Record of Dunhuang,' a historical document.
    • 王樊 - King Fan, a historical figure.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • The phrase '常坐一龟，畏日光' describes the person's relationship with the turtle and its sensitivity to sunlight.
    • The phrase '二千年一出头' describes the rarity of the turtle's appearances.
    • The phrase '安坐来见龟五出头矣' indicates the person's long-term observation of the turtle.
    • The phrase '符坚三年，凤凰集于东阁' describes a symbolic event during Fu Jian's reign.
    • The phrase '欲赦国中，时无有知者' describes Fu Jian's intention to grant amnesty and the lack of knowledge about it.
    • The phrase '忽有一童儿，绯帕幕首言于市' describes the sudden appearance of a child in the market.
    • The phrase '官家有赦' indicates the child's message about the amnesty.
    • The phrase '苍蝇立于笔端，久而飞去，化为童子' describes a supernatural event involving a fly and a child.
    • The phrase '以告市人也' explains the child's purpose in appearing.
    • The phrase '禹治水，渡浙江，风涛甚' describes Yu the Great's efforts to control the floods.
    • The phrase '二花蛇龙负舟而过' describes a fantastical event during Yu's journey.
    • The phrase '左右恐惧，惟禹安然无畏' contrasts the attendants' fear with Yu's composure.
    • The phrase '王伐吴，于蜀江造战舰' describes Wang Jun's military campaign and the construction of warships.
    • The phrase '长二百四十步，上起走马楼' describes the size and features of the warships.
    • The phrase '舟船之盛，自古莫比' emphasizes the grandeur of the fleet.
    • The phrase '唐贞元中，有乞者解如海' introduces a beggar with a unique condition.
    • The phrase '手自臂而堕，足自胫而脱' describes the beggar's physical anomaly.
    • The phrase '善击球、樗蒲戏，又善剑舞、数丹丸' lists the beggar's skills.
    • The phrase '挟二妻，生子数人' describes the beggar's personal life.
    • The phrase '至元和末犹在，长安戏场中日集数千人观之' indicates the beggar's longevity and popularity.
    • The phrase '敦煌实录云' introduces a historical document.
    • The phrase '王樊卒，有盗开其冢' describes a grave robbery.
    • The phrase '见王樊与人樗蒲' describes a surprising event in the tomb.
    • The phrase '以酒赐盗者，盗者惶怖饮之' describes the supernatural interaction with the thief.
    • The phrase '见有人牵铜马出冢者' describes a fantastical event.
    • The phrase '夜有神至城门，自言是王樊使' describes a supernatural visit.
    • The phrase '今有人发冢，以酒墨其唇，但至，可以验而擒之' gives instructions.
    • The phrase '盗既入城，城门者乃缚诘之' indicates the capture.
    • The phrase '如神言' confirms the accuracy of the spirit's instructions.

    In ancient China, there was a man named Huang An, whose origins are unknown. He often sat on a turtle that feared sunlight. The turtle appeared once every two thousand years, and Huang An had seen it five times.

    In the third year of Fu Jian's reign, a phoenix gathered at the east hall. Fu Jian wanted to grant amnesty in the country, but no one knew about it. Suddenly, a child appeared in the market, wearing a red scarf over his head, and announced, "The emperor has granted amnesty." Fu Jian investigated further and learned that the child had been transformed from a green fly that had stood on the tip of a pen, flew away, and then changed into a child to inform the people in the market.

    Yu the Great, while controlling the floods, crossed the Qiantang River during a severe storm. Two flowered snake dragons carried his boat past. His attendants were afraid, but Yu remained calm and fearless.

    Wang Jun, during his campaign against Wu, built warships on the Shu River. These ships were 240 steps long and had a horse-riding floor on top. The grandeur of these ships was unparalleled in ancient times.

    During the middle of Emperor Dezong of Tang's reign, there was a beggar named Jie Ruhai. His hands fell from his arms, and his feet fell from his shins. He was skilled at playing ball games and dice games, and also at sword dancing and counting pills. He had two wives and several children. Until the end of the Yuanhe period, he was still alive, and thousands of people gathered daily in the theater in Chang'an to watch him.

    According to the "Record of Dunhuang," after King Fan died, a thief opened his tomb and saw King Fan playing dice with someone. King Fan gave wine to the thief, who drank it in fear. The thief then saw someone pulling a copper horse out of the tomb. At night, a spirit arrived at the city gate and said he was sent by King Fan. The spirit informed the thief that someone had opened the tomb, and the thief's lips were marked with wine. The spirit instructed the thief to go to the tomb, where he could verify and capture the culprit. The thief entered the city, and the gatekeeper bound and questioned him, confirming the spirit's instructions. [8]

    唐李为淮西将，元和十二年送款归国。裴公破吴元济，入其城，汉军有剥妇人衣至裸体者。有新妇姜氏，怀妊五月矣，为乱卒所劫，以刀划其腹，姜氏气绝踣地。归见之，腹开尺余，因脱衣襦裹之。妇一夕复苏，傅以神药而平。满十月而产一男。朝廷以归国功，授一子官。子曰行修，年三十余，为南海节度，罢归，卒于道。
    　　韩晋公镇浙西，威令大行。时陈少游为淮南节度，理民有冤不得伸者，往诣晋公，必据而平之。浙右进钱，船渡江，为惊涛所溺。篙工募人漉出，二缗不得，众以钱填其数。自至津部视之，乃责江神，因指其钱曰：“此钱干，非水中得之者。”问吏，吏具实对。复投词诟责，俄然二缗浮出波上，遂以取之。 [9]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [9]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 送款歸國 - Diplomatic term meaning 'to send a message of surrender and return to one's own country.'
    • 氣絕踣地 - Describes the act of losing consciousness and falling to the ground.
    • 投詞诟責 - To cast a written accusation and scold a deity.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 唐李 - Refers to Li Bo, a historical figure during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 淮西 - A region during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 元和十二年 - The twelfth year of the Yuanhe era (817 AD).
    • 裴公 - Refers to Pei Du, a Tang general.
    • 吳元濟 - A historical figure.
    • 韓晉公 - Refers to Han Yu, a prominent Tang official.
    • 浙西 - The area west of the Zhejiang River.
    • 陳少游 - A historical figure.
    • 淮南 - A region during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 南海 - A region during the Tang Dynasty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 唐 - The Tang Dynasty.
    • 李 - A historical figure, Li Bo.
    • 淮西 - A region during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 元和十二年 - The twelfth year of the Yuanhe era (817 AD).
    • 裴公 - Pei Du, a Tang general.
    • 吳元濟 - A historical figure.
    • 韓晉公 - Han Yu, a prominent Tang official.
    • 浙西 - The area west of the Zhejiang River.
    • 陳少游 - A historical figure.
    • 淮南 - A region during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 南海 - A region during the Tang Dynasty.

    During the Tang Dynasty, Li Bo served as a general in Huai West. In the twelfth year of the Yuanhe era (817 AD), he sent a message of surrender and returned to his own country. Pei Du defeated Wu Yuanji, entered his city, and the Han army committed acts of violence against women, stripping them of their clothing until they were naked. Li Bo's newly married wife, Jiang, was five months pregnant when she was seized by unruly soldiers. They cut into her abdomen with a knife, and Jiang lost consciousness and fell to the ground. Upon returning home, Li Bo saw her with a wound that was more than a foot long. He wrapped her in his own clothing. That night, she revived and was treated with miraculous medicine, eventually recovering. She gave birth to a son after ten months. The imperial court granted an official position to one of Li Bo's sons. His son, named Xingxiu, was over thirty years old and served as the military governor of Nanhai. After his term ended, he returned home and died on the road.

    Han Yu, the Governor of Zhe West, enforced strict orders. At that time, Chen Shaoyou served as the military governor of Huainan. People who had grievances that could not be resolved went to Han Yu, and he would always resolve them. Money from the area west of Zhejiang was being transported across the river when it was drowned by a sudden storm. Boatmen recruited workers to dredge it out, but two units of currency could not be found. The workers filled the amount with additional money. Han Yu personally went to the crossing point to inspect the situation. He blamed the river god and pointed to the money, saying, "This money is dry; it was not found in the water." He asked the officials, who reported the truth. Han Yu then cast a written accusation and scolded the river god. Suddenly, the two units of currency floated to the surface, and he retrieved them. [9]

    玄宗打羯鼓。天宝初，春景甚煦，而卉物未柝，乃命鼓座于殿阶，击之，满树花发。帝谓左右曰：“一曲未终，而花烂然。得不以我为圣耶！”
    　　刘伶好酒，常袒露不挂丝，人见而责之。伶曰：“我以天地为栋宇，屋室为衤昆衤夸，君等无事，何得入我衤昆衤夸中”其人笑而退。
    　　唐开元五年春，司天密奏云：“玄象有谪见，其灾甚重。”玄宗大惊，问曰：“何祥”对曰：“当有名士三十八人同日冤死，今新进及第进士正应其数。”内一人李蒙者，贵主家婿，上不得已，言其事，密戒主曰：“每有大游宴，汝爱婿可闭留其家。”主居昭国里。时大合乐，音曲远畅，曲江涨水，联舟数十艘，进士毕集。蒙闻之，乃逾垣走赴，群众惬望。方登舟，移就池中，暴风忽起，画舸平沈，声伎、持篙楫者不知纪极，三十八人无一生者。 [10]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [10]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 玄宗打羯鼓 - Literal description of Emperor Xuanzong playing the jiegǔ (a type of drum).
    • 天宝初，春景甚煦，而卉物未柝 - Poetic description of the early spring season in the Tianbao era, where the weather was warm but plants had not yet blossomed.
    • 一曲未终，而花烂然 - Metaphorical expression indicating that the drumming caused the flowers to bloom suddenly.
    • 得不以我为圣耶 - Rhetorical question expressing the emperor's self-importance, asking if he is not considered a sage.
    • 刘伶好酒，常袒露不挂丝 - Colloquial expression describing Duan Ling's love for alcohol and his habit of appearing unclothed.
    • 我以天地为栋宇，屋室为衤昆衤夸 - Metaphorical statement where Duan Ling compares the world to a house and himself to someone who does not need to enter a house.
    • 何得入我衤昆衤夸中 - Rhetorical question expressing the idea that others should not intrude on his personal space.
    • 唐开元五年春，司天密奏云 - Formal statement indicating that in the fifth year of the Kaiyuan era, the court astronomer made a secret report.
    • 玄象有谪见，其灾甚重 - Formal expression indicating that there were ominous signs in the heavens, suggesting a severe disaster.
    • 当有名人三十八人同日冤死 - Prediction that 38 prominent individuals would die unjustly on the same day.
    • 内一人李蒙者，贵主家婿 - Formal statement identifying Li Meng as the son-in-law of a noble princess.
    • 每有大游宴，汝爱婿可闭留其家 - Formal instruction to the princess to keep her son-in-law at home during large banquets.
    • 昭国里 - Name of a specific area in the capital city.
    • 时大合乐，音曲远畅 - Formal description of a grand musical performance with music spreading far and wide.
    • 曲江涨水，联舟数十艘 - Formal description of the Qujiang River overflowing and several boats being connected together.
    • 蒙闻之，乃逾垣走赴 - Formal description of Li Meng climbing over a wall to join the festivities.
    • 暴风忽起，画舸平沈 - Formal description of a sudden storm causing the boats to sink.
    • 声伎、持篙楫者不知纪极 - Formal description of the chaos caused by the storm, with performers and boatmen unable to be counted.
    • 三十八人无一生者 - Formal statement indicating that all 38 people died in the disaster.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 玄宗 - Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, a famous ruler known for his cultural and political achievements.
    • 天宝 - The era name of Emperor Xuanzong, referring to the period from 742 to 756.
    • 开元 - The era name of Emperor Xuanzong, referring to the period from 713 to 741.
    • 司天 - The court astronomer or astrologer, responsible for interpreting celestial signs.
    • 曲江 - A famous scenic area in Chang'an (modern-day Xi'an), often used for banquets and cultural activities.
    • 进士 - A high-ranking degree in the imperial examination system, indicating a scholar-official.
    • 贵主家婿 - The son-in-law of a noble princess, indicating a high social status.
    • 昭国里 - A specific area in Chang'an, likely a residential district for nobility or officials.
    • 大合乐 - A grand musical performance, often held during important occasions.
    • 联舟 - Boats connected together, often used for transportation or as a platform for performances.

    Proper Nouns

    • 玄宗 - Emperor Xuanzong of Tang.
    • 天宝 - The era name of Emperor Xuanzong.
    • 开元 - The era name of Emperor Xuanzong.
    • 司天 - The court astronomer or astrologer.
    • 曲江 - A famous scenic area in Chang'an.
    • 进士 - A high-ranking degree in the imperial examination system.
    • 贵主家婿 - The son-in-law of a noble princess.
    • 昭国里 - A specific area in Chang'an.
    • 李蒙 - A specific individual, the son-in-law of a noble princess.

    Emperor Xuanzong played the jiegǔ. In the early days of the Tianbao era, the spring weather was very warm, but the plants had not yet blossomed. He then ordered the drum to be placed on the palace steps and played it, causing the flowers on the trees to bloom. The emperor said to those around him, "Before the melody ends, the flowers bloom brilliantly. Am I not considered a sage?"

    Duan Ling loved alcohol and often appeared unclothed, without any clothing. People saw him and scolded him. Duan Ling said, "I consider the heavens and earth as my house and the rooms as my clothing. Since you have nothing to do, why should you enter my room?" The people laughed and left.

    In the fifth year of the Kaiyuan era of the Tang Dynasty, the court astronomer secretly reported, "There are ominous signs in the heavens, indicating a severe disaster." Emperor Xuanzong was greatly alarmed and asked, "What is the omen?" The court astronomer replied, "There will be 38 prominent individuals who will die unjustly on the same day. The newly appointed jinshi (imperial examination graduates) correspond to this number." One of them was Li Meng, the son-in-law of a noble princess. The emperor, unable to avoid it, told her about the matter and secretly instructed her, "Whenever there are large banquets, keep your beloved son-in-law at home." The princess lived in Zhaoguo Li. At the time, there was a grand musical performance with music spreading far and wide. The Qujiang River overflowed, and several boats were connected together, with all the jinshi gathered. Li Meng heard about it and climbed over the wall to join the festivities, much to the delight of the crowd. As he was boarding the boat, moving towards the pond, a sudden storm arose, causing the boats to sink. The performers and boatmen were so numerous that they could not be counted, and all 38 people died in the disaster. [10]

    唐刘辟初登第，诣卜者葫芦生筮得一卦，以定官禄。葫芦生双瞽，卦成，谓辟曰：“自此二十年，禄在西南，不得善终。”辟留束素与之。其后脱褐，从韦令公于西川，官至御史大夫，为行军司马。既二十年，韦病薨，使辟入奏，请益东川。诏未允，辟乃微服单骑，复诣葫芦生。筮之，揲蓍成卦，谓辟曰：“吾二十年前常与一人曾卜得‘无妄之随’，今复得此卦，非曩昔贤乎！”辟即依阿唯诺。葫芦生曰：“若审其人，祸将至矣。”辟不甚信，乃归蜀，果叛。宪宗皇帝擒之，戮于藁街。 [11]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [11]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 初登第 - Passing the imperial examinations and being granted an official position.
    • 诣卜者 - To visit a fortune teller.
    • 禄在西南 - Career will flourish in the southwestern region.
    • 不得善终 - Will not have a good end or will not die a natural death.
    • 留束素与之 - Left a bundle of silk with him, referring to a gift.
    • 脱褐 - Leaving one's official robes, indicating the start of a new career.
    • 从韦令公于西川 - Served under Wei Linggong in Western Sichuan.
    • 官至御史大夫 - Reached the rank of Grand Censor.
    • 行军司马 - Military advisor or general.
    • 韦病薨 - Wei died of illness.
    • 使辟入奏 - Sent Li Bi to report to the court.
    • 请益东川 - Asked for additional troops from Eastern Sichuan.
    • 诏未允 - The imperial edict was not granted.
    • 微服单骑 - Dressed in plain clothes and riding alone.
    • 揲蓍成卦 - Process of divination using yarrow stalks.
    • 无妄之随 - Specific hexagram combination in the I Ching (Book of Changes).
    • 若审其人 - If you recognize the person.
    • 祸将至矣 - Disaster is imminent.
    • 依阿唯诺 - To agree hesitantly and nod.
    • 果叛 - Ultimately rebelled.
    • 宪宗皇帝擒之 - Emperor Xianzong captured him.
    • 戮于藁街 - Executed him in the straw street (a place for executions).

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 唐 - Refers to the Tang Dynasty.
    • 刘辟 - Historical figure who served under Wei Linggong.
    • 韦令公 - Wei Linggong, a high-ranking official during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 西川 - Refers to the region of Western Sichuan.
    • 御史大夫 - High-ranking official responsible for overseeing government affairs.
    • 行军司马 - Military advisor or general.
    • 韦病薨 - Refers to the death of Wei Linggong.
    • 宪宗皇帝 - Emperor Xianzong of Tang, who ruled from 805 to 820.
    • 藁街 - A place in the capital city where executions were carried out.

    Proper Nouns

    • 唐 - Tang Dynasty.
    • 刘辟 - Li Bi.
    • 韦令公 - Wei Linggong.
    • 西川 - Western Sichuan.
    • 御史大夫 - Grand Censor.
    • 行军司马 - Military Advisor/General.
    • 韦病薨 - Death of Wei Linggong.
    • 宪宗皇帝 - Emperor Xianzong of Tang.
    • 藁街 - Place of execution.

    During the Tang Dynasty, Liu Bi, upon passing the imperial examinations and receiving an official position, visited a fortune teller named Hu Lu Sheng to have his future divined. Hu Lu Sheng, who was completely blind, cast a hexagram for Liu Bi to determine his official prospects. The fortune teller foretold that within twenty years, Liu Bi's career would flourish in the southwestern region but warned that he would not have a good end. Liu Bi left a bundle of silk with Hu Lu Sheng as a gift. Later, Liu Bi left his official robes and served under Wei Linggong in Western Sichuan, eventually rising to the rank of Grand Censor and becoming a military advisor.

    Twenty years later, Wei Linggong fell ill and died. Liu Bi was sent to report to the court and request additional troops from Eastern Sichuan. However, the imperial edict was not granted. Liu Bi then dressed in plain clothes and rode alone to visit Hu Lu Sheng again. After casting another hexagram, Hu Lu Sheng interpreted the result and said, "Twenty years ago, I once divined for someone who received the hexagram 'No Misfortune Followed by Following.' Now, you have received the same hexagram. Is it not the same person?" Liu Bi hesitantly agreed. Hu Lu Sheng warned him, "If you recognize the person, disaster is imminent." Liu Bi did not fully believe the warning and returned to Sichuan, where he ultimately rebelled. Emperor Xianzong of Tang captured him and executed him in the straw street. [11]

    唐贺知章，会稽永真人。进士擢第，太常少卿，秘书监，为太子诸王侍读。性落托放纵，逸思过人。年八十余，因醉赋诗，问左右曰：“纸多少”对曰：“有十幅。”乃书告老，乞归乡里。皇帝及皇太子、诸王皆赋送行诗，赐越中剡曲以给之。
    　　孔子行过少陵原，闻妇人哭甚哀，使子贡问焉：“何哭之悲也”妇人曰：“向者刈薪而遗簪。”孔子复问曰：“刈薪遗簪乃常也，而哭悲者何也”答曰：“非惜一簪。所以悲，不忘故也。” [12]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [12]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 進士擢第 - Passing the imperial examination and being selected as a jinshi (advanced scholar).
    • 太常少卿 - Refers to the position of Assistant Minister of Rites.
    • 秘书监 - Refers to the position of Chief Librarian or Keeper of the Imperial Library.
    • 太子諸王侍讀 - Means 'tutor to the crown prince and other princes.'
    • 性落托放縱 - Describes a person who is unrestrained and free-spirited.
    • 逸思過人 - Means 'having extraordinary imagination or creativity.'
    • 因醉賦詩 - Means 'writing poetry while drunk.'
    • 紙多少 - A colloquial way of asking for paper.
    • 有十幅 - Means 'there are ten sheets.'
    • 書告老 - Means 'write a petition for retirement.'
    • 乞歸鄉里 - Means 'request to return to one's hometown.'
    • 皇帝及皇太子、諸王皆賦送行詩 - Means 'the emperor, crown prince, and other princes all wrote farewell poems.'
    • 賜越中剡曲以給之 - Means 'bestowed the area of Yuezhou and Tianqu to him as a gift.'
    • 孔子行過少陵原 - Means 'Confucius was traveling through Shaoling Plain.'
    • 聞婦人哭甚哀 - Means 'heard a woman crying very sorrowfully.'
    • 使子貢問焉 - Means 'sent Zigan to ask her.'
    • 何哭之悲也 - Means 'why are you crying so sadly?'
    • 向者刈薪而遺簪 - Means 'earlier, when cutting firewood, I lost an ornament.'
    • 非惜一簪 - Means 'not because I am grieving over losing one ornament.'
    • 所以悲，不忘故也 - Means 'it is because I cannot forget the past.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 賀知章 - A famous poet and calligrapher of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 會稽永真人 - His place of origin, Huiji, which is now part of Shaoxing, Zhejiang.
    • 孔子 - Confucius, the great philosopher and founder of Confucianism.
    • 少陵原 - Shaoling Plain, a location mentioned in the story.
    • 子貢 - One of Confucius' most famous disciples.

    Proper Nouns

    • 賀知章 - A famous poet and calligrapher of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 會稽永真人 - His place of origin, Huiji, which is now part of Shaoxing, Zhejiang.
    • 孔子 - Confucius, the great philosopher and founder of Confucianism.
    • 少陵原 - Shaoling Plain, a location mentioned in the story.
    • 子貢 - One of Confucius' most famous disciples.

    Final Translation:

    Tang Dynasty's He Zhizhang, a native of Yongcheng, Huiji. He passed the imperial examination and was selected as a jinshi, serving as Assistant Minister of Rites, Chief Librarian, and Tutor to the Crown Prince and other Princes. He was known for his unrestrained and free-spirited nature and his extraordinary imagination. In his eighties, he wrote poetry while drunk and asked his attendants, "How much paper do we have?" They replied, "We have ten sheets." He then wrote a petition for retirement, requesting to return to his hometown. The emperor, the Crown Prince, and other princes all wrote farewell poems, and he was bestowed the area of Yuezhou and Tianqu as a gift.

    Confucius was traveling through Shaoling Plain when he heard a woman crying very sorrowfully. He sent Zigan to ask her, "Why are you crying so sadly?" The woman replied, "Earlier, when cutting firewood, I lost an ornament." Confucius asked again, "Losing an ornament while cutting firewood is common, but why are you crying so sadly?" She answered, "It is not because I am grieving over losing one ornament. It is because I cannot forget the past." [12]

    唐天后朝，处士孙思邈居于嵩山修道。时大旱，有敕选洛阳德行僧徒数千百人于天宫寺讲《仁王经》，以祈雨泽。有二人在众中，须眉皓白。讲僧昙林遣人谓二老人曰：“罢后可过一院。”既至，问其所来，二老人曰：“某伊洛二水龙也，闻至言当得改化。”林曰：“讲经祈雨，二圣知之乎！”答曰：“安得不知然雨者，须天符乃能致之，居常何敢自施也”林曰：“为之奈何？”二老曰：“有修道人以章疏闻天，因而滂沱，某可力为之。”林乃入启。则天发使嵩阳召思邈。内殿飞章，其夕天雨大降。思邈亦不自明，退诣讲席，语林曰：“吾修心五十年，不为天知，何也？”因请问二老。二老答曰：“非利济生人，岂得升仙。”于是思邈归蜀青城山，撰《千金方》三十卷，既成，而白日冲天。 [13]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [13]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 唐天后朝 - The reign of Empress Wu Zetian, known as the 'Tianhou' (Heavenly Queen), during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 处士 - A recluse or a person of high moral character who chooses not to enter official service.
    • 嵩山 - Mount Song, one of the Five Great Mountains of China, known for its spiritual and cultural significance.
    • 修道 - To practice Daoism or to cultivate the Dao.
    • 大旱 - A severe drought.
    • 敕选 - Imperial decree to select.
    • 德行僧徒 - Monks with good moral conduct.
    • 讲《仁王经》 - To lecture on the *Rénwáng Jing*, a Buddhist scripture.
    • 祈雨泽 - To pray for rain.
    • 二圣 - The Buddha and the Bodhisattva.
    • 天符 - A divine mandate or celestial decree.
    • 章疏 - A memorial or petition.
    • 滂沱 - To pour down heavily or to rain heavily.
    • 冲天 - To ascend to heaven.
    • 千金方 - A famous medical text compiled by Sun Simiao, known as the *Qian Jin Fang* (Thousand Gold Prescriptions).

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 唐天后朝 - The reign of Empress Wu Zetian, who ruled as the only female emperor in Chinese history.
    • 孙思邈 - A renowned Chinese physician and alchemist, known for his contributions to traditional Chinese medicine.
    • 嵩山 - A sacred mountain in Henan Province, known for its Taoist and Buddhist temples.
    • 天宫寺 - A temple in Luoyang, Henan Province, where the monks were gathered for the lecture.
    • 《仁王经》 - A Buddhist scripture that emphasizes the protection of the Dharma.
    • 二圣 - The Buddha and the Bodhisattva, often invoked in Buddhist rituals.
    • 青城山 - A sacred mountain in Sichuan Province, known for its Taoist temples and natural beauty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 唐天后朝 - The reign of Empress Wu Zetian.
    • 孙思邈 - Sun Simiao, the renowned physician.
    • 嵩山 - Mount Song.
    • 天宫寺 - Tian Gong Temple.
    • 《仁王经》 - *Rénwáng Jing*.
    • 二圣 - The Buddha and the Bodhisattva.
    • 青城山 - Qingcheng Mountain.
    • 《千金方》 - *Qian Jin Fang*.

    During the reign of Empress Wu Zetian, the recluse Sun Simiao resided on Mount Song practicing Daoism. At that time, there was a severe drought, and an imperial decree selected several thousand monks and disciples with good moral conduct from Luoyang to gather at Tian Gong Temple to lecture on the Rénwáng Jing in order to pray for rain. Among the assembly, there were two elderly men with white beards and eyebrows. The lecturer, Master Tan Lin, sent someone to invite the two old men and said, "After the lecture, you may visit a courtyard." Upon arrival, he asked where they had come from. The two old men replied, "We are the spirits of the Yellow and Luo rivers. We heard that your words would bring about transformation." Lin asked, "Do the Buddha and the Bodhisattva know about this?" They answered, "How could they not know? But to bring rain, one must have a divine mandate from heaven. We dare not act on our own." Lin then asked, "What should we do?" The two old men said, "There is a Daoist who can write a memorial to heaven, and then it will rain heavily. We can help with this." Lin then went inside to inform the empress. Empress Wu Zetian sent envoys to Mount Song to summon Sun Simiao. That evening, after Sun Simiao presented a memorial in the inner palace, it rained heavily. Sun Simiao did not take credit for himself but returned to the lecture hall and spoke to Lin, saying, "I have cultivated my mind for fifty years, yet the heavens do not know me. Why is that?" He then asked the two old men. The two old men replied, "If you do not benefit humanity, how could you ascend to immortality?" Thus, Sun Simiao returned to Qingcheng Mountain in Sichuan and compiled the Qian Jin Fang, thirty volumes in total. After completing it, he ascended to heaven during the daytime. [13]

    晋尚书令卞壶死苏峻之难，葬在上元县。后盗发其墓，见壶鬓发苍白，面色如生，两手皆拳，甲穿于手背，安帝赐钱十万，令改葬焉。
    　　挚虞《要注》云：晋武帝时，有彭权为侍中。帝问“髦头”义，权答曰：“秦时有奇怪，触山截水，无不崩溃，惟畏髦头。”帝乃令虎贲之士戴之，以卫左右。
    　　河东裴章者，其父胄，曾镇荆州。门僧昙照，道行甚高，能知休咎。章幼时为昙照所重，言其官班位望过于其父。章弱冠，父为娶李氏女。乃三十年余，章从职太原，弃其妻于洛中，过门不入，别有所挈。李氏自感其薄命，常褐衣ヮ髻，读佛书，蔬食。又十年，严经自荆州移镇太原，昙照随之。章因见昙照叙旧。照惊噫久之，谓之曰：“贫道五十年前常谓郎君必贵，今削尽，何也”章自以薄妻之事启之。照曰：“夫人生魂诉上帝，以罪处君。”后旬日，为其下以刀划腹于浴斛，五脏堕地遂死。 [14]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [14]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 晋尚书令卞壶死苏峻之难 - Refers to the death of Bian Hu, a high-ranking official during the Battle of Jiankang (also known as the Battle of the Western Capital), which took place during the reign of Emperor Mu of Jin.
    • 葬在上元县 - Indicates that Bian Hu was buried in Shangyuan County, which is located in present-day Jiangsu Province.
    • 后盗发其墓 - Means 'later, tomb robbers opened his tomb.'
    • 见壶鬓发苍白，面色如生，两手皆拳，甲穿于手背 - Describes the condition of Bian Hu's body when the tomb was opened: his hair and beard were white, his face looked as if he were alive, his hands were clenched into fists, and his nails had penetrated his palms.
    • 安帝赐钱十万，令改葬焉 - States that Emperor An granted ten thousand coins to have Bian Hu reburied.
    • 挚虞《要注》云 - Indicates a citation from Zhi Yu's *Yaozhu* (Essential Annotations).
    • 晋武帝时，有彭权为侍中 - Refers to the time of Emperor Wu of Jin when Peng Quan served as a high-ranking official.
    • 帝问“髦头”义 - Means 'the emperor asked about the meaning of 'maotou'.
    • 权答曰：“秦时有奇怪，触山截水，无不崩溃，惟畏髦头。” - Peng Quan's response explains that 'maotou' refers to something from the Qin dynasty that could destroy mountains and rivers but was feared by the 'maotou'.
    • 帝乃令虎贲之士戴之，以卫左右 - States that Emperor Wu ordered the Tiger Guards to wear 'maotou' to protect him.
    • 河东裴章者，其父胄，曾镇荆州 - Introduces Pei Zhang, whose father Pei Zou once governed Jingzhou.
    • 门僧昙照，道行甚高，能知休咎 - Describes Men Seng Tan Zao, a monk with high spiritual attainments who could predict good and bad omens.
    • 章幼时为昙照所重，言其官班位望过于其父 - States that Pei Zhang was highly regarded by Tan Zao, who predicted that Pei Zhang would surpass his father in rank and reputation.
    • 章弱冠，父为娶李氏女 - Indicates that Pei Zhang, when he came of age, was married to a woman from the Li family.
    • 乃三十年余，章从职太原，弃其妻于洛中，过门不入，别有所挈 - Describes how Pei Zhang, after thirty years, left his wife in Luoyang and moved to Taiyuan without visiting her, taking another woman with him.
    • 李氏自感其薄命，常褐衣ヮ髻，读佛书，蔬食 - States that Pei Zhang's first wife felt her fate was unfortunate, so she dressed simply, wore her hair in a simple style, read Buddhist scriptures, and ate a vegetarian diet.
    • 又十年，严经自荆州移镇太原，昙照随之 - Indicates that after another ten years, Yan Jing moved from Jingzhou to govern Taiyuan, and Tan Zao followed him.
    • 章因见昙照叙旧 - States that Pei Zhang met Tan Zao and reminisced about the past.
    • 照惊噫久之，谓之曰：“贫道五十年前常谓郎君必贵，今削尽，何也” - Tan Zao was surprised and exclaimed for a long time, saying, 'Fifty years ago, I always said that you would become a nobleman. Now it seems all is lost, why is this?'
    • 章自以薄妻之事启之 - Pei Zhang revealed to Tan Zao that he had abandoned his first wife for another woman.
    • 照曰：“夫人生魂诉上帝，以罪处君。” - Tan Zao explained that Pei Zhang's first wife had complained to the heavens about him, and he was punished for his sins.
    • 后旬日，为其下以刀划腹于浴斛，五脏堕地遂死 - States that ten days later, Pei Zhang was killed by his subordinates who cut open his abdomen in a bathtub, causing his internal organs to fall out and leading to his death.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋尚书令卞壶 - Bian Hu was a high-ranking official during the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 苏峻之难 - Refers to the Battle of Jiankang, a rebellion led by Su Jun against the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 安帝 - Emperor An of Jin, who ruled from 386 to 396.
    • 挚虞《要注》 - Zhi Yu's *Yaozhu* (Essential Annotations) is a historical commentary.
    • 晋武帝 - Emperor Wu of Jin, who ruled from 265 to 290.
    • 彭权 - Peng Quan was a high-ranking official during the Jin dynasty.
    • 髦头 - A term used in ancient Chinese to describe a type of hat or helmet.
    • 门僧昙照 - Men Seng Tan Zao was a monk with high spiritual attainments.
    • 河东裴章 - Pei Zhang was a person from Hedong (present-day Shanxi Province).
    • 严经 - Yan Jing was a high-ranking official during the Jin dynasty.
    • 佛书 - Buddhist scriptures.
    • 蔬食 - Vegetarian diet.
    • 上帝 - The supreme deity in Chinese mythology.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋 - The Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 尚书令 - A high-ranking official position.
    • 卞壶 - Bian Hu, a high-ranking official during the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 苏峻之难 - The Battle of Jiankang.
    • 安帝 - Emperor An of Jin.
    • 挚虞《要注》 - Zhi Yu's *Yaozhu* (Essential Annotations).
    • 晋武帝 - Emperor Wu of Jin.
