    • 彭权 - Peng Quan, a high-ranking official during the Jin dynasty.
    • 髦头 - A type of hat or helmet.
    • 门僧昙照 - Men Seng Tan Zao.

    During the Battle of Jiankang, Jiang Shi's Minister of Works, Bian Hu, died. He was buried in Shangyuan County. Later, tomb robbers opened his tomb and found that his hair and beard were white, his face looked as if he were alive, his hands were clenched into fists, and his nails had penetrated his palms. Emperor An of Jin granted ten thousand coins to have him reburied.

    Zhi Yu's Yaozhu (Essential Annotations) records: During the reign of Emperor Wu of Jin, Peng Quan served as a high-ranking official. The emperor asked about the meaning of "maotou." Peng Quan replied, "In the Qin dynasty, there was something strange that could destroy mountains and rivers but was feared by the 'maotou.'" The emperor then ordered the Tiger Guards to wear "maotou" to protect him.

    Pei Zhang of Hedong was the son of Pei Zou, who once governed Jingzhou. Men Seng Tan Zao was a monk with high spiritual attainments who could predict good and bad omens. When Pei Zhang was young, he was highly regarded by Tan Zao, who predicted that he would surpass his father in rank and reputation. When Pei Zhang came of age, his father married him to a woman from the Li family. After thirty years, Pei Zhang went to serve in Taiyuan, leaving his wife in Luoyang and not visiting her, taking another woman with him. Pei Zhang's first wife felt her fate was unfortunate, so she dressed simply, wore her hair in a simple style, read Buddhist scriptures, and ate a vegetarian diet. After another ten years, Yan Jing moved from Jingzhou to govern Taiyuan, and Tan Zao followed him. Pei Zhang met Tan Zao and reminisced about the past. Tan Zao was surprised and exclaimed for a long time, saying, "Fifty years ago, I always said that you would become a nobleman. Now it seems all is lost, why is this?" Pei Zhang revealed to Tan Zao that he had abandoned his first wife for another woman. Tan Zao explained, "Your first wife complained to the heavens about you, and you were punished for your sins." Ten days later, Pei Zhang was killed by his subordinates who cut open his abdomen in a bathtub, causing his internal organs to fall out and leading to his death. [14]

    唐贞观中，张宝藏为金吾长史，尝因下直归栎阳，路逢少年畋猎，割鲜野食。倚树长叹曰：“张宝藏身年七十，未尝得一食酒肉如此者，可悲哉！”傍有一僧，指曰：“六十日内，官登三品，何足叹也。”言讫不见。宝藏异之，即时还京。太宗苦病痢疾，众医不效，即下诏问殿廷左右，有能治此疾者，当重赏之。宝藏曾困此疾，即具疏以“乳煎荜茇方”进。上服之，立差。宣下宰臣，与五品官。魏征难之，逾月不进拟。上疾复作，问左右曰：“吾前服乳煎荜茇有效。”复令进之，一啜又平复。上问曰：“尝令与进方人五品官，不见除授，何也”征惧曰：“奉诏之际，未知文武二吏。”上怒曰：“治得宰相，不妨授三品。我天子也，岂不及汝邪”乃厉声曰：“与三品文官。”立授鸿胪卿，时正六十日矣。 [15]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [15]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 可悲哉 - Rhetorical expression meaning 'how pitiable!'
    • 六十日内，官登三品 - Means 'within sixty days, you will be promoted to a third-rank official.'
    • 立授 - Means 'immediately granted.'
    • 六十日矣 - Means 'exactly sixty days.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 唐贞观中 - Refers to the Zhenguan era of the Tang dynasty, known for its prosperity and good governance under Emperor Taizong.
    • 张宝藏 - Historical figure, Zhang Baocang, a military official.
    • 金吾长史 - High-ranking military position in the Tang dynasty.
    • 鸿胪卿 - High-ranking official responsible for foreign affairs and ceremonies.
    • 太宗 - Refers to Emperor Taizong of Tang, the second emperor of the Tang dynasty.
    • 乳煎荜茇方 - Medicinal prescription, 'formula of milk and long pepper decoction.'
    • 魏征 - Historical figure, Wei Zheng, a famous advisor to Emperor Taizong known for his honesty and integrity.
    • 三品官 - Refers to a third-rank official in the Tang dynasty's nine-rank system.

    Proper Nouns

    • 唐贞观 - The Zhenguan era of the Tang dynasty.
    • 张宝藏 - Zhang Baocang, a historical figure.
    • 金吾长史 - A title.
    • 栎阳 - Liyang, an ancient city.
    • 太宗 - Emperor Taizong of Tang.
    • 魏征 - Wei Zheng, a historical figure.
    • 鸿胪卿 - A title.
    • 乳煎荜茇方 - A medicinal prescription.

    During the Zhenguan era of the Tang dynasty, Zhang Baocang served as the Commandant of the Imperial Guards. Once, after finishing his duties, he returned to Liyang and encountered a young man hunting and feasting on freshly killed game. Leaning against a tree, Zhang Baocang sighed deeply, saying, "At seventy years old, I have never tasted such a feast of wine and meat. How pitiable!" Beside him stood a monk who pointed and said, "Within sixty days, you will be promoted to a third-rank official. There is no need to lament." The monk then vanished. Zhang Baocang found this strange and immediately returned to the capital. Emperor Taizong was suffering from dysentery, and none of the doctors could cure him. He then issued an edict to the officials around him, promising a generous reward to anyone who could treat his illness. Zhang Baocang, who had once suffered from this disease, submitted a prescription for "milk and long pepper decoction." The emperor took it and was immediately cured. The edict was then issued to the prime minister, granting Zhang Baocang a fifth-rank official position. Wei Zheng hesitated and did not submit the proposal for over a month. When the emperor's illness recurred, he asked the officials, "Did I not previously take the milk and long pepper decoction and find it effective?" They were ordered to prepare it again, and after one dose, the emperor was once more cured. The emperor then asked, "I once ordered that the person who submitted the prescription be granted a fifth-rank official position, but I did not see it carried out. Why?" Wei Zheng, fearing retribution, replied, "At the time of the edict, I did not know whether the person was a civil or military official." The emperor became angry and said, "If you can treat the prime minister, there is no reason not to grant him a third-rank position. I am the Son of Heaven, how could I not grant you a promotion?" He then sternly said, "Grant him a third-rank civil official position." Zhang Baocang was immediately appointed as the Minister of Ceremonies, exactly sixty days after the monk's prophecy. [15]

    陶弘景隐居茆山，梁武帝每有大事，飞诏与之参诀。时人谓隐居为“山中宰相”。
    　　梁张僧繇善画，为吴兴太守。武帝每思诸王在外藩者，即令僧繇乘传往写其貌，如对其面。尝于江陵天皇寺画佛并仲尼及十哲，帝曰：“释门之内画此，何也”对曰：“异日赖之。”至后周焚灭佛教，以此殿有儒圣，独不焚之。又于金陵安乐寺画四龙，不点睛。人问之，答曰：“点则飞去。”众人以为虚诞，固请点之。顷刻雷霆，二龙乘云腾上，其二不点者犹在。画之通神若此。 [16]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [16]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 隱居 - Reclusive residence, referring to living in seclusion.
    • 飛诏 - Rapid or urgent imperial decree.
    • 參诀 - Seeking advice and making decisions together.
    • 山中宰相 - Metaphorical title meaning 'the chancellor of the mountains,' used to describe someone who is wise and influential despite being in seclusion.
    • 乘传 - Traveling by official means.
    • 写其貌 - Depict his appearance, meaning to paint or draw a person's likeness.
    • 如对其面 - As if facing him, meaning the portrait is so lifelike.
    • 异日赖之 - Rely on it in the future, suggesting that the paintings will be useful in the future.
    • 焚灭佛教 - Burn and destroy Buddhism, referring to the persecution of Buddhism.
    • 通神 - Communicating with the divine, meaning highly skilled or supernatural ability.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 陶弘景 - Famous alchemist, physician, and scholar of the Liang Dynasty.
    • 茆山 - Mountain in Jiangsu Province, known for its scenic beauty and historical significance.
    • 梁武帝 - Emperor Wu of Liang, a significant ruler of the Liang Dynasty.
    • 吴兴太守 - Governor of Wu County (present-day Huzhou, Zhejiang).
    • 张僧繇 - Renowned painter of the Liang Dynasty, known for his realistic and lifelike portraits.
    • 江陵 - Ancient city in Hubei Province, an important cultural and political center.
    • 天皇寺 - Temple in Jingzhou (modern-day Jingzhou, Hubei).
    • 仲尼 - Another name for Confucius.
    • 十哲 - The ten most prominent disciples of Confucius.
    • 金陵 - Another name for Nanjing, the capital of the Liang Dynasty.
    • 安乐寺 - Temple in Nanjing.
    • 后周 - The Northern Zhou Dynasty, which followed the Northern Wei Dynasty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 陶弘景 - Tao Hongjing
    • 茆山 - Maoshan
    • 梁武帝 - Emperor Wu of Liang
    • 吴兴太守 - Governor of Wu County
    • 张僧繇 - Zhang Sengyao
    • 江陵 - Jingzhou
    • 天皇寺 - Tianhuang Temple
    • 仲尼 - Confucius
    • 十哲 - Ten Philosophers
    • 金陵 - Nanjing
    • 安乐寺 - Anle Temple
    • 后周 - Northern Zhou Dynasty

    Tao Hongjing lived in seclusion on Maoshan Mountain. Whenever Emperor Wu of Liang had important matters, he would send urgent imperial decrees to consult Tao Hongjing. At the time, people referred to Tao Hongjing as the "Chancellor of the Mountains."

    Zhang Sengyao, a renowned painter of the Liang Dynasty, served as the governor of Wu County. Whenever Emperor Wu thought of his sons who were stationed in the provinces, he would send Zhang Sengyao to travel by official means to paint their likenesses, which were so lifelike that they seemed as if the subjects were present. Once, Zhang Sengyao painted a Buddha and Confucius along with his ten most prominent disciples in the Tianhuang Temple in Jingzhou. The emperor asked, "Why are you painting these Confucian figures within the Buddhist temple?" Zhang Sengyao replied, "We will rely on these paintings in the future." When the Northern Zhou Dynasty persecuted Buddhism, this temple was spared because it housed the Confucian sages.

    Zhang Sengyao also painted four dragons in the Anle Temple in Nanjing, but he did not paint their eyes. When people asked him why, he said, "If I paint their eyes, they will fly away." Many people thought this was absurd and insisted that he paint their eyes. Immediately, thunder and lightning struck, and two of the dragons flew up into the sky. The other two, which were not painted, remained on the wall. Zhang Sengyao's skill in painting was truly divine. [16]

    晋何曾，家法修整，年五十以上，每见妻，必正衣冠，自坐面南，妻坐面北。上酒酬酢，一岁不至再三。年八十而终焉。
    　　唐贞元初，河南少尹李则卒，未殓。有一朱衣人投刺申吊，自称苏郎中。既入，哀恸尤甚。俄顷，亡者遂起，与之相搏，家人子弟惊走出堂。二人闭门殴击，抵暮方息。孝子乃敢入，见二尸并卧一床，长短、形状、姿貌、鬓髯、衣服一无差异。于是聚族不能定识，遂同棺葬之。
    　　汉武陈皇后，本其姑公主鼓女也，色衰，弃后宫，乃以黄金五百斤赠司马相如，令作赋。赋成，帝见之，再得宠幸。
    　　侯景常有一瘤，如小龟。每战胜，龟则起。及其败死之日，瘤入一寸。
    　　东汉耿秉镇抚西边，单于匈奴多怀其恩，闻秉卒，有嫠面流血哀痛者，举国发丧。 [17]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [17]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 家法修整 - Refers to strict family discipline and rules.
    • 年五十以上，每見妻，必正衣冠，自坐面南，妻坐面北 - Describes a formal and respectful way of greeting one's wife, with the husband sitting facing south and the wife facing north.
    • 上酒酬酢，一岁不至再三 - Means that drinking and toasting are done sparingly, no more than three times a year.
    • 年八十而终焉 - Indicates that the person lived to be eighty years old and then passed away.
    • 河南少尹李则卒，未殓 - Describes the death of Li Ze, a deputy governor of Henan, before he was buried.
    • 朱衣人投刺申吊，自称苏郎中 - Refers to a man in red clothing who presented a card to express condolences, identifying himself as a Langzhong (an official title).
    • 哀恸尤甚 - Means that the visitor was extremely sad and grief-stricken.
    • 俄顷，亡者遂起，与之相搏 - Describes how the deceased suddenly got up and fought with the visitor.
    • 孝子乃敢入，见二尸并卧一床，长短、形状、姿貌、鬓髯、衣服一无差异 - The son was able to enter and saw two bodies lying side by side on a bed, indistinguishable in height, shape, appearance, hair, and clothing.
    • 聚族不能定识，遂同棺葬之 - The family could not identify which body was the deceased, so they buried them together in one coffin.
    • 本其姑公主鼓女也 - Refers to Tanchao, the wife of Emperor Wu, being the daughter of a princess.
    • 色衰，弃后宫 - Indicates that Tanchao was discarded from the imperial harem because her beauty faded.
    • 乃以黄金五百斤赠司马相如，令作赋 - Describes how Tanchao gave 500 gold pieces to Sima Xiangru, a famous writer, to compose a poem.
    • 赋成，帝见之，再得宠幸 - The poem was well-received by the emperor, and Tanchao regained his favor.
    • 侯景常有一瘤，如小龟 - Describes Hou Jing, a notorious general, having a tumor shaped like a small turtle.
    • 每战胜，龟则起 - The tumor would rise whenever he won a battle.
    • 及其败死之日，瘤入一寸 - When he was defeated and died, the tumor disappeared.
    • 东汉耿秉镇抚西边，单于匈奴多怀其恩 - Describes General Geng Bing's role in pacifying the western frontier and earning the gratitude of many Xiongnu chieftains.
    • 闻秉卒，有嫠面流血哀痛者，举国发丧 - The news of Geng Bing's death caused many people to mourn, some even cutting their faces and shedding tears, leading to a national mourning period.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋何曾 - A prominent figure during the Jin Dynasty known for his strict family discipline.
    • 唐贞元初 - The early period of the Tang Dynasty, specifically the reign of Emperor Dezong.
    • 河南少尹李则 - A deputy governor of Henan during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 司马相如 - A famous writer and poet during the Han Dynasty, known for his romantic poetry and prose.
    • 侯景 - A general during the Southern and Northern Dynasties, known for his treachery and the subsequent Anarchy of the Five Dynasties.
    • 东汉耿秉 - A military general during the Eastern Han Dynasty, known for his campaigns against the Xiongnu.
    • 单于匈奴 - Refers to the Xiongnu, a nomadic people who were often in conflict with the Chinese empires.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋何曾 - A historical figure from the Jin Dynasty.
    • 唐贞元初 - A specific time period in the Tang Dynasty.
    • 河南少尹李则 - A historical figure from the Tang Dynasty.
    • 司马相如 - A historical figure from the Han Dynasty.
    • 侯景 - A historical figure from the Southern and Northern Dynasties.
    • 东汉耿秉 - A historical figure from the Eastern Han Dynasty.
    • 单于匈奴 - Refers to the Xiongnu, a historical ethnic group.

    晋何曾，家法修整，年五十以上，每见妻，必正衣冠，自坐面南，妻坐面北。上酒酬酢，一岁不至再三。年八十而终焉。

    During the Jin Dynasty, He Zeng was known for his strict family discipline. When he was over fifty years old, he always dressed formally and sat facing south when greeting his wife, who sat facing north. They would drink and toast sparingly, no more than three times a year. He lived to be eighty years old and then passed away.

    唐贞元初，河南少尹李则卒，未殓。有一朱衣人投刺申吊，自称苏郎中。既入，哀恸尤甚。俄顷，亡者遂起，与之相搏，家人子弟惊走出堂。二人闭门殴击，抵暮方息。孝子乃敢入，见二尸并卧一床，长短、形状、姿貌、鬓髯、衣服一无差异。于是聚族不能定识，遂同棺葬之。

    In the early years of the Tang Dynasty's reign of Emperor Dezong, Li Ze, the deputy governor of Henan, died but was not yet buried. A man in red clothing presented a card to express condolences, identifying himself as a Langzhong (an official title). Upon entering, he was extremely grief-stricken. Shortly after, the deceased suddenly got up and fought with him. The family members and children were so shocked that they ran out of the house. The two closed the door and continued to fight until dusk. The son then dared to enter and saw two bodies lying side by side on a bed, indistinguishable in height, shape, appearance, hair, and clothing. The family could not identify which body was the deceased, so they buried them together in one coffin.

    汉武陈皇后，本其姑公主鼓女也，色衰，弃后宫，乃以黄金五百斤赠司马相如，令作赋。赋成，帝见之，再得宠幸。

    Emperor Wu's consort, Tanchao, was originally the daughter of a princess. When her beauty faded, she was discarded from the imperial harem. She then gave 500 gold pieces to Sima Xiangru, a famous writer, to compose a poem. The poem was well-received by the emperor, and Tanchao regained his favor.

    侯景常有一瘤，如小龟。每战胜，龟则起。及其败死之日，瘤入一寸。

    Hou Jing, a notorious general during the Southern and Northern Dynasties, had a tumor shaped like a small turtle. The tumor would rise whenever he won a battle. When he was defeated and died, the tumor disappeared.

    东汉耿秉镇抚西边，单于匈奴多怀其恩，闻秉卒，有嫠面流血哀痛者，举国发丧。

    During the Eastern Han Dynasty, General Geng Bing pacified the western frontier and earned the gratitude of many Xiongnu chieftains. Upon hearing of Geng Bing's death, many people mourned, some even cutting their faces and shedding tears, leading to a national mourning period. [17]

    唐元和初，有天水赵云，客游，过中部县。县寮有宴，吏擒一囚至，其罪不甚重，官寮愿纵之。云醉，因劝加于刑责，于是杖之二十。累月，云出塞，行及芦子关，道逢一人，邀之言款。日暮，延云下道过其居，去路数里。于是命酒偶酌，既而问之曰：“君省相识否”云曰：“未尝。此行实昧平昔。”乃曰：“前月中部值君，遭罹横罪。与君素无仇隙，为君所劝，因被重刑。”云遽起，谢之。其人曰：“吾望子久矣，岂虞于此获雪小耻。”乃命左右拽入一室，室有大坑，深三丈余，中唯贮酒糟数十斛。剥去其衣，推云于中。饥食其糟，渴饮其汁，旦夕昏民。几一月，乃传出之，使人蹙鼻额，ソ捩肢体、手指、肩髀，皆改于旧，提出风中，倏然凝定，至于声韵亦改，以为贱隶。弟为御史出按灵州狱。云以前事密疏示之。其弟告于观察使李。由是发卒讨寻，尽得奸人，而覆灭其党。临刑亦无隐匿，云前后变改人者数代矣。 [18]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [18]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 客游 - Means 'traveling as a guest in ,' where  is an ancient place name, likely referring to a region or city.
    • 酒糟 - Wine lees, the residue left after fermentation.
    • 遭罹横罪 - Means 'suffered a wrongful punishment.'
    • 变改人者数代矣 - Means 'had undergone several generations of changes.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 唐元和初 - The Yuanhe era (806-820 AD) of the Tang dynasty.
    •  - An ancient place name, likely referring to a region or city.
    • 中部县 - Zhongbu County, an ancient administrative division.
    • 县寮 - Local officials or bureaucrats.
    • 吏 - Officials or servants.
    • 囚 - Prisoner.
    • 官寮 - Officials or bureaucrats.
    • 赵云 - Zhou, the main character.
    • 芦子关 - Lusi Pass, an ancient pass or checkpoint.
    • 御史 - Censor, an official responsible for investigating and reporting on government affairs.
    • 灵州 - Lingzhou, an ancient administrative division.
    • 观察使 - Regional governor, an official responsible for overseeing a region.
    • 李 - Li, the regional governor mentioned in the text.

    Proper Nouns

    • 唐元和初 - The Yuanhe era of the Tang dynasty.
    •  - An ancient place name.
    • 中部县 - Zhongbu County.
    • 赵云 - Zhou, the main character.
    • 芦子关 - Lusi Pass.
    • 御史 - Censor.
    • 灵州 - Lingzhou.
    • 观察使 - Regional governor.
    • 李 - Li, the regional governor.

    In the early years of the Yuanhe era of the Tang dynasty, there was a man from Tianshui named Zhou who traveled as a guest in  and passed through Zhongbu County. The local officials held a banquet, and a prisoner was captured and brought to the banquet. The prisoner's crime was not very serious, and the officials wished to release him. Zhou was drunk and therefore persuaded to impose additional punishment, so the prisoner was beaten with a stick twenty times. After several months, Zhou went out of the frontier and traveled up to Lusi Pass. On the road, he met a man who invited him for a conversation. As evening fell, the man invited Zhou to his home, which was several miles off the road. They drank wine together, and the man then asked, "Do you recognize me?" Zhou replied, "I have never met you before. This journey is indeed unfamiliar." The man said, "Last month in Zhongbu County, I met you and suffered a wrongful punishment. I had no grudge against you, but because of your persuasion, I was given a severe punishment." Zhou immediately stood up and apologized. The man said, "I have been waiting for you for a long time, did not expect to clear this small disgrace here." He then ordered his attendants to drag Zhou into a room. The room had a large pit, about thirty feet deep, filled with dozens of hu of wine lees. They stripped him of his clothes and pushed Zhou into the pit. He ate the lees when hungry and drank the juice when thirsty, living in darkness and confusion day and night. After about a month, he was taken out. His nose and forehead were flattened, his limbs, fingers, shoulders, and hips were twisted, all changed from their original state. He was taken out into the wind and suddenly became solid. His voice and tone also changed, and he was considered a lowly servant. His younger brother, who was a censor, went to investigate the prison in Lingzhou. Zhou secretly wrote a report about the previous events and showed it to his brother. His brother reported it to the regional governor Li. As a result, soldiers were sent to investigate, and all the villains were captured, and their gang was destroyed. Even at the time of execution, they did not hide anything. Zhou had undergone several generations of changes. [18]

    郑覃历官三十任，未尝出都门，便登相位，以至于终。
    　　唐萧尝因内宴，上曰：“自知一座最贵者，先把酒。”时长孙无忌、房玄龄等相顾未言，引手取杯。帝问曰：“卿有何说”曰：“臣是梁朝天子儿、隋朝皇后弟、尚书左仆射、天子亲家翁。”太宗抚掌，极欢而罢。
    　　唐贞观初，丹阳令王琼三年调集，遭黜。琼甚愤惋，乃赍百千，诣茆山道士叶灵中，求章奏以问吉凶。灵中年九十，强为奏之，其章随香烟飞去，缥眇不见。食顷后坠地，有朱书批其末，云：“受金百两，折禄三年；枉杀二人，死后处断。”一岁，琼无疾暴卒。 [19]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [19]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 鄭覃歷官三十任，未嘗出都門，便登相位，以至于终 - Describes Zheng Tan's career trajectory, emphasizing his long service and rapid rise to power.
    • 自知一座最贵者，先把酒 - A rule of etiquette at banquets, indicating that the most esteemed person should drink first.
    • 相顾未言 - Indicates a moment of hesitation or surprise, where people look at each other without speaking.
    • 引手取杯 - A physical action indicating assertiveness, where someone reaches out to take a cup.
    • 抚掌 - A gesture of approval or amusement, where someone claps hands.
    • 赍百千 - Indicates carrying a significant sum of money, specifically a hundred thousand coins.
    • 食顷后坠地 - Indicates a time frame, where something falls to the ground after a meal.
    • 朱书批其末 - Indicates a final verdict or judgment, where red writing is at the end.
    • 无疾暴卒 - Indicates an unexpected death without illness.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 唐萧 - Xiao Xun of the Tang dynasty, a historical figure.
    • 唐贞观初 - The early years of the Zhenguan era of the Tang dynasty, a specific historical period.
    • 丹阳令王琼 - Wang Qiong, the magistrate of Danyang, a specific historical figure.
    • 长孙无忌 - Changsun Wuji, a prominent official and statesman of the Tang dynasty.
    • 房玄龄 - Fang Xuanling, a prominent official and statesman of the Tang dynasty.
    • 茅山道士叶灵中 - Ye Lingzhong, a Daoist master at Maoshan, a specific historical figure.
    • 太宗 - Emperor Taizong, the second emperor of the Tang dynasty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 郑覃 - Zheng Tan, a historical figure.
    • 萧 - Xiao Xun, a historical figure.
    • 长孙无忌 - Changsun Wuji, a historical figure.
    • 房玄龄 - Fang Xuanling, a historical figure.
    • 叶灵中 - Ye Lingzhong, a historical figure.
    • 茅山 - Maoshan, a specific location.
    • 太宗 - Emperor Taizong, a historical figure.
    • 王琼 - Wang Qiong, a historical figure.
    • 丹阳 - Danyang, a specific location.
    • 贞观 - The Zhenguan era, a specific historical period.

    Zheng Tan served in thirty different positions, never leaving the capital, and immediately ascended to the position of chancellor, until his death.

    During the reign of Emperor Taizong, Xiao Xun once attended an inner palace banquet. The emperor said, "The one who knows he is the most esteemed in the room should drink first." At that time, Changsun Wuji, Fang Xuanling, and others looked at each other without speaking. Xiao Xun reached out to take the cup. The emperor asked, "What do you have to say?" Xiao Xun replied, "I am the son of a Tianzi from the Liang dynasty, the younger brother of a Huanghou from the Sui dynasty, the Left Shangshu, and the son-in-law of the emperor." Emperor Taizong clapped his hands and was extremely pleased, and the banquet ended with great joy.

    In the early years of the Zhenguan era of the Tang dynasty, Wang Qiong, the magistrate of Danyang, was demoted after three years of service. Qiong was very angry and saddened, so he carried a hundred thousand coins to visit the Daoist master Ye Lingzhong at Maoshan to seek a talisman to inquire about his fortune. Ye Lingzhong, who was ninety years old, reluctantly wrote the talisman for him. The talisman flew away with the incense smoke, disappearing into the distance. After a meal, it fell to the ground, with red writing at the end, which read, "Accepting a hundred taels of gold, deducting three years of salary; wrongfully killing two people, to be judged after death." One year later, Qiong died suddenly without illness. [19]

    唐初有僧玄奘往西域取经，一去十七年。始去之日，于齐州灵岩寺院，有松一本立于庭，奘以手摩其枝曰：“吾西去求佛教，汝可西长。若归，即此枝东向，使吾门人弟子知之。”及去，年年西指，约长数丈。一年忽东向指，门人弟子曰：“教主归矣。”乃西迎之。奘果还归，得佛经六百部。至今众谓之“摩顶松。”
    　　东晋大将军赵固所乘马暴卒，将军悲惋⊥至，吏不敢通。郭璞造门语曰：“余能活此马。”将军遽召见。璞令三十人悉持长竿，东行三十里，遇丘陵社林，即散击，俄顷擒一兽如猿。持归至马前，兽以鼻吸马，马起跃如。今以猕猴置马厩，此其义也。
    　　《说苑》曰：汤时大旱七年，煎沙烂石。于是使人以三足鼎祝山川，教之祝曰：“政不节耶使民疾耶苞苴行耶谗夫昌耶宫室崇耶女谒盛耶何不雨之极也”言未既，天大雨。
    　　汉霍光立宣帝，帝谒太庙为初献。帝行而光在后，帝恐惧，如负荆棘。 [20]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [20]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 為之一空 - Set phrase meaning completely emptied; fields were left barren.

    Proper Nouns

    • 紹興 - Era name of the Southern Song; rendered as Shaoxing.

    During the early Tang Dynasty, there was a monk named Xuanzang who traveled to the Western Regions to obtain Buddhist scriptures, and he was gone for seventeen years. On the day he departed, at Lingyan Temple in Qi Prefecture, there was a pine tree standing in the courtyard. Xuanzang placed his hand on one of its branches and said, "I am going west to seek Buddhism. You may grow westward. If I return, let this branch point eastward so that my disciples may know." As he left, the branch of the tree pointed west each year, growing about ten feet long. One year, it suddenly pointed eastward, and his disciples said, "The Master has returned." They then went west to meet him. Indeed, Xuanzang returned, bringing back six hundred volumes of Buddhist scriptures. To this day, the tree is known as the "Mò Dǐng Sōng" (摩顶松).

    During the Eastern Jin Dynasty, General Zhao Gu's horse died suddenly, and the general was deeply grieved. His officials dared not disturb him. Guo Pu came to the gate and said, "I can revive this horse." The general immediately summoned him. Guo Pu ordered thirty men to carry long poles and travel thirty miles east, where they encountered a hill and a forest believed to be inhabited by spirits. They scattered and attacked, and soon captured an animal resembling a monkey. They brought it back to the horse and placed it in front of it. The monkey used its nose to breathe on the horse, and the horse jumped up as if alive. Today, monkeys are kept in stables, and this is the reason for it.

    The Shuo Yuan (说苑) states: During the reign of King Tang, there was a severe drought that lasted seven years, and the sand and stones were so hot that they were almost melted. The king then ordered the use of a three-legged tripod to perform a ritual for the mountains and rivers, teaching the ritual words: "Is the government not disciplined? Is the people suffering? Are bribes being offered? Are slanderers flourishing? Are the palaces too grand? Are women exerting too much influence? Why is there no rain?" Before he finished speaking, it began to rain heavily.

    During the Han Dynasty, Huo Guang installed Emperor Xuan, and the emperor performed the initial offering at the ancestral temple. As he walked, Huo Guang followed behind, and the emperor felt deeply ashamed, as if carrying thorns. [20]

    后汉贾琮为冀州刺史，传车垂赤帷裳，琮升车褰帏曰：“刺史当远视广听，纠察善恶，何垂帷裳以自掩塞乎”百姓闻之，振悚耳目。
    　　吴隐之为广州刺史。旧有贪泉，人饮之则贪黩。隐之酌而饮之，兼赋诗曰：“古人云此水，一歃怀千金。试使夷齐饮，终当不易心。”又居母丧，过礼，家贫，无以候宵分，常有双鹤至夜半惊唳，隐之起哭，不失其时。
    　　伍员吹箫行乞，食于市。
    　　庖丁善解牛，投刃皆虚。丁曰：“臣始解牛之时，所见无非全牛。三年之后，未尝见全牛也。及今十九年而刃无肯綮，若初发硎，游刃必有余地也。”
    　　郢人以垩漫其鼻，薄如蝇翼，使匠石斫之。匠石运斤成风，尽去其垩而不伤鼻。
    　　唐高宗尝苦头风而目闭心乱，乃召医工。工曰：“当于眉间刺血，即差。”天后怒曰：“天子头是汝出血处”命扑之。帝曰：“若因血获差，幸也。”遂针之，血出，溅黼衣，眼遂明而悉复平。天后自抱绘帛以赠医工。 [21]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [21]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 垂赤帷裳 - Metaphor for the ceremonial robe of a high-ranking official, symbolizing authority and status.
    • 遠視廣聽 - Means 'to look far and listen widely,' a metaphor for broad and impartial oversight.
    • 糾察善惡 - Means 'to investigate and correct good and evil,' referring to the duty of a governor to enforce justice.
    • 垂帷裳以自掩塞乎 - Rhetorical question emphasizing the absurdity of hiding behind a curtain.
    • 振悚耳目 - Means 'to shock and startle the senses,' describing the impact of the governor's words on the people.
    • 過禮 - Means 'to exceed the prescribed rituals,' indicating that the official observed more than the required mourning customs.
    • 宵分 - Refers to the time when night falls, often used to describe the end of the day.
    • 驚唳 - Means 'to cry out in alarm,' describing the hens' reaction to the official's grief.
    • 伍員吹蕭行乞 - Refers to the legendary story of Wu Zixu, who played the flute while begging, symbolizing his exile and hardship.
    • 庖丁解牛 - Refers to the story of a butcher named Ding who mastered the art of butchering, symbolizing the perfection of skill through practice.
    • 投刃皆虛 - Means 'to throw the knife and leave no trace,' describing the butcher's effortless technique.
    • 初發硎 - Means 'as if freshly sharpened,' emphasizing the sharpness and readiness of the knife.
    • 游刃必有余地 - Means 'there is always room for the knife to move,' describing the ease and precision of the butcher's skill.
    • 郢人以垩漫其鼻 - Refers to a story where a painter named Cai uses a thin layer of plaster to cover a man's nose, symbolizing the precision of his art.
    • 薄如蝇翼 - Means 'as thin as a fly's wing,' emphasizing the delicacy of the plaster layer.
    • 运斤成风 - Means 'to wield the axe with the speed of the wind,' describing the painter's swift and precise technique.
    • 尽去其垩而不伤鼻 - Means 'to remove all the plaster without harming the nose,' highlighting the painter's skill and precision.
    • 天子头是汝出血处 - Rhetorical statement emphasizing the gravity of the situation and the potential consequences of the doctor's actions.
    • 若因血获差 - Means 'if it is because of the blood that he gets better,' expressing a conditional hope.
    • 扑之 - Means 'to beat or strike,' indicating the punishment for the doctor.
    • 天后自抱绘帛以赠医工 - Means 'Empress Wu herself wrapped a piece of silk to give to the doctor,' showing her appreciation for the doctor's skill.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 贾琮 - Governor during the Eastern Han Dynasty known for his integrity and efficiency.
    • 冀州 - One of the administrative regions during the Han Dynasty.
    • 刺史 - An official responsible for overseeing local administration and maintaining order.
    • 吴隐之 - Governor during the Eastern Jin Dynasty known for his integrity and frugality.
    • 广州 - A province in southern China.
    • 贪泉 - A legendary spring in Guangzhou, said to make anyone who drinks from it greedy.
    • 伍员 - Also known as Wu Zixu, a famous general and statesman from the Spring and Autumn period.
    • 庖丁 - A butcher from the Warring States period, famous for his skill in butchering.
    • 郢人 - A painter from the Warring States period, known for his precision.
    • 垩 - Plaster or lime used in painting.
    • 天后 - Empress Wu Zetian, the only female emperor in Chinese history.
    • 天子 - The emperor, the supreme ruler of the empire.

    Proper Nouns

    • 贾琮 - The governor of Ji province.
    • 冀州 - The province governed by Dr. Cao.
    • 吴隐之 - The governor of Guangzhou.
    • 广州 - The province governed by Wu Yin.
    • 伍员 - Also known as Wu Zixu, a famous general and statesman.
    • 庖丁 - A famous butcher.
    • 郢人 - A famous painter.
    • 垩 - Plaster or lime used in painting.
    • 天后 - Empress Wu Zetian.
    • 天子 - The emperor.

    During the Eastern Han Dynasty, Jia Cong served as the governor of Ji Province. His official carriage was adorned with a red curtain, symbolizing his high status. Upon ascending the carriage, Jia Cong lifted the curtain and declared, "As a governor, I should see far and hear widely, investigate and correct good and evil. Why would I hide behind a curtain?" The people heard this and were both shocked and awed.

    In the Eastern Jin Dynasty, Wu Yin served as the governor of Guangzhou. There was a legendary spring called the Greedy Spring, and anyone who drank from it would become greedy. Wu Yin drank from it and composed a poem: "Ancient people said of this water, a single sip would make one covet a thousand gold. If Yi and Qi were to drink, their hearts would remain unchanged." Additionally, during his mother's mourning period, Wu Yin observed more than the prescribed rituals. Despite his poverty, he could not afford to observe the mourning customs properly. At night, he would often hear two cranes cry out in alarm at midnight, and Wu Yin would rise and cry, never missing the time.

    Wu Zixu, a famous general, played the flute while begging and ate in the marketplace.

    A butcher named Ding was skilled in butchering cattle. He would throw the knife and leave no trace of effort. Ding said, "When I first started butchering, I saw nothing but a whole cow. After three years, I no longer saw the whole cow. By now, after nineteen years, the knife is as sharp as if it had just been sharpened, and there is always room for the knife to move."

    A painter named Cai covered a man's nose with a thin layer of plaster, as thin as a fly's wing, and asked a carpenter named Shi to carve it away. Shi wielded his axe with the speed of the wind, removing all the plaster without harming the nose.

    Emperor Gaozong of Tang once suffered from headaches and had closed eyes and a confused mind. He summoned a physician. The physician said, "You should prick your forehead with a needle to draw blood, and it will heal." Empress Wu Zetian became angry and said, "The emperor's head is where you will draw blood." She ordered the physician to be beaten. The emperor said, "If it is because of the blood that he gets better, that would be fortunate." The physician then pricked the emperor's forehead, and blood splattered his ceremonial robe. The emperor's eyes then opened, and he was completely healed. Empress Wu Zetian herself wrapped a piece of silk to give to the physician as a gift. [21]

    阮籍放旷，有兵家女极有容色，未嫁而死。籍不识其父兄，遂往哭之，尽哀而返。
    　　唐太宗皇帝虬须，可以挂弓。
    　　唐韩干善画马，闲居之际，忽有一人，玄冠朱衣而至。干问曰：“何缘及此”对曰：“我鬼使也。闻君善画良马，愿赐一匹。”干立画焚之。数日因出，有人揖而谢曰：“蒙君惠骏足，免为山水跋涉之苦，亦有以酬效。”明日，有人送素缣百匹，不知其来，干收而用之。
    　　谢灵运临刑，剪其须施广州佛寺。须长三尺，今存焉。
    　　《博物志》曰：太公为灌坛令，文王梦一人哭于当道，问其故，乃曰：“吾泰山神女，嫁为西海妇。吾行必以暴风雨，灌坛当吾道，不敢以疾风暴雨也。”梦觉，召太公。三日，果疾风暴雨过境。
    　　晋桓豁镇荆州，有一参军，五月五日采鸲鹆雏，剪其舌，令学人语，经年遂能言。后因大会，豁出之，令遍学座客话。有一人患＜邕鼻＞鼻，鸲乃遽飞入瓮中，语与患者无异。举席皆笑。 [22]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [22]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 阮籍放旷 - Describes Ruan Ji's carefree and unrestrained lifestyle.
    • 有兵家女极有容色 - Refers to a beautiful military officer's daughter.
    • 籍不识其父兄，遂往哭之，尽哀而返 - Ruan Ji expressed deep sorrow and returned, despite not knowing the family.
    • 唐太宗皇帝虬须 - Describes Emperor Taizong's curly beard.
    • 可以挂弓 - Idiomatic expression meaning the beard was strong enough to hang a bow.
    • 唐韩干善画马 - Describes Han Gan's skill in painting horses.
    • 闲居之际，忽有一人，玄冠朱衣而至 - Describes the appearance of a person in black cap and red robe.
    • 干问曰：‘何缘及此’对曰：‘吾鬼使也’ - Dialogue clarifying the identity of the person who appeared.
    • 闻君善画良马，愿赐一匹 - The ghost's request for a horse painting.
    • 干立画焚之 - Han Gan's immediate action of drawing and burning the painting.
    • 数日因出，有人揖而谢曰 - The ghost's gesture of gratitude.
    • 蒙君惠骏足，免为山水跋涉之苦，亦有以酬效 - The ghost's gratitude and the gift of white silk.
    • 明日，有人送素缣百匹，不知其来 - Describes the mysterious origin of the gift.
    • 谢灵运临刑，剪其须施广州佛寺 - Xie Lingyun's final act before execution.
    • 须长三尺，今存焉 - Details about the length of Xie Lingyun's beard and its current status.
    • 《博物志》曰 - Introduces the source of the following story.
    • 太公为灌坛令，文王梦一人哭于当道 - Sets up the story of King Wen's dream.
    • 吾泰山神女，嫁为西海妇 - Explains the identity of the goddess in the dream.
    • 吾行必以暴风雨，灌坛当吾道，不敢以疾风暴雨也 - The goddess's explanation of her travel conditions.
    • 梦觉，召太公 - Describes King Wen's action after awakening from the dream.
    • 三日，果疾风暴雨过境 - Describes the fulfillment of the dream.
    • 晋桓豁镇荆州 - Introduces Huan Huo's governance of Jingzhou.
    • 有一参军，五月五日采鸲鹆雏，剪其舌，令学人语 - Describes the training process of the magpie.
    • 经年遂能言 - The magpie's progress after a year.
    • 后因大会，豁出之，令遍学座客话 - The magpie's performance at a grand gathering.
    • 有一人患＜邕鼻＞鼻 - Refers to a specific condition or ailment.
    • 鸲乃遽飞入瓮中，语与患者无异 - The magpie's imitation of the person with the ailment.
    • 举席皆笑 - Describes the reaction to the magpie's performance.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 阮籍 - A famous poet and philosopher of the Wei-Jin period, known for his carefree and unrestrained lifestyle.
    • 唐太宗皇帝 - Emperor Taizong of Tang, one of the most famous emperors in Chinese history, known for his military prowess and cultural achievements.
    • 韩干 - A renowned painter of the Tang dynasty, known for his skill in painting horses.
    • 谢灵运 - A famous poet of the Southern Song dynasty, known for his contributions to landscape poetry.
    • 《博物志》 - A classical text compiled by Zhang Hua, containing various records of natural phenomena, historical events, and folklore.
    • 太公 - Jiang Ziya, a legendary figure in Chinese history, known as the founder of the Zhou dynasty.
    • 灌坛 - A place name, possibly referring to a specific location or official position.
    • 文王 - King Wen of Zhou, a legendary figure in Chinese history, known for his wisdom and virtue.
    • 泰山神女 - A goddess associated with Mount Tai, one of the most sacred mountains in Chinese culture.
    • 西海 - Refers to the Western Sea, a mythical or distant place in classical Chinese literature.
    • 晋桓豁 - Huan Huo, a military general of the Eastern Jin dynasty, known for his governance of Jingzhou.
    • 鸲鹆 - A type of bird, specifically a magpie, often used in classical literature for its ability to mimic human speech.

    Ruan Ji was known for his carefree and unrestrained nature. There was a daughter of a military officer who was exceptionally beautiful, but she died unmarried before her wedding. Ruan Ji did not know her father or brothers, yet he went to mourn her, expressing his deepest sorrow before returning.

    Emperor Taizong of Tang had a curly beard that was strong enough to hang a bow.

    Han Gan of the Tang dynasty was skilled in painting horses. During his leisure time, a person suddenly appeared wearing a black cap and red robe. Han Gan asked, "Why have you come here?" The person replied, "I am a messenger of the underworld. I have heard of your skill in painting fine horses and wish to receive one." Han Gan immediately drew a horse and burned it. A few days later, as he was about to leave, someone bowed and thanked him, saying, "I received your gift of a fine horse, which saved me from the hardship of crossing mountains and rivers. I also have something to repay you." The next day, someone sent him a hundred pieces of white silk, whose origin was unknown, and Han Gan accepted and used them.

    Before his execution, Xie Lingyun cut off his beard and donated it to a Buddhist temple in Guangzhou. His beard was three feet long and still exists today.

    According to Records of Marvelous Things, Jiang Ziya served as the magistrate of Guantan. King Wen of Zhou had a dream in which a person wept in the middle of the road. He asked the reason, and the person replied, "I am a goddess of Mount Tai, married to a woman of the Western Sea. I must travel with violent storms, and Guantan is on my path, so I dare not use strong winds and heavy rains." King Wen awoke from the dream and summoned Jiang Ziya. Three days later, strong winds and heavy rains indeed passed through the region.

    Huan Huo of the Eastern Jin dynasty governed Jingzhou. One of his military officers, on the fifth day of the fifth month, captured a young magpie, cut its tongue, and trained it to mimic human speech. After a year, the magpie could speak. Later, during a grand gathering, Huan Huo brought it out and made it learn the conversation of all the guests. One person had a condition affecting their nose, and the magpie quickly flew into a jar and spoke just like the person with the ailment. Everyone at the gathering laughed. [22]

    玄宗朝宰相卢怀慎无疾暴终，夫人崔氏止儿女，不令号哭，曰：“公命未终，我得知之。”语曰：“公清俭而廉洁，蹇进而谦退，四方赂遗毫发不留。与张燕公同时为相，张纳货山积，其人尚在，奢俭之报岂虚也哉”及宵分，公复生，左右以夫人之言启陈，公曰：“理固不同，冥司有三十炉，日夕鼓橐，为说铸横财，我无一焉，恶可匹哉”言讫复绝。
    　　唐裴晋公度寝疾永乐里，暮春之月，忽遇游南园，令家仆僮舁至药栏，语曰：“我不见此花而死，可悲也。”怅然而返。明早，报牡丹一丛先发，公视之，三日乃薨。
    　　晋孟嘉，少知名。庾亮大会州府人士，嘉坐甚远。亮问江州刺史曰：“闻有孟嘉，其人何在”守曰：“在坐，君自认之。”俄然，指曰：“彼君少异于众，非嘉乎”曰：“然。”亮大笑，喜得嘉。 [23]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [23]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 無疾暴終 - Means 'died suddenly without illness,' indicating an unexpected death.
    • 公命未终，我得知之 - A superstitious belief that the wife could foresee the husband's fate.
    • 四方赂遗毫发不留 - Means 'not a single hair or feather was accepted from bribes,' emphasizing the man's integrity.
    • 理固不同，冥司有三十炉，日夕鼓橐，为说铸横财 - Describes the underworld's process of casting ill-gotten wealth, with '三十炉' meaning 'thirty furnaces' and '鼓橐' referring to the bellows used in smelting.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 玄宗朝宰相卢怀慎 - Refers to Lu Huishen, a chancellor during the reign of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, known for his integrity and frugality.
    • 张燕公 - Refers to Zhang Yan, another chancellor during the same period, known for his extravagance and acceptance of bribes.
    • 冥司 - Refers to the underworld or hell, a common concept in Chinese folk religion and Buddhism.
    • 裴晋公度 - Refers to Pei Jigong Du, a prominent statesman and poet of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 牡丹 - Refers to peonies, a symbol of wealth and honor in Chinese culture.
    • 晋孟嘉 - Refers to Jin Sheng Jia, a famous scholar and official of the Jin Dynasty, known for his wit and integrity.
    • 庾亮 - Refers to Yu Liang, a high-ranking official and military leader of the Eastern Jin Dynasty, known for his political acumen and literary talent.

    Proper Nouns

    • 玄宗 - Emperor Xuanzong of Tang.
    • 卢怀慎 - Lu Huishen, a chancellor during the reign of Emperor Xuanzong.
    • 崔氏 - Cui Shi, the wife of Lu Huishen.
    • 张燕公 - Zhang Yan, another chancellor during the same period.
    • 裴晋公度 - Pei Jigong Du, a prominent statesman and poet of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 牡丹 - Peonies, a symbol of wealth and honor in Chinese culture.
    • 晋孟嘉 - Jin Sheng Jia, a famous scholar and official of the Jin Dynasty.
    • 庾亮 - Yu Liang, a high-ranking official and military leader of the Eastern Jin Dynasty.

    During the reign of Emperor Xuanzong, the chancellor Lu Huishen died suddenly without illness. His wife Cui Shi instructed the children not to cry, saying, "Your father's fate has not yet ended, and I know it." She continued, "He was frugal and incorruptible, humble in advancement and modest in retreat, and not a single hair or feather was accepted from bribes. At the same time as Zhang Yan, he was chancellor, but Zhang Yan accepted bribes in mountains, and he is still alive. How could the rewards of frugality and extravagance be empty?" By midnight, Lu Huishen revived. Those around him reported his wife's words to him, and he said, "The principles are indeed different. In the underworld, there are thirty furnaces, and they are constantly stirring the bellows to cast ill-gotten wealth. I have none of that, so how can I match them?" After speaking, he died again.

    In the Tang Dynasty, Pei Jigong Du was ill in Yongle Lane during the late spring month. Suddenly, he went to visit the Southern Garden and ordered his servants to carry him to the medicinal fence. He said, "I cannot die without seeing this flower, which is sad." He returned home in disappointment. The next morning, it was reported that a cluster of peonies had bloomed early. Pei Jigong Du saw them and died three days later.

    Jin Sheng Jia was known since his youth. Yu Liang held a grand gathering of officials from the state and its prefectures. Jia was seated far away. Yu Liang asked the governor of Jiangzhou, "I have heard of Sheng Jia. Where is he?" The official replied, "He is here; you can recognize him yourself." Soon, he pointed out, "That man is different from the rest; isn't he Jia?" Yu Liang said, "Yes," and laughed, delighted to have found Jia. [23]

    王敦为驸马，如厕，左右侍者甚众，敦乃脱衣裸体而登厕，无羞愧色。有一侍女曰：“此人必能作贼。”其后果为乱也。
    　　宋武帝未杀殷仲文之时，仲文每照镜，常不见其首，后数日，果为武帝所杀。
    　　汉宣帝时，有人于疏属山石盖下得二人，俱被桎梏，将至长安，乃变为石。宣帝集群臣问之，无一知者，刘向对曰：“此是黄帝时｛宀契｝窳国负贰之臣，犯罪大逆，黄帝不忍诛，流之疏属山，若有明君，当得出外。”帝不信，谓其妖言，收向系狱。其子歆自出，应募以救其父，曰：“须七岁女子以乳之，即复变。”帝使女子乳，于是复为人，便能言语，应对如刘向之言。帝大悦，拜向大中大夫，歆为宗正卿。诏曰：“何以知之”歆曰：“出《山海经》。”
    　　汉东方朔，岁星精也。自入仕汉武帝，天上岁星不见。至其死后，星乃出。 [24]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [24]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 為驸马 - Literally means 'to be a son-in-law of the emperor,' referring to marrying into the imperial family.
    • 如厕 - Literally means 'to go to the toilet,' but in classical Chinese, it often implies a private or intimate setting.
    • 无羞愧色 - Means 'without any signs of shame or embarrassment.'
    • 此人必能作贼 - A statement implying that someone who behaves in such a way must be capable of committing crimes.
    • 后果为乱也 - Means 'he eventually caused chaos.'
    • 岁星精也 - Refers to someone being a manifestation of Jupiter, often associated with good fortune or divine favor.
    • 天上岁星不见 - Means 'the star of Jupiter was not visible in the sky.'
    • 星乃出 - Means 'the star then appeared.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 王敦 - A historical figure, a general during the Eastern Jin Dynasty known for his rebellious actions.
    • 宋武帝 - Emperor Wu of Song, a ruler of the Southern Dynasties period.
    • 殷仲文 - A historical figure, a scholar and official during the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 汉宣帝 - Emperor Xuan of Han, a ruler of the Western Han Dynasty.
    • 疏属山 - A mountain range in ancient China, often associated with mysterious or supernatural events.
    • 刘向 - A famous historian, literary critic, and scholar during the Han Dynasty.
    • 刘歆 - The son of Liu Xiang, also a notable scholar and historian.
    • 《山海经》 - A classic Chinese text that describes various mythical creatures, places, and phenomena.
    • 汉东方朔 - A famous scholar and entertainer during the Han Dynasty, known for his wit and wisdom.

    Proper Nouns

    • 王敦 - A historical figure.
    • 宋武帝 - Emperor Wu of Song.
    • 殷仲文 - A historical figure.
    • 汉宣帝 - Emperor Xuan of Han.
    • 疏属山 - A mountain range.
    • 刘向 - A famous historian and scholar.
    • 刘歆 - The son of Liu Xiang.
    • 《山海经》 - A classic Chinese text.
    • 汉东方朔 - A famous scholar and entertainer.
    • 汉武帝 - Emperor Wu of Han.

    Wang Dun, being a son-in-law of the emperor, went to the toilet, where there were many attendants around him. Wang Dun then removed his clothes and climbed onto the toilet naked, showing no signs of shame or embarrassment. One of the maidservants said, "This person must be capable of committing crimes." Indeed, he later caused chaos.

    During the reign of Emperor Wu of Song, before he killed Yin Zhongwen, Yin would often look into the mirror and never see his head. After a few days, he was indeed killed by Emperor Wu.

    During the reign of Emperor Xuan of Han, there were two people found under a stone cover in the Shuzhu Mountain, both bound with shackles. They were taken to Chang'an and turned into stone. Emperor Xuan gathered his ministers to ask about this, but none of them knew. Liu Xiang replied, "These are officials from the ancient state of Xiuyu who committed a great crime against the emperor. The Yellow Emperor did not wish to punish them and exiled them to the Shuzhu Mountain. If there were a wise ruler, they would be released." The emperor did not believe him and accused him of speaking nonsense, putting Liu Xiang in prison. Liu Xiang's son, Liu Xin, volunteered to go out and offered to save his father by saying, "We need a seven-year-old girl to nurse them, and they will turn back into people." The emperor had a girl nurse them, and they indeed turned back into people, able to speak and answer questions just like Liu Xiang had said. The emperor was greatly pleased and appointed Liu Xiang as a Grand Doctor and Liu Xin as the Minister of the Imperial Clan. He asked, "How do you know this?" Liu Xin replied, "It is written in the Classic of Mountains and Seas."

    Han Fangshuo was a manifestation of Jupiter. Since he entered the service of Emperor Wu of Han, the star of Jupiter was not visible in the sky. After his death, the star then appeared. [24]

    东汉王霸，善抚士卒，每有人死者，脱衣殓之。
    　　刘琨，字越石。尝为胡骑所围，救兵不至，城中窘迫无计。琨乃登楼清啸，贼闻之凄然。日中，又奏胡笳。贼皆流涕怀土。至晚复吹之，贼舍围而去。
    　　宋景公时，荧惑守心，子韦吊曰：“祸当君，可移于相。”公曰：“相，所理国家，不可也。”可移于百姓。”公曰：“百姓，国之本，不可也。”“可移于岁。”公曰：“岁，民之本，不可也。”子韦曰：“君有至德之言三，天必赏君。”于是荧惑退三舍。
    　　唐贞元中，李师古暇日常宴其从事，适有日者预坐，师古遣遍视幕客皇甫弼、贾直言之徒，凡十辈。答曰：“十日之内，俱有重祸。”又指一从事王生者，曰：“此先忌马厄。”时有从事姓魏者，师古之妻党，移第凿池，积土其傍，上构高亭，极为弘敞，既成，即迎入舍，乐之，饮酣，亭忽摧塌，以其下土弱，不胜其任。坐客皆折手足，不至于死。王生因为角马木长钉横贯其脑，立死。 [25]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [25]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 善抚士卒 - To be good at comforting soldiers, where '善' means 'good at' or 'skilled in,' and '抚' means 'to comfort' or 'to console.'
    • 脱衣殓之 - To take off one's clothes to dress the deceased, where '脱衣' means 'to take off one's clothes,' and '殓' means 'to dress the deceased.'
    • 尝为胡骑所围 - Once surrounded by barbarian cavalry, where '尝' means 'once,' '为...所' is a passive construction meaning 'to be... by,' and '胡骑' refers to 'barbarian cavalry.'
    • 登楼清啸 - To climb a tower and emit a clear whistle, where '清啸' means 'to emit a clear whistle.'
    • 凄然 - Sadly or with sadness.
    • 日中 - At noon.
    • 奏胡笳 - To play the barbarian flute, where '胡笳' refers to 'the barbarian flute.'
    • 流涕怀土 - To shed tears and think of one's homeland, where '流涕' means 'to shed tears,' and '怀土' means 'to think of one's homeland.'
    • 至晚复吹之 - Until late evening, to play it again, where '至晚' means 'until late evening,' and '复吹之' means 'to play it again.'
    • 舍围而去 - To abandon the encirclement and leave, where '舍' means 'to abandon,' and '去' means 'to leave.'
    • 荧惑守心 - An astronomical phenomenon where Mars (荧惑) is said to be 'guarding' the heart star (心), which is considered an omen of disaster.
    • 至德之言三 - Three words of great virtue, where '至德' means 'great virtue,' and '三' means 'three.'
    • 岁 - Refers to 'the year' or 'the agricultural cycle.'
    • 岁，民之本 - The year, the foundation of the people, where '民之本' means 'the foundation of the people.'
    • 可移于相 - Can be transferred to the chancellor, where '相' refers to 'the chancellor.'
    • 可移于百姓 - Can be transferred to the common people, where '百姓' refers to 'the common people.'
    • 可移于岁 - Can be transferred to the year, where '岁' refers to 'the year.'
    • 贞元中 - During the Zhengyuan era, where '贞元' is the era name of Emperor Dezong of Tang.
    • 李师古 - Refers to Li Shigu, a Tang dynasty military governor.
    • 暇日 - Leisure time.
    • 从事 - Refers to 'officials' or 'assistants.'
    • 日者 - Refers to 'astrologers' or 'fortune-tellers.'
    • 预坐 - To sit among the guests, where '预' means 'to participate' or 'to sit among.'
    • 十日之内 - Within ten days.
    • 俱有重祸 - All will suffer a great misfortune, where '俱' means 'all,' and '重祸' means 'great misfortune.'
    • 先忌马厄 - Firstly, beware of horse-related disasters, where '先忌' means 'firstly, beware of,' and '马厄' means 'horse-related disasters.'
    • 妻党 - Refers to 'in-laws' or 'relatives through marriage.'
    • 移第凿池 - To move to a new residence and dig a pond, where '移第' means 'to move to a new residence,' and '凿池' means 'to dig a pond.'
    • 积土其傍 - To pile up earth on the side, where '积土' means 'to pile up earth,' and '其傍' means 'on the side.'
    • 上构高亭 - To build a high pavilion on top, where '上构' means 'to build on top,' and '高亭' means 'a high pavilion.'
    • 极为弘敞 - Extremely spacious and grand, where '极为' means 'extremely,' and '弘敞' means 'spacious and grand.'
    • 既成 - Once completed.
    • 即迎入舍 - Immediately invited to live there, where '即' means 'immediately,' and '迎入舍' means 'invited to live there.'
    • 乐之 - To enjoy it.
    • 亭忽摧塌 - The pavilion suddenly collapsed, where '忽' means 'suddenly,' and '摧塌' means 'collapsed.'
    • 土弱 - Weak soil.
    • 不胜其任 - Unable to bear the weight, where '不胜' means 'unable to bear,' and '其任' means 'the weight.'
    • 坐客皆折手足 - All the guests sitting there broke their hands and feet, where '坐客' means 'guests sitting there,' and '折手足' means 'broke their hands and feet.'
    • 不至于死 - But did not die, where '不至于' means 'but did not,' and '死' means 'die.'
    • 角马木长钉横贯其脑 - A long nail from a horse's harness pierced through his brain, where '角马木' means 'a long nail from a horse's harness,' and '横贯其脑' means 'pierced through his brain.'
    • 立死 - Died immediately, where '立' means 'immediately,' and '死' means 'died.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 东汉王霸 - Refers to Wang Ba, a general during the Eastern Han dynasty known for his kindness towards soldiers.
    • 刘琨 - Refers to Liu Kun, a famous general and poet during the Western Jin dynasty.
    • 胡骑 - Refers to the cavalry of the Xiongnu, a nomadic people who frequently invaded China.
    • 荧惑守心 - Refers to an astronomical phenomenon in Chinese astrology, where Mars (荧惑) is said to be 'guarding' the heart star (心), which is considered an omen of disaster.
    • 子韦 - Refers to Zi Wei, a court astrologer during the Spring and Autumn period.
    • 宋景公 - Refers to Duke Jing of Song, a ruler during the Spring and Autumn period.
    • 唐贞元中 - Refers to the Zhengyuan era (785-805) of the Tang dynasty.
    • 李师古 - Refers to Li Shigu, a military governor during the Tang dynasty.
    • 从事 - Refers to officials or assistants in the Tang dynasty bureaucracy.
    • 日者 - Refers to astrologers or fortune-tellers in ancient China.
    • 魏生 - Refers to a specific individual, likely a guest at Li Shigu's gathering.

    During the Eastern Han dynasty, Wang Ba was renowned for his kindness towards his soldiers. Whenever one of them died, he would take off his own clothes to dress the deceased.

    Liu Kun, whose courtesy name was Yueshi, was once surrounded by barbarian cavalry. With no relief army arriving, the city was in great distress. Liu Kun then climbed a tower and emitted a clear whistle. The enemy soldiers heard it and felt sad. At noon, he played the barbarian flute again. The enemy soldiers all shed tears and thought of their homeland. Until late evening, he played it again, and the enemy abandoned the encirclement and left.

    During the reign of Duke Jing of Song, Mars (荧惑) was said to be "guarding" the heart star (心), which was considered an omen of disaster. Zi Wei, the court astrologer, lamented, "The calamity should befall the ruler, but it can be transferred to the chancellor." The duke replied, "The chancellor is responsible for governing the state, so that is not possible." Zi Wei then suggested, "It can be transferred to the common people." The duke said, "The common people are the foundation of the state, so that is not possible." Finally, Zi Wei proposed, "It can be transferred to the year." The duke replied, "The year is the foundation of the people, so that is not possible." Zi Wei then said, "The ruler has spoken three words of great virtue, and the heavens will surely reward him." As a result, Mars retreated three degrees.

    During the Zhengyuan era of the Tang dynasty, Li Shigu, a military governor, often held banquets for his officials during his leisure time. One day, an astrologer was invited to sit among the guests. Li Shigu had him examine his officials, including Huangfu Bi and Jia Zhiyan, among ten others. The astrologer replied, "Within ten days, all of you will suffer a great misfortune." He then pointed to a certain official named Wang, saying, "This person should first beware of horse-related disasters." At that time, there was an official named Wei, who was related to Li Shigu through marriage. Wei had moved to a new residence and dug a pond, piling up earth on the side. On top of this, he built a high and spacious pavilion. Once completed, he immediately invited Wei to live there, and they enjoyed it. During a drinking session, the pavilion suddenly collapsed because the soil beneath it was weak and unable to bear the weight. All the guests sitting there broke their hands and feet, but did not die. Wang, however, was struck by a long nail from a horse's harness, which pierced through his brain, and he died immediately. [25]

    楚昭王与吴战，败走四十步，忽遗其履，取之。左右曰：“楚国虽贫，而无一履哉”王曰：“吾悲与其俱出而不得与其俱返。”于是国无相弃者。
    　　隋杨素家富侈之极，家僮数千人，后庭曳罗绮之女亦数千。都会之处，邸店碾，不知纪极。性贪营利，心无厌足时，议鄙之。
    　　李善，本李元家苍头也。建武中，元家遭疾疠，子孙尽死，唯一子续生数旬。群奴欲杀之，分其财。善窃之，将入瑕丘山中。至七岁，出理于官。钟离意为令，杀群奴，表善之忠孝。善后拜郡守。
    　　魏太祖军中粮乏，令主仓吏用小斗。后军众有言，太祖归罪主吏，谓曰：“借汝死，令压众谤。”词遂息焉。
    　　秦惠王伐蜀，乃刻五石牛，置金于后。曰：“此天牛，能粪金，以遗王。”王以为然，即发五丁力士拖成道，秦使张仪随其后开蜀。
    　　汉于公门坏，大治之，教曰：“稍高其门，可容车马。我治狱多阴德，后世必昌。”子定国果为丞相。 [26]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [26]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 楚國雖貧，而無一履哉 - Rhetorical question emphasizing the triviality of the lost shoe compared to the grander concerns of state.
    • 吾悲與其俱出而不得與其俱返 - Poignant statement expressing regret for the loss of a companion, even if it is just a shoe.
    • 隋楊素家富侈之極 - Hyperbolic description of Yang Su’s extreme wealth and luxury.
    • 都會之處，邸店碾硙，不知紀極 - Description of Yang Su’s extensive property holdings, with the phrase '不知紀極' indicating boundless wealth.
    • 性貪營利，心無厭足時 - Characterization of Yang Su’s greed and insatiable desire for profit.
    • 稍高其門，可容車馬 - Yu Gong’s reasoning for raising the door, emphasizing the need for future expansion.
    • 我治獄多陰德，後世必昌 - Yu Gong’s belief that his past actions would bring future prosperity.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 楚昭王與吳戰 - Refers to the historical conflict between the states of Chu and Wu during the Spring and Autumn period.
    • 隋楊素 - Refers to Yang Su, a prominent official during the Sui dynasty, known for his wealth and luxurious lifestyle.
    • 李善 - Refers to Li Shan, a historical figure known for his loyalty and filial piety.
    • 魏太祖 - Refers to Cao Cao, the founder of the Wei dynasty during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 秦惠王 - Refers to the King of Qin who employed cunning strategies to conquer Shu.
    • 漢於公 - Refers to Yu Gong, a historical figure known for his wisdom and foresight.

    Proper Nouns

    • 楚昭王 - King of Chu during the Spring and Autumn period.
    • 隋楊素 - A prominent official during the Sui dynasty.
    • 李善 - A historical figure known for his loyalty and filial piety.
    • 魏太祖 - Cao Cao, the founder of the Wei dynasty.
    • 秦惠王 - The King of Qin who conquered Shu.
    • 漢於公 - Yu Gong, a historical figure known for his wisdom.

    King Chu of Chu fought against Wu and, after retreating forty steps, suddenly lost his shoe and went back to retrieve it. His attendants said, "Although Chu is poor, it is not as if it lacks even one shoe!" The king replied, "I lament that I could not return with it, having taken it out with me." Thus, the state of Chu had no instances of people abandoning each other.

    Yang Su of the Sui dynasty was extremely wealthy and luxurious. He had thousands of servants and thousands of women in his rear courtyard dressed in fine silk. In major cities, he owned countless shops and mills, to the point where his wealth was boundless. He was greedy for profit, never satisfied, and was widely criticized for it.

    Li Shan, originally a servant in the household of Li Yuan, found himself in a dire situation during the Jianwu period when Li's family suffered from a plague, killing all their descendants except for one infant who survived for several weeks. The group of servants wanted to kill the infant to divide the family's wealth. Li Shan secretly took the child and fled to the Xueqiu mountains. When the child reached the age of seven, he was discovered and taken care of by officials. Zhongli Yi, the magistrate, punished the servants and commended Li Shan for his loyalty and filial piety. Later, Li Shan was appointed as a prefect.

    Cao Cao of Wei faced a shortage of food in his army and ordered the granary manager to use smaller measures to distribute grain. Later, when the soldiers complained, Cao Cao blamed the granary manager and said, "I will execute you to silence the many critics." The complaints then ceased.

    King Hui of Qin attacked Shu by carving five stone oxen and placing gold behind them, claiming they were divine cattle that could produce gold to be given to the king. The Shu king believed this and sent five thousand laborers to drag a path for the oxen. Qin then sent Zhang Yi to follow and conquer Shu.

    Yu Gong of Han rebuilt his house, raising the door higher so that it could accommodate carriages and horses. He said, "I have done many good deeds in secret, and my descendants will surely prosper." His son Dingguo indeed became a chancellor. [26]

    殷仲堪节俭，为荆州刺史。每食，饣卞落席，自拾食之。
    　　伊尹耻其君不及尧舜，若挞之于市。
    　　禹伤其父功不成，乃南逃衡山，斩马以祭之，仰天而啸。忽梦神人，自称玄夷苍水使者，谓禹曰：“欲得我书者，斋焉。”禹遂斋三日。乃降金简玉字之书，得治水之要。
    　　孙坚，字文台。战而堕马，军吏失之。所爱骏马，入营踣地悲鸣，人异之，逐马往，得坚于草中。
    　　王粲尝谒蔡邕，倒屣迎之，满座皆惊，曰：“王孙久有异才，吾家书籍悉以赠之。”
    　　越王勾践为吴所败于会稽，将以勉励于众。尝施一器，悬胆于门，出入尝之，令士卒不忘其苦。
    　　隋贺若弼父敦，临死之日，令弼吐其舌，以锥刺之，流血及地，戒曰：“吾以舌败，汝可戒言。”及死，弼亦以言议伤炀帝，为炀帝所杀。
    　　宋刘裕贫贱时，尝盖布被，用牛尾作蝇拂子。及登极，亦不弃之，敕其女彭城公主谨收藏，以遗子孙。 [27]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [27]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 殷仲堪節儉 - Describes Yin Zhongkan's frugality, a key character trait.
    • 每食，饣卞落席，自拾食之 - A vivid description of Yin Zhongkan picking up his food from the floor during meals.
    • 若撻之於市 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'as if whipping him in the marketplace,' indicating public disgrace.
    • 禹傷其父功不成，乃南逃衡山，斬馬以祭之，仰天而嘯 - Describes Yu's grief over his father's unfinished work and his subsequent retreat to Mount Heng, including the ritual of sacrificing a horse and shouting to heaven.
    • 忽夢神人，自稱玄夷蒼水使者 - Yu's dream of a divine figure who calls himself the 'Black Water Messenger.'
    • 欲得我書者，齋焉 - The divine figure's instruction to Yu to fast before receiving the divine book.
    • 乃降金簡玉字之書，得治水之要 - The divine revelation of the golden tablet with the key to flood control.
    • 孫堅，字文台 - Introduction of Sun Jian, a historical figure.
    • 戰而墮馬，軍吏失之 - Describes Sun Jian's fall from his horse and the loss of his whereabouts.
    • 所愛駿馬，入營踣地悲鳴 - The horse's dramatic reaction, indicating Sun Jian's location.
    • 人異之，逐馬往，得堅於草中 - The soldiers' reaction and their discovery of Sun Jian.
    • 王粲嘗謁蔡邕，倒屣迎之 - Describes Wang Can's visit to Cai Yong, who rushes to greet him by removing his shoes.
    • 滿座皆驚，曰：「王孫久有異才，吾家書籍悉以贈之」 - Cai Yong's admiration and his decision to give all his books to Wang Can.
    • 越王勾踐為吳所敗於會稽 - The historical event of King Goujian's defeat by the Wu state at the Battle of Kuaiji.
    • 將以勉勵於眾 - Goujian's intention to inspire his people.
    • 嘗施一器，懸膽於門 - Goujian's practice of suspending a gall bladder at his door.
    • 出入嘗之，令士卒不忘其苦 - The purpose of the gall bladder as a reminder of hardship.
    • 隋賀若弼父敦，臨死之日，令弼吐其舌，以錐刺之 - Describes Duan's final instruction to his son.
    • 流血及地，戒曰：「吾以舌敗，汝可戒言」 - Duan's warning about the dangers of excessive speech.
    • 及死，弼亦以言議傷煬帝，為煬帝所殺 - The tragic fate of Duan's son.
    • 宋劉裕貧賤時，嘗蓋布被 - Describes Liu Yu's humble origins.
    • 用牛尾作蠅拂子 - Liu Yu's resourcefulness in making a fly swatter.
    • 及登極，亦不棄之 - Liu Yu's retention of his humble possessions after becoming emperor.
    • 敕其女彭城公主謹收藏，以遺子孙 - Liu Yu's instructions to his daughter to preserve the fly swatter for future generations.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 殷仲堪 - A historical figure known for his frugality.
    • 伊尹 - A legendary minister of the Shang dynasty, known for his wisdom and service.
    • 堯舜 - The idealized rulers of ancient China, symbolizing virtue and governance.
    • 大禹 - The legendary founder of the Xia dynasty, known for his flood control efforts.
    • 孫堅 - A historical figure and founder of the Eastern Han dynasty.
    • 王粲 - A famous poet and scholar of the Han dynasty.
    • 蔡邕 - A renowned scholar and musician of the Han dynasty.
    • 越王勾踐 - The king of Yue who famously defeated the Wu state.
    • 隋賀若弼 - A historical figure from the Sui dynasty.
    • 劉裕 - The founder of the Liu Song dynasty, known for his humble origins.

    Proper Nouns

    • 殷仲堪 - A historical figure.
    • 伊尹 - A legendary minister.
    • 堯舜 - Idealized rulers.
    • 大禹 - The founder of the Xia dynasty.
    • 孫堅 - A historical figure.
    • 王粲 - A famous poet.
    • 蔡邕 - A renowned scholar.
    • 越王勾踐 - The king of Yue.
    • 隋賀若弼 - A historical figure.
    • 劉裕 - The founder of the Liu Song dynasty.
    • 彭城公主 - The daughter of Liu Yu.

    殷 Zhongkan was known for his frugality and served as the governor of Jingzhou. At every meal, crumbs would fall to the floor, and he would pick them up to eat himself.

    Yi Yin was ashamed that his ruler fell short of the ideals of Yao and Shun, as if he were being whipped in public.

    Yu lamented that his father's work remained unfinished and thus retreated to Mount Heng in the south. He sacrificed a horse in honor of his father, looked up to heaven, and let out a shout. Suddenly, he dreamed of a divine figure who introduced himself as the Black Water Messenger, telling Yu, "If you wish to receive my book, you must fast." Yu then fasted for three days. A golden tablet inscribed with jade characters descended, revealing the secrets of flood control.

    Sun Jian, known as Wentai, fell from his horse during battle, and his whereabouts were lost among the soldiers. His beloved steed entered the camp, fell to the ground, and mourned loudly. The soldiers were astonished and followed the horse, finding Sun Jian hidden in the grass.

    Wang Can once visited Cai Yong, who rushed to greet him by removing his shoes. Everyone in the room was amazed, and Cai Yong said, "Wang Sun has long possessed extraordinary talent; I will give all my books to him."

    King Goujian of Yue was defeated by the Wu state at Kuaiji but sought to inspire his people. He would place a gall bladder in a vessel and hang it at his door, tasting it upon entering or leaving, so that his soldiers would not forget their hardships.

    Duan, the father of Sui's He Ruo Bi, on his deathbed, instructed his son to spit out his tongue, which he then stabbed with a needle until blood flowed to the ground. He warned, "I was destroyed by my tongue; you must be cautious with your words." After his father's death, He Ruo Bi was also killed by Emperor Yang for his words.

    Liu Yu, when poor and lowly, would cover himself with a cotton quilt and make a fly swatter from a cow's tail. Even after becoming emperor, he did not discard these humble possessions. He instructed his daughter, Princess Pengcheng, to carefully preserve the fly swatter for future generations. [27]

    梁武帝酷好佛法，然性多含恕，敕天下贡献绫罗锦绮，不令织鸟兽之形，恐裁剪之时，有伤生物之意也。
    　　薛大鼎为沧州刺史，引海水，利鱼盐，邑人歌之。时瀛州刺史贾敦顺、冀州刺史郑德本俱有美政，河北称为“铛脚刺史”。
    　　楚王铸作三剑，晋、郑求之不得，兵围楚，三年不解。楚王登城，引太阿麾之，晋军血流，郑卒奔走。
    　　晋谢玄，字幼度，有才业，甚为从父安所重。少好佩紫罗香囊，安鄙之，而不欲伤其意，因戏赌得焚之。
    　　汉武帝自回中郡，绕一山曲，见一物盘地，状若牛，推之不去，击之不散。问左右，无能知者。东方朔进曰：“请以酒一斛浇之。”帝命酒浇之，立散。复问朔。曰：“此必秦之故狱，积其怨气所致，酒能消愁耳。”帝抚朔曰：“人之多知，有如此者。”
    　　宋明帝借张南菀三百年，敕云：“期毕便申。” [28]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [28]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 酷好佛法 - Means 'extremely fond of Buddhism,' indicating a deep and intense devotion.
    • 含恕 - Means 'benevolent' or 'forbearing,' suggesting a compassionate and tolerant nature.
    • 敕天下贡献绫罗锦绮 - Means 'imperially ordered contributions of fine silk fabrics,' referring to the emperor's command to collect luxurious textiles.
    • 织鸟兽之形 - Means 'to weave patterns of birds and beasts,' indicating the creation of animal motifs in fabric.
    • 裁剪之时，有伤生物之意也 - Means 'when cutting, it may cause harm to living beings,' suggesting a concern for the ethical implications of the process.
    • 引海水 - Means 'to divert seawater,' indicating the action of channeling or directing seawater.
    • 利鱼盐 - Means 'to benefit fish and salt production,' indicating the economic advantages derived from the diversion.
    • 邑人歌之 - Means 'the people of the town sang about it,' indicating the positive reception and celebration of the project.
    • 铛脚刺史 - A colloquial term for 'excellent officials,' suggesting that the officials were highly regarded and effective.
    • 铸作三剑 - Means 'to cast three swords,' indicating the creation of three swords.
    • 兵围楚，三年不解 - Means 'armies surrounded Chu for three years without resolving the conflict,' indicating a prolonged military siege.
    • 太阿麾之 - Means 'to wave the Ta'e sword,' indicating the use of a legendary sword in a dramatic gesture.
    • 晋军血流，郑卒奔走 - Means 'Jin troops bled, and Zheng soldiers fled,' indicating the devastating effects of the sword's power.
    • 佩紫罗香囊 - Means 'to wear a purple silk sachet,' indicating a fashionable accessory.
    • 鄙之 - Means 'to despise it,' indicating a negative reaction.
    • 戏赌得焚之 - Means 'to win it through a game and burn it,' indicating a playful and decisive action.
    • 回中郡 - Refers to a specific region, likely a historical place name.
    • 推之不去，击之不散 - Means 'cannot be pushed away, cannot be dispersed,' indicating the persistence of the phenomenon.
    • 无能知者 - Means 'no one could understand it,' indicating the mystery surrounding the object.
    • 请以酒一斛浇之 - Means 'please pour a measure of wine over it,' indicating a suggested solution.
    • 期毕便申 - Means 'upon the completion of the period, it will be returned,' indicating a temporary loan with a specified return date.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 梁武帝 - Refers to Emperor Wu of Liang, a significant figure in Chinese history known for his patronage of Buddhism.
    • 佛法 - Refers to Buddhist teachings and practices.
    • 绫罗锦绮 - Refers to fine silk fabrics, often associated with luxury and wealth.
    • 沧州 - Refers to a specific region, likely a historical place name.
    • 薛大鼎 - Refers to a historical figure, likely a governor or official.
    • 瀛州 - Refers to a specific region, likely a historical place name.
    • 贾敦顺 - Refers to a historical figure, likely a governor or official.
    • 冀州 - Refers to a specific region, likely a historical place name.
    • 郑德本 - Refers to a historical figure, likely a governor or official.
    • 楚王 - Refers to a historical figure, likely a king of the state of Chu.
    • 太阿 - Refers to a legendary sword, known for its power and significance.
    • 晋 - Refers to the state of Jin, a major power in ancient China.
    • 郑 - Refers to the state of Zheng, another major power in ancient China.
    • 宋明帝 - Refers to Emperor Ming of Song, a significant figure in Chinese history.
    • 张南菀 - Refers to a historical figure, likely a noble or official.
    • 汉武帝 - Refers to Emperor Wu of Han.
    • 回中郡 - Refers to a specific region, likely a historical place name.

    Proper Nouns

    • 梁武帝 - Emperor Wu of Liang.
    • 沧州 - A specific region.
    • 薛大鼎 - A historical figure.
    • 瀛州 - A specific region.
    • 贾敦顺 - A historical figure.
    • 冀州 - A specific region.
    • 郑德本 - A historical figure.
    • 楚王 - A historical figure.
    • 太阿 - A legendary sword.
    • 晋 - The state of Jin.
    • 郑 - The state of Zheng.
    • 宋明帝 - Emperor Ming of Song.
    • 张南菀 - A historical figure.
    • 汉武帝 - Emperor Wu of Han.
    • 回中郡 - A specific region.

    Emperor Wu of Liang was extremely fond of Buddhism, yet he had a benevolent and tolerant nature. He imperially ordered contributions of fine silk fabrics, but prohibited the weaving of patterns of birds and beasts, fearing that when cutting, it might cause harm to living beings.

    Xue Dading, the governor of Cangzhou, diverted seawater, which benefited fish and salt production, and the people of the town sang about it. At the time, the governor of Yingzhou, Jia Dunshun, and the governor of Jizhou, Zheng Deb [28]

    汉光武爱惜士卒，每欲发一兵，头须悉白。
    　　东方朔伏日对武帝拔剑割肉，帝令自责。曰：“拔剑割肉，一何勇也；割之不多，一何廉也；归遗细君，一何仁也。”帝曰：“令卿自责，反自誉也。”蒙赐酒一斛、肉一百斤，令遗细君。
    　　秦穆公亡善马，岐山野人共得而食之。吏欲法之，公曰：“君子不以畜害人。吾闻食马肉不饮酒，伤人。”皆赐酒沃之。后与晋战，而三百人争死以报恩。
    　　卫为性通恕，常自戒曰：“人之不逮，可以情恕；非意相干，可以理遣。”故终身无怨怒。
    　　汉阴丈人抱瓮入井，负水灌园。有人教其为桔槔，用力寡而利用多。丈人曰：“吾宁自倦败，不可以机为用，有伤真性。”
    　　秦始皇欲观日，乃造石桥海岸，驱使鬼运。始皇曰：“欲见君形，可乎”海神遂出，谓始皇左右曰：“我形甚丑，勿画我形。”其下有巧者，暗以脚画地图之。神怒，海岸遂崩。始皇脱走，仅免死，左右皆陷没焉。 [29]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [29]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 愛惜士卒 - Cherishing or caring for soldiers, emphasizing concern for troops.
    • 頭須悉白 - Hair and beard turned completely white, indicating extreme worry or stress.
    • 伏日 - A specific day during the伏 period, a time of heat and rest in ancient China.
    • 自責 - To blame oneself, a gesture of self-criticism or humility.
    • 一何勇也 - How brave it is, an exclamation emphasizing boldness.
    • 割之不多 - He didn't cut too much, suggesting moderation in the act.
    • 一何廉也 - How frugal it is, an exclamation praising restraint.
    • 歸遺細君 - To give it back to his wife, indicating a gesture of kindness.
    • 一何仁也 - How kind it is, an exclamation praising the act of giving to his wife.
    • 一斛 - A unit of measurement for wine.
    • 一百斤 - A unit of measurement for meat.
    • 亡善馬 - Lost a prized horse.
    • 岐山野人 - The people of Qishan.
    • 共得而食之 - Shared and ate the horse.
    • 法之 - Punished according to the law.
    • 君子不以畜害人 - A gentleman does not harm people for the sake of animals.
    • 吾聞食馬肉不飲酒，傷人 - Eating horse meat without drinking wine harms people, a traditional belief.
    • 皆賜酒沃之 - They were all given wine to drink.
    • 後與晉戰 - Later, they fought against Jin.
    • 而三百人爭死以報恩 - Three hundred men fought to the death to repay the favor, indicating loyalty and gratitude.
    • 衛為性通恕 - Wèi  had a nature that was broad and forgiving.
    • 常自戒曰 - He often warned himself, indicating self-discipline.
    • 人之不逮 - When people fail to meet expectations.
    • 可以情恕 - It can be forgiven out of compassion.
    • 非意相干 - When someone unintentionally causes harm.
    • 可以理遣 - It can be resolved through reason.
    • 故終身無怨怒 - Therefore, he never harbored resentment or anger, indicating a peaceful and forgiving nature.
    • 抱瓮入井 - Carried a jar into the well.
    • 負水灌園 - Carried water to irrigate his garden.
    • 為桔槔 - Used a shadoof, a type of lever for lifting water.
    • 用力寡而利用多 - Requires little effort but provides great utility, indicating efficiency.
    • 吾寧自倦敗，不可以機為用，有傷真性 - Would rather tire himself out than use a machine, as it would harm his true nature.
    • 秦始皇欲觀日 - Wanted to see the sun, indicating a desire to witness a celestial event.
    • 乃造石橋海岸 - Built a stone bridge on the coast.
    • 驅使鬼運 - Commanded spirits to transport materials, indicating supernatural assistance.
    • 欲見君形，可乎 - Can I see your form?
    • 海神遂出 - The sea god then appeared.
    • 謂始皇左右曰 - Said to Emperor Qin's attendants.
    • 我形甚丑，勿畫我形 - My form is very ugly, do not draw my form, indicating modesty.
    • 其下有巧者 - Among his attendants, there was a clever one.
    • 暗以腳畫地圖之 - Secretly drew a map with his feet, indicating a covert act.
    • 神怒 - The god became angry.
    • 海岸遂崩 - The coastline then collapsed, indicating destruction caused by the god's anger.
    • 始皇脫走 - Emperor Qin escaped.
    • 僅免死 - Barely escaped death, indicating the narrowness of his escape.
    • 左右皆陷沒焉 - His attendants were all drowned.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 漢光武 - Emperor Guangwu of Han, the founder of the Eastern Han Dynasty.
    • 伏日 - A traditional Chinese festival during the伏 period, a time of heat and rest.
    • 東方朔 - A famous courtier and scholar during the Han Dynasty, known for his wit and wisdom.
    • 武帝 - Emperor Wu of Han, a powerful and influential emperor of the Han Dynasty.
    • 秦穆公 - Emperor Mu of Qin, a ruler of the Qin state during the Warring States period.
    • 岐山 - A mountain in Shaanxi Province, historically significant in Chinese culture.
    • 秦始皇 - Emperor Qin Shi Huang, the first emperor of a unified China.
    • 晉 - The state of Jin, one of the major states during the Warring States period.
    • 衛 - A historical figure known for his broad-minded and forgiving nature.
    • 漢陰丈人 - An old man from Hanyin, a place in ancient China, known for his traditional methods.
    • 桔槔 - A type of lever used for lifting water, a simple but effective tool.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • 漢光武愛惜士卒，每欲發一兵，頭須悉白 - Describes Emperor Guangwu's concern for his soldiers, leading to his hair and beard turning white. The phrase '每欲發一兵' can be interpreted as 'every time he wanted to send out a single soldier,' indicating his reluctance to engage in conflict.
    • 伏日對武帝拔劍割肉，帝令自責 - Describes Eastern朔's act of cutting meat on the伏 day and Emperor Wu's command for him to blame himself. The phrase '帝令自責' can be interpreted as 'the emperor ordered him to blame himself,' indicating a gesture of humility.
    • 秦穆公亡善馬，岐山野人共得而食之 - Describes Emperor Mu of Qin's loss of his favorite horse and the people of Qishan eating it. The phrase '共得而食之' can be interpreted as 'they shared and ate it,' indicating a communal act.
    • 吾聞食馬肉不飲酒，傷人 - Describes the belief that eating horse meat without drinking wine harms people. The phrase '吾聞' can be interpreted as 'I have heard,' indicating a traditional belief.
    • 而三百人爭死以報恩 - Describes three hundred men fighting to the death to repay the favor. The phrase '爭死以報恩' can be interpreted as 'they fought to the death to repay the favor,' indicating their loyalty and gratitude.
    • 衛為性通恕，常自戒曰 - Describes Wèi 's broad-minded and forgiving nature and his self-discipline.

    Emperor Guangwu of Han cherished his soldiers, and whenever he wanted to send out a single soldier, his hair and beard turned completely white.

    On the day of伏, Eastern朔, in the presence of Emperor Wu, pulled out a sword to cut meat. The emperor ordered him to blame himself. Eastern朔 said, "Pulling out a sword to cut meat, how brave it is; he didn't cut too much, how frugal it is; giving it back to his wife, how kind it is." The emperor replied, "Ordering you to blame yourself, you ended up praising yourself instead." He was then granted a measure of wine and a hundred catties of meat, and ordered to give it to his wife.

    Emperor Mu of Qin lost his favorite horse, and the people of Qishan ate it together. The officials wanted to punish them, but the emperor said, "A gentleman does not harm people for the sake of animals. I have heard that eating horse meat without drinking wine harms people." They were all given wine to drink. Later, they fought against Jin, and three hundred men fought to the death to repay the favor.

    Wèi  had a nature that was broad and forgiving. He often warned himself, saying, "When people fail to meet expectations, it can be forgiven out of compassion; when someone unintentionally causes harm, it can be resolved through reason." Therefore, he never harbored resentment or anger.

    An old man from Hanyin carried a jar into the well and carried water to irrigate his garden. Someone taught him to use a shadoof, which required little effort but provided great utility. The old man said, "I would rather tire myself out than use a machine, as it would harm my true nature."

    Emperor Qin Shi Huang wanted to see the sun, so he built a stone bridge on the coast and commanded spirits to transport materials. He said, "Can I see your form?" The sea god then appeared and said to Emperor Qin's attendants, "My form is very ugly, do not draw my form." Among his attendants, there was a clever one who secretly drew a map with his feet. The god became angry, and the coastline then collapsed. Emperor Qin escaped, barely escaping death, while his attendants were all drowned. [29]

    历阳县有一媪，常为善。忽有少年过门求食。待之甚恭，临去，谓媪曰：“时往县，见门阃有血，即可登山避难。”自是，媪日往之门。吏问其状，媪答以少年所教。吏即戏以鸡血涂门阃。明日，媪见有血，乃携鸡笼走山上。其夕，县陷为湖，今和州历阳湖是也。
    　　傅奕常不信佛法。高祖时有西国胡僧，能口吐火以威胁众。奕对高祖曰：“此胡法不足信，若火能烧臣，即为圣者。”高祖试之，立胡僧于殿西，奕于殿东，乃令胡僧作法。于是跳跃禁咒，火出僧口，直触奕，奕端笏曰：“乾，元亨利贞。邪不干正。”由是火返焰，烧僧立死。
    　　晋孝武欲为晋陵公主求婿，问王曰：“得及刘真长、王子敬便足。”曰：“谢琨不及刘真长，不减王子敬。”帝然之。未几，帝崩。后司空袁崧欲以女妻谢琨，曰：“卿勿近禁脔。”元帝初渡江，国内常乏，朝士每烹猪，以项肉一斤尤脆美，进充御食。时人以此为“禁脔”。 [30]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [30]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 歷陽縣 - Place name, meaning 'Liyang County.'
    • 忽有少年過門求食 - Suddenly, a young man passed by the door asking for food.
    • 待之甚恭 - Treated him with great respect.
