    • 時往縣，見門阃有血，即可登山避難 - Whenever you go to the county, if you see blood at the threshold, climb the mountain to avoid disaster.
    • 日往之門 - Go to the door every day.
    • 吏問其狀 - The official asked about the situation.
    • 戲以雞血涂門阃 - Played a trick by painting the threshold with chicken blood.
    • 傅奕常不信佛法 - Fu Yi often did not believe in Buddhism.
    • 高祖時有西國胡僧 - During the reign of Emperor Gaozu, there was a foreign monk from the Western countries.
    • 能口吐火以威嚇眾 - Could spit fire to intimidate the crowd.
    • 此胡法不足信 - This foreign method is not trustworthy.
    • 若火能燒臣，即為聖者 - If the fire can burn me, then he is a saint.
    • 立胡僧於殿西，奕於殿东 - The monk was placed on the west side of the hall, and Fu Yi on the east side.
    • 乃令胡僧作法 - Then ordered the monk to perform his magic.
    • 乾，元亨利贞 - Qian, yuan heng li zhen (the opening hexagram of the *I Ching*).
    • 邪不干正 - Evil cannot harm righteousness.
    • 晋孝武欲为晋陵公主求婿 - Emperor Xiaowu of Jin wanted to find a husband for Princess Jinling.
    • 得及刘真长、王子敬便足 - If he could match Liu Zhenzhang and Wang Zijing, that would be enough.
    • 谢琨不及刘真长，不减王子敬 - Xie Kun is not as good as Liu Zhenzhang, but not inferior to Wang Zijing.
    • 元帝初渡江 - During the early years of Emperor Yuan's crossing of the river.
    • 国内常乏 - The country was often short of resources.
    • 朝士每烹猪，以项肉一斤尤脆美 - Officials often cooked pork, and the neck meat was especially tender and delicious.
    • 禁脔 - A forbidden delicacy.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 傅奕 - A historical figure, a scholar and critic of Buddhism.
    • 高祖 - Refers to Emperor Gaozu of Tang, the founder of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 胡僧 - Refers to a foreign monk, likely from Central Asia.
    • 晋孝武 - Emperor Xiaowu of Jin, a ruler of the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 晋陵公主 - Princess Jinling, a member of the Jin royal family.
    • 刘真长 - Liu Zhenzhang, a prominent official and scholar of the Jin Dynasty.
    • 王子敬 - Wang Zijing, a famous calligrapher and official of the Jin Dynasty.
    • 谢琨 - Xie Kun, a historical figure, likely a relative or associate of the Xie family.
    • 元帝 - Emperor Yuan of Jin, a ruler of the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 禁脔 - A term for a delicacy that was off-limits or reserved for special occasions.

    Proper Nouns

    • 傅奕 - A historical figure, a scholar and critic of Buddhism.
    • 高祖 - Refers to Emperor Gaozu of Tang.
    • 胡僧 - Refers to a foreign monk.
    • 晋孝武 - Emperor Xiaowu of Jin.
    • 晋陵公主 - Princess Jinling.
    • 刘真长 - Liu Zhenzhang.
    • 王子敬 - Wang Zijing.
    • 谢琨 - Xie Kun.
    • 元帝 - Emperor Yuan of Jin.

    Liyang County had an elderly woman who often did good deeds. Suddenly, a young man passed by her door asking for food. She treated him with great respect. As he was leaving, he told the old woman, "Whenever you go to the county, if you see blood at the threshold, climb the mountain to avoid disaster." From then on, the old woman went to the door every day. An official asked her about the situation, and she replied that the young man had told her. The official then played a trick by painting the threshold with chicken blood. The next day, the old woman saw blood and took the chicken cage up the mountain. That night, the county sank into a lake, which is now known as Liyang Lake in Hezhou.

    Fu Yi often did not believe in Buddhism. During the reign of Emperor Gaozu, there was a foreign monk from the Western countries who could spit fire to intimidate the crowd. Fu Yi told Emperor Gaozu, "This foreign method is not trustworthy. If the fire can burn me, then he is a saint." Emperor Gaozu tested him by placing the monk on the west side of the hall and Fu Yi on the east side, then ordered the monk to perform his magic. The monk jumped and chanted spells, and fire emerged from his mouth, directly touching Fu Yi. Fu Yi held his tablet and said, "Qian, yuan heng li zhen. Evil cannot harm righteousness." As a result, the fire turned back and burned the monk, killing him instantly.

    Emperor Xiaowu of Jin wanted to find a husband for Princess Jinling and asked Wang Zijing, "If he can match Liu Zhenzhang and Wang Zijing, that would be enough." Wang Zijing replied, "Xie Kun is not as good as Liu Zhenzhang, but not inferior to Wang Zijing." The emperor agreed. Soon after, the emperor died. Later, the Chancellor Yuan Song wanted to marry his daughter to Xie Kun, and Wang Zijing said, "Do not get too close to the forbidden delicacy." During the early years of Emperor Yuan's crossing of the river, the country was often short of resources. Officials often cooked pork, and the neck meat was especially tender and delicious, becoming a favorite dish for the imperial table. People at the time referred to this as "the forbidden delicacy." [30]

    唐大历中，万年尉侯彝者，好侠尚义，常匿国贼。御史推鞠，理穷，终不言贼所往。御史曰：“贼在汝右膝盖下。”彝遂揭阶砖，自击其膝盖，翻示御史曰：“贼安在”即以钅敖贮烈火，置其腹上，烟火蓬勃，左右皆不忍视。彝叫曰：“何不加炭”御史奇之，奏闻代宗。即召对：“何为隐贼自贻其苦若是。”彝答曰：“贼，实臣藏之，已然诺其人，终死不可得。”遂以贼故，贬为瑞州高安尉。
    　　周穆王南征，一军尽化为猿鹤，君子为鹤，小人为猿。
    　　陈胜以丹帛书“陈胜王”字，置鱼腹中，令卖。有市得者，烹食之，见而怪之，遂立胜为王。
    　　刘备尝乘爱马号“的卢”。居樊城，刘表欲因会杀之，备走，陷檀溪，乃语的卢曰：“今日之意，得不努力乎”马一跃三丈，遂脱难。
    　　任公子为钓，用十五，蹲于会稽，期年无所得。一日获大鱼，自荆江东皆厌腥臊。 [31]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [31]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 好侠尚义 - Describes someone who values chivalry and righteousness.
    • 今日之意，得不努力乎 - A rhetorical question implying that the horse should try its best.
    • 一跃三丈 - A metaphorical expression indicating a great leap.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 唐大历 - The Dali era of the Tang Dynasty (766-779 AD).
    • 万年尉 - A title for a county magistrate.
    • 侯彝 - The name of the main character.
    • 御史 - A high-ranking official responsible for oversight and investigation.
    • 会稽 - A region in present-day Zhejiang Province.
    • 荆江 - A section of the Yangtze River in Hubei Province.
    • 任公子 - A legendary figure known for his fishing skills.
    • 十五 - A unit of measurement for the length of a fishing line, symbolizing patience and persistence.
    • 期年 - One year.
    • 荆江东 - The eastern part of the Jing River.

    Proper Nouns

    • 唐大历 - The Dali era of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 万年尉 - A title for a county magistrate.
    • 侯彝 - The name of the main character.
    • 御史 - A high-ranking official.
    • 会稽 - A region in Zhejiang Province.
    • 荆江 - A section of the Yangtze River.
    • 任公子 - A legendary figure.
    • 十五 - A unit of measurement for the length of a fishing line.
    • 期年 - One year.
    • 荆江东 - The eastern part of the Jing River.

    During the Dali era of the Tang Dynasty, there was a county magistrate named Hou Yi in the county of Wan Nian. He was known for his chivalry and righteousness and often harbored traitors. When the censor investigated him, all logical arguments were exhausted, but he still refused to reveal the whereabouts of the traitor. The censor said, "The traitor is hidden in your right knee." Hou Yi then lifted a brick from the floor and struck his own knee, turning to show the censor and saying, "Where is the traitor?" He then placed a metal container with a fire on his stomach, and the smoke and flames rose vigorously, making those around him unable to bear the sight. Hou Yi shouted, "Why not add more charcoal?" The censor was amazed and reported this to Emperor Dali. The emperor summoned him and asked, "Why did you hide the traitor and endure such suffering?" Hou Yi replied, "The traitor is indeed hidden by me, but I have made a promise to him, and it is impossible for me to reveal him even at the cost of my life." Because of this, he was demoted to the position of magistrate in Gao'an, Rui Prefecture.

    During the reign of King Mu of Zhou, he led a southern campaign. One day, all the soldiers were transformed into monkeys and cranes. The nobles became cranes, and the commoners became monkeys.

    Chen Sheng wrote the words "Chen Sheng is king" on a piece of red silk and placed it in a fish's belly. Someone bought the fish, cooked it, and found the silk, which surprised him. He then proclaimed Chen Sheng as king.

    Liu Bei once rode a beloved horse named "Dilu." While staying in Fan Cheng, Liu Biao wanted to kill him during a banquet. Liu Bei fled and fell into the Tan Creek. He then told Dilu, "Today, you must try your best." The horse leaped three meters and helped Liu Bei escape.

    Ren Gongzi went fishing, using a fishing line that was fifteen  long. He sat by the West Lake and waited for a year without catching anything. One day, he caught a large fish, and the smell of the fish spread throughout the Jing River area. [31]

    齐田子方出，见老马于道，喟然有志焉，以问御者：“何马也”对曰：“以公家畜罢而不用，故出放之。”子方曰：“少尽其力，老弃其身，仁者不为。”遂束物以赎之。穷士闻之，知所归矣。
    　　吴起〔吮座疽〕，卒母哭曰：“往年吴起吮其父疽，父不旋踵而死。今吮其子，妾不知〔其〕死所也。”
    　　塞翁失马，乡人皆唁，翁曰：“未必不为福。”明年，引群马至。人复贺，翁曰：“未必不为祸。”子孙、家僮出入多爱乘马，坠折四肢。乡人复唁，翁曰：“未必不为福。”又明年，西胡入国，国中但能披甲者，皆征行之。子孙、家僮以残毁免。

    　　●卷中
    　　吴公子光飨王僚，令专诸侍。置剑于蒸鱼腹中，因进鱼，抽剑刺杀王僚。
    　　晋羊祜，字叔子，为荆州守，有恩及闾里。及死，阖境并不言祜字，其有同音，亦改讳之。襄阳百姓于岘山立堕泪碑。 [32]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [32]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 喟然有志焉 - Expresses a sigh of deep emotion or reflection.
    • 以問御者 - Means 'to ask the driver.'
    • 何馬也 - A straightforward question meaning 'What kind of horse is this?'
    • 以公家畜 - Refers to a horse belonging to the state or public.
    • 罷而不用 - Means 'tired and not used,' indicating the horse is old and no longer in service.
    • 少盡其力 - Means 'when young, it has expended its strength.'
    • 老棄其身 - Means 'when old, it is discarded.'
    • 仁者不為 - A statement of moral principle, meaning 'a benevolent person would not do this.'
    • 遂束物以贖之 - Means 'he then tied up some goods to redeem it.'
    • 知所歸矣 - Means 'knew where to return.'
    • 吳起吮座疽 - Refers to Wu Qi, a historical figure, sucking a wound on a seat.
    • 卒母哭曰 - Means 'the mother of the deceased cried and said.'
    • 往年吳起吮其父疽 - Means 'last year Wu Qi sucked his father's wound.'
    • 父不旋踵而死 - Means 'his father died without turning his foot.'
    • 妾不知其死所也 - Means 'I don't know where he will die.'
    • 塞翁失馬 - A well-known Chinese idiom meaning 'the old man lost his horse,' often used to illustrate the principle of good and bad luck being relative.
    • 鄉人皆唁 - Means 'all the villagers came to mourn.'
    • 未必不為福 - Means 'it may not be a misfortune.'
    • 明年，引群馬至 - Means 'the next year, a group of horses arrived.'
    • 人復賀 - Means 'people came to congratulate.'
    • 未必不為禍 - Means 'it may not be a blessing.'
    • 子弟、家僮出入多愛乘馬 - Means 'the children and servants often rode the horses.'
    • 墜折四肢 - Means 'falling and breaking limbs.'
    • 闔境並不言祜字 - Means 'throughout the territory, no one mentioned the character 祜.'
    • 同音，亦改諱之 - Means 'even if it had the same sound, they also changed it.'
    • 襄陽百姓於峴山立墮淚碑 - Means 'the people of Xiangyang erected a weeping stone on Xianshan.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 齊田子方 - Tian Zifang, a historical figure from the state of Qi.
    • 吳起 - Wu Qi, a famous general and statesman from the Warring States period.
    • 塞翁 - The old man from the idiom '塞翁失马' (the old man lost his horse).
    • 羊祜 - Yang Hu, a high-ranking official and military leader from the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 襄陽 - Xiangyang, a city in Hubei Province, China.
    • 峴山 - Xianshan, a mountain near Xiangyang.

    Proper Nouns

    • 齊田子方 - Tian Zifang.
    • 吳起 - Wu Qi.
    • 塞翁 - The old man from the idiom '塞翁失马.'
    • 羊祜 - Yang Hu.
    • 襄陽 - Xiangyang.
    • 峴山 - Xianshan.

    When Tian Zifang of Qi left, he saw an old horse by the road and sighed deeply, moved by the sight. He asked the driver, "What kind of horse is this?" The driver replied, "It is a horse belonging to the state, tired and no longer used, so it has been released." Tian Zifang said, "When young, it has expended its strength, and when old, it is discarded. A benevolent person would not do this." He then tied up some goods to redeem the horse. The poor scholar, upon hearing this, knew where to return.

    Wu Qi sucked a wound on a seat, and the mother of the deceased cried, saying, "Last year, Wu Qi sucked his father's wound, and his father died without turning his foot. Now he is sucking his son, and I don't know where he will die."

    The old man lost his horse, and all the villagers came to mourn. The old man said, "It may not be a misfortune." The next year, a group of horses arrived. People came to congratulate, and the old man said, "It may not be a blessing." The children and servants often rode the horses, and falling and breaking limbs occurred. The villagers came to mourn again, and the old man said, "It may not be a misfortune."

    Throughout the territory, no one mentioned the character 祜. Even if it had the same sound, they also changed it. The people of Xiangyang erected a weeping stone on Xianshan.

    Volume II

    Prince Guang of Wu entertained Lord Liao of Wu, ordering Zhuan Zhu to serve. He placed a sword in the belly of a steamed fish and, as the fish was served, drew the sword to stab and kill Lord Liao.

    Yang Hu, styled Shizi, served as the governor of Jingzhou, and his benevolence reached the common people. Upon his death, the entire territory did not mention the character 祜. Even if it had the same sound, they also changed it. The people of Xiangyang erected a weeping stone on Xianshan. [32]

    王允欲杀蔡邕，马日曰：“邕逸才多艺，详汉传事，何可害之”允曰：“汉武不杀司马迁，使作谤书流于后。今岂可使佞臣执笔，我辈等蒙其讪议耶”遂杀之。
    　　魏陈寿撰《三国志》，丁е、丁仪俱有盛名于魏，寿谓其子曰：“与我千斛米，当为尊公立佳传。”其子不与之。遂不作传。
    　　晋王蒙、刘忄炎并为中书侍郎，及宋，辅政，俱加侍中。时人故号为“入室之宾”也。
    　　汉赵尧为周昌侍御史，人谓之曰：“赵尧乃奇士也，必代君为大夫。”昌曰：“尧，刀笔吏。何至此也”后昌为赵王相，高帝持大夫印，视尧曰：“无以易尧。”乃授尧。
    　　帝问王夷甫曰：“寿阳以东常涝，寿阳以西常旱，何也”更甫曰：“寿阳以东，吴人，亡国哀音，鼎足强邦，一朝失职，叹愤为阴，阴积成水，故常涝；寿阳以西，中国，新平强吴，美宝尽入，志盈心满，常欢娱，故旱。” [33]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [33]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 逸才多艺 - Describes someone as having exceptional talent and multiple skills.
    • 刀笔吏 - Refers to a clerk or scribe, often used to describe someone who handles official documents.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 王允 - A prominent politician and general during the late Eastern Han dynasty.
    • 蔡邕 - A renowned scholar, calligrapher, and musician of the Eastern Han dynasty.
    • 汉武 - Emperor Wu of Han, a powerful emperor known for his military campaigns and cultural achievements.
    • 司马迁 - A historian and writer of the 'Records of the Grand Historian'.
    • 魏 - The state of Wei during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 陈寿 - A historian who wrote the 'Records of the Three Kingdoms'.
    • 丁е - A prominent figure in the state of Wei.
    • 丁仪 - A prominent figure in the state of Wei.
    • 晋 - The state of Jin during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 赵尧 - A figure in the Han dynasty.
    • 周昌 - A high-ranking official in the Han dynasty.
    • 高帝 - Emperor Gao of Han, the founder of the Han dynasty.
    • 寿阳 - A place name, possibly referring to a region in China.
    • 吴 - The state of Wu during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 中国 - Refers to the Central States, a term used to describe the core regions of China.
    • 王夷甫 - A historical figure, possibly a scholar or official.

    Proper Nouns

    • 王允 - Wang Yun
    • 蔡邕 - Cai Yong
    • 马日 - Ma Rong
    • 汉武 - Emperor Wu of Han
    • 司马迁 - Sima Qian
    • 魏 - Wei
    • 陈寿 - Chen Shou
    • 丁е - Ding E
    • 丁仪 - Ding Yi
    • 晋 - Jin
    • 王蒙 - Wang Meng
    • 刘忄炎 - Liu Yi
    • 赵尧 - Zhao Yao
    • 周昌 - Zhou Chang
    • 高帝 - Emperor Gao of Han
    • 寿阳 - Shouyang
    • 吴 - Wu
    • 中国 - Central States
    • 王夷甫 - Wang YiFu

    Wang Yun intended to kill Cai Yong, but Ma Rong said, "Cai Yong is a man of exceptional talent and many skills, as recorded in the Han histories. How can he be harmed?" Wang Yun replied, "Emperor Wu of Han did not kill Sima Qian, allowing him to write a defamatory book that spread later. How can we now allow a sycophantic minister to write, and subject ourselves to his ridicule and criticism?" And so, Cai Yong was killed.

    Chen Shou of Wei wrote the "Records of the Three Kingdoms." Ding E and Ding Yi both had great fame in Wei. Chen Shou told his son, "Give me a thousand bushels of rice, and I will write a good biography for your father." His son did not give it to him, and so Chen Shou did not write the biography.

    Wang Meng and Liu Yi both served as Secretaries of the Central Secretariat during the Jin dynasty. When they came to the Song dynasty, they both served as advisors and were promoted to the position of Attendant. People at the time called them "Inner Chamber Guests."

    Zhao Yao of Han served as a Censor for Zhou Chang. People said to him, "Zhao Yao is a remarkable man, and he will surely replace you as a Doctor." Zhou Chang said, "Zhao Yao is just a clerk. How did he get here?" Later, Zhou Chang became the Chancellor of the State of Zhao. Emperor Gao held the seal of a Doctor and looked at Zhao Yao, saying, "There is no one better than Zhao Yao." And so, Zhao Yao was appointed to the position.

    The emperor asked Wang YiFu, "Why is it that east of Shouyang there is often flooding, and west of Shouyang there is often drought?" Wang YiFu said, "East of Shouyang, the people of Wu, with the lamentations of a lost state, a powerful nation that once stood as a tripod, lost their position, their sorrow and anger turned to darkness, which accumulated into water, hence the frequent flooding. West of Shouyang, the Central States, having recently subdued the strong Wu, all the treasures were taken in, their hearts filled with ambition and joy, hence the frequent drought." [33]

    因畅为河南尹，时久旱，祷祠无应，乃收葬傍城客死骸骨百余具。遽降大雨，年谷丰稔。
    　　后魏曹彰，性倜傥。偶逢骏马，爱之，其主所惜也。彰曰：“余有美妾可换，唯君所选。”马主因指一妓，彰遂换之。马号曰“白鹘”。后因猎，献于文帝。
    　　《武陵记》曰：后汉马融勤学，梦见一林，花如绣锦，梦中摘此花食之。及寤，见天下文词，无所不知。时人号为“绣囊”。
    　　崔豹《古今注》：齐王后怨死，尸化为蝉，遂登庭树，ィ唳而鸣。后王悔恨，闻蝉即悲叹。
    　　陈宣帝时，扬州人严泰江行逢渔舟，问之，云：“有龟五十头。”泰用钱五百赎放之。行数十步，渔舟乃覆。其夕，有乌衣五十人扣泰门，谓其父母曰：“贤郎附钱五百，可领之。”缗皆濡湿。父母虽受钱，不知其由。泰归，问焉，乃赎龟之异。因以其居为寺。里人号“法严寺”。 [34]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [34]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 因畅为河南尹 - Formal statement indicating that someone named 'Chang' became the governor of Henan.
    • 祷祠无应 - Means 'prayers and sacrifices were unanswered,' referring to the failure of religious rituals to bring rain.
    • 收葬傍城客死骸骨百余具 - Means 'to collect and bury over a hundred corpses of travelers who died near the city.' The term '客死' (died as a guest) is used to describe travelers who died away from home.
    • 遽降大雨，年谷丰稔 - Means 'it suddenly rained heavily, and the crops were abundant.'
    • 偶逢骏马，爱之，其主所惜也 - Means 'chanced upon a fine horse, loved it, which its owner cherished.'
    • 余有美妾可换，唯君所选 - Means 'I have a beautiful concubine who can be exchanged, you may choose her.'
    • 马号曰‘白鹘’ - The horse was named 'Bai Gu' (White Hawk).
    • 后因猎，献于文帝 - Means 'later, during a hunt, he presented it to Emperor Wen.'
    • 《武陵记》曰 - Refers to the *Wuling Ji* (Records of Wu Ling), an ancient text.
    • 后汉马融勤学，梦见一林，花如绣锦，梦中摘此花食之 - Means 'During the Eastern Han dynasty, Ma Rong, who was diligent in his studies, dreamed of a forest where the flowers were like embroidered brocade, and in the dream, he picked and ate these flowers.'
    • 及寤，见天下文词，无所不知 - Means 'Upon waking, he found that he knew all the literary works in the world.'
    • 时人号为‘绣囊’ - Means 'People at the time called him the 'Embroidered Bag.'
    • 崔豹《古今注》 - Refers to Cui Bao's *Gujin Zhu* (Notes on Ancient and Modern Times), an ancient text.
    • 齐王后怨死，尸化为蝉，遂登庭树，ィ唳而鸣 - Means 'The queen of Qi died in resentment, and her body transformed into a cicada, which then climbed onto a courtyard tree and chirped.'
    • 后王悔恨，闻蝉即悲叹 - Means 'Later, the king regretted and would weep upon hearing the cicadas.'
    • 陈宣帝时，扬州人严泰江行逢渔舟，问之，云：“有龟五十头。” - Means 'During the reign of Emperor Xuan of Chen, a man named Yan Tai from Yangzhou encountered a fishing boat while traveling on the river and asked about it; the fisherman said, 'There are fifty turtles.'
    • 泰用钱五百赎放之 - Means 'Yan Tai paid 500 coins to redeem and release them.'
    • 行数十步，渔舟乃覆 - Means 'After walking several dozen steps, the fishing boat capsized.'
    • 其夕，有乌衣五十人扣泰门，谓其父母曰：“贤郎附钱五百，可领之。” - Means 'That night, fifty people dressed in black robes knocked on Yan Tai's door and told his parents, 'Your son has brought 500 coins; you may collect them.'
    • 缗皆濡湿 - Means 'The coins were all wet.'
    • 父母虽受钱，不知其由 - Means 'His parents accepted the coins but did not know the reason.'
    • 泰归，问焉，乃赎龟之异 - Means 'Yan Tai returned home and asked about it, and it turned out to be a strange event related to redeeming the turtles.'
    • 因以其居为寺 - Means 'He then built a temple on the site.'
    • 里人号‘法严寺’ - Means 'The locals called it 'Fayin Temple.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 后魏 - Refers to the Northern Wei dynasty, a significant period in Chinese history.
    • 曹彰 - A historical figure known for his bravery and leadership.
    • 后汉 - Refers to the Eastern Han dynasty, another significant period in Chinese history.
    • 马融 - A famous scholar and literary figure during the Eastern Han dynasty.
    • 绣囊 - A nickname given to Ma Rong due to his exceptional knowledge.
    • 崔豹《古今注》 - An ancient text that records various historical and cultural facts.
    • 齐王 - Refers to the king of the state of Qi during the Warring States period.
    • 陈宣帝 - Refers to Emperor Xuan of Chen, a ruler during the Southern Dynasties period.
    • 法严寺 - A temple built on the site where a miraculous event occurred.

    Proper Nouns

    • 后魏 - The Northern Wei dynasty.
    • 曹彰 - A historical figure.
    • 后汉 - The Eastern Han dynasty.
    • 马融 - A famous scholar.
    • 绣囊 - A nickname for Ma Rong.
    • 崔豹《古今注》 - An ancient text.
    • 齐王 - The king of the state of Qi.
    • 陈宣帝 - Emperor Xuan of Chen.
    • 法严寺 - A temple.

    Translation

    Chang served as the governor of Henan during a prolonged drought when prayers and sacrifices went unanswered. He then collected and buried over a hundred traveler corpses who had died near the city. Suddenly, it rained heavily, and the crops were abundant.

    Cao Zhang of the Northern Wei dynasty was known for his free-spirited and unrestrained character. One day, he chanced upon a fine horse and loved it, which its owner cherished. Cao Zhang said, "I have a beautiful concubine who can be exchanged; you may choose her." The horse owner pointed to a courtesan, and Cao Zhang exchanged the horse for her. The horse was named "Bai Gu" (White Hawk). Later, during a hunt, he presented the horse to Emperor Wen.

    According to the Wuling Ji (Records of Wu Ling), during the Eastern Han dynasty, Ma Rong, who was diligent in his studies, dreamed of a forest where the flowers were like embroidered brocade. In the dream, he picked and ate these flowers. Upon waking, he found that he knew all the literary works in the world. People at the time called him the "Embroidered Bag."

    Cui Bao's Gujin Zhu (Notes on Ancient and Modern Times) records that the queen of Qi died in resentment, and her body transformed into a cicada, which then climbed onto a courtyard tree and chirped. Later, the king regretted and would weep upon hearing the cicadas.

    During the reign of Emperor Xuan of Chen, a man named Yan Tai from Yangzhou encountered a fishing boat while traveling on the river and asked about it; the fisherman said, "There are fifty turtles." Yan Tai paid 500 coins to redeem and release them. After walking several dozen steps, the fishing boat capsized. That night, fifty people dressed in black robes knocked on Yan Tai's door and told his parents, "Your son has brought 500 coins; you may collect them." The coins were all wet. His parents accepted the coins but did not know the reason. Yan Tai returned home and asked about it, and it turned out to be a strange event related to redeeming the turtles. He then built a temple on the site, and the locals called it "Fayin Temple." [34]

    晋王导子悦，年二十，有名，为中书郎。导尝梦人以百万买悦，于梦中领之。导寤，不乐，亟为祈祷。未几，修墙，掘得钱百万，导意恶之，一皆不用。及悦病，导复梦一被甲持刀，自称蒋山侯，索食。食毕，作色谓导曰：“公儿巳卖与他。”言讫，觉。翌日，悦卒。
    　　汉景帝好游猎，见虎不能得之，乃为珍馔祭所见之虎。帝乃梦虎曰：“汝祭我，欲得我牙皮耶我自杀，从汝取之。”明日，帝之山，果见此虎死在祭所，乃命剥取皮牙。余肉悉化为虎而去。
    　　后汉马略，年十七，闭室读书。九年不出，三日一食，续命而已。乡里谓之“潜龙”。三十，谒桓帝，曰：“我，贤人也。”遂拜关内侯光州刺史。略弃官入海，恶虫猛兽悉避路。
    　　《吕氏春秋》曰：齐有二烈士别于路，相与沽酒共饮。其人欲市肉，一人曰：“子亦肉也，我亦肉也，无须往市。”因以刀各割身肉递相食啖。须臾，酒与肉皆尽而俱死。 [35]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [35]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 领之 - Literally means 'to lead or take,' used here in the context of the dream.
    • 恶之 - Means 'to consider it evil' or 'to regard it as ominous.'
    • 作色 - Means 'to change color' or 'to become angry.'
    • 言讫 - Means 'after speaking.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋王导 - Refers to Wang Dao, a prominent politician and military leader during the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 汉景帝 - Refers to Emperor Jing of Han, the sixth emperor of the Han dynasty.
    • 蒋山侯 - A title given to a noble or a deity.
    • 桓帝 - Refers to Emperor Huan of Han, the tenth emperor of the Eastern Han dynasty.
    • 关内侯 - A title of nobility in the Han dynasty.
    • 刺史 - An official title in the Han dynasty, similar to a governor.
    • 吕氏春秋 - A philosophical and historical text compiled by Lü Buwei, a chancellor of the Qin state during the Warring States period.

    Proper Nouns

    • 子悦 - Wang Dao's son, named Yue.
    • 中书郎 - An official position in the imperial government.
    • 珍馔 - Fine dishes or delicacies.
    • 后汉马略 - Ma Lüo from the Later Han dynasty.
    • 潜龙 - A metaphorical term often used to describe a person of great potential who is not yet recognized.

    Wang Dao's son, Yue, was twenty years old, famous, and held the position of Zhongshu Lang. Wang Dao once dreamed that someone bought Yue for a million coins and led him away in the dream. Wang Dao woke up feeling unhappy and prayed urgently. Soon after, while repairing the wall, they dug up a million coins, which Wang Dao considered ominous and did not use. When Yue fell ill, Wang Dao had another dream where a person wearing armor and holding a knife, calling himself Prince Jiangshan, asked for food. After eating, the person in the dream became angry and told Wang Dao, "Your son has already been sold to someone else." After speaking, Wang Dao woke up. The next day, Yue died.

    Emperor Jing of Han enjoyed hunting and once saw a tiger but could not catch it, so he prepared fine dishes to sacrifice to the tiger. Emperor Jing then had a dream where the tiger said, "You sacrificed to me, do you want my teeth and skin? I will kill myself, and you can take them." The next day, Emperor Jing went to the mountain and indeed found the tiger dead at the place of sacrifice, and he ordered the skin and teeth to be taken. The remaining flesh all turned into tigers and left.

    Ma Lüo from the Later Han dynasty, at the age of seventeen, studied in seclusion for nine years, eating only once every three days to sustain his life. The villagers called him "Hidden Dragon." At the age of thirty, Ma Lüo paid homage to Emperor Huan and said, "I am a virtuous person." He was then appointed as Marquis of the Inner Pass and Governor of Guangzhou. Ma Lüo resigned from office and entered the sea, and all evil insects and fierce beasts avoided the path.

    According to the Lüshi Chunqiu, two virtuous men from Qi met on the road and bought wine to drink together. One of them wanted to buy meat, and the other said, "You are also meat, and I am also meat, there is no need to go to the market." They then used knives to cut their own flesh and fed each other. Soon after, both the wine and the meat were finished, and they both died. [35]

    梁武帝大清三年，侯景反，围台城，远近不通。简文与太子大器为计，缚鸢飞空，告急于外。侯景谋臣谓景曰：“此必厌胜术，不然即事达人。”令左右射之。及堕，皆化为禽鸟飞去，不知所在。
    　　《京房列传》曰：房临刑之时，谓人曰：“吾死之后，客星入天井。”举朝皆哀之。
    　　王充《论衡》云：汉李子长为政，欲知囚情，以桐木刻为囚象，凿地为坎，致木人拷讯之，若正罪则木人不动，如冤枉则木人摇其头。精感立政，动神如此。
    　　王子年《拾遗记》曰：燕昭王时，波弋国人贡茶芜之香，若焚，著衣而弥月不绝，过地则土石皆香，经朽木与腐草则皆荣秀，用薰枯骨则肌肉再生。
    　　后汉明帝杨后，花面美色，有颠狂病，发则杀人。唯内傅孟召为文哀怨，后每读之，颠狂辄醒。时人语曰：“孟召文，差颠狂。” [36]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [36]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 梁武帝大清三年 - Specific date in the reign of Emperor Wu of Liang, using the era name 'Dachen'.
    • 侯景反，圍台城 - Refers to the rebellion of Hou Jing, who besieged Tai Cheng, the capital of the Liang dynasty.
    • 遠近不通 - Concise phrase meaning 'communication was cut off', indicating the isolation caused by the siege.
    • 簡文與太子大器為計 - Means 'Emperor Jianwen and Crown Prince Daqi devised a plan'.
    • 繫鴉飛空 - Refers to the act of tying a kite to a message and sending it into the air, a method of communication during the siege.
    • 告急于外 - Means 'to send an urgent message to the outside world'.
    • 侯景謀臣謂景曰 - Means 'Hou Jing's advisors told Hou Jing'.
    • 此必厭勝術 - Refers to the idea that the kite might be a form of magical or superstitious ritual.
    • 不然即事达人 - Means 'otherwise, it is a clever device to reach people'.
    • 令左右射之 - Means 'ordered his attendants to shoot it down'.
    • 及墮，皆化為禽鳥飛去 - Means 'when it fell, it transformed into birds and flew away'.
    • 不知所在 - Means 'no one knew where they went'.
    • 《京房列傳》曰 - Refers to the biography of Jing Fang in the Book of Han.
    • 房臨刑之时 - Means 'Jing Fang was about to be executed'.
    • 謂人曰 - Means 'told someone'.
    • 吾死之后，客星入天井 - Refers to a prediction that after his death, a guest star would enter the well (天井), a metaphor for his tomb.
    • 舉朝皆哀之 - Means 'the entire court mourned him'.
    • 王充《論衡》云 - Refers to Wang Chong's Lunheng (Balanced Discourse).
    • 漢李子長為政 - Refers to Li Zichang, a figure in Wang Chong's text.
    • 欲知囚情 - Means 'wanted to know the truth of the prisoner's situation'.
    • 以桐木刻為囚象 - Means 'carved a wooden figure of the prisoner'.
    • 致木人拷訊之 - Means 'placed the wooden figure under interrogation'.
    • 若正罪則木人不動 - Means 'if the prisoner was guilty, the wooden figure would not move'.
    • 如冤枉則木人搖其頭 - Means 'if the prisoner was innocent, the wooden figure would shake its head'.
    • 精感立政 - Means 'the power of spirit and emotion can establish justice'.
    • 王子年《拾遺記》曰 - Refers to Wang Zini's Shiyiji (Record of Lost and Found).
    • 燕昭王时 - Refers to the time of King Zhao of Yan.
    • 波弋国人贡茶芜之香 - Refers to the gift of a fragrant tea from the people of Boyi.
    • 若焚，著衣而弥月不绝 - Means 'if burned, the fragrance would linger on clothing for a month'.
    • 过地则土石皆香 - Means 'if passed over the ground, the earth and stones would become fragrant'.
    • 经朽木与腐草则皆荣秀 - Means 'if passed over decaying wood and rotten grass, they would become lush and beautiful'.
    • 用薰枯骨则肌肉再生 - Means 'if used to fumigate dry bones, flesh would regenerate'.
    • 后汉明帝杨后 - Refers to Empress Yang of the Eastern Han dynasty.
    • 花面美色 - Means 'beautiful and colorful face'.
    • 有颠狂病 - Means 'suffered from a form of mental illness'.
    • 发则杀人 - Means 'when she became violent, she would kill people'.
    • 唯内傅孟召为文哀怨 - Refers to the tutor Meng Zhao, who wrote mournful and sad texts.
    • 后每读之，颠狂辄醒 - Means 'Empress Yang would read them, and her madness would subside'.
    • 时人语曰 - Means 'people at the time said'.
    • 孟召文，差颠狂 - Means 'Meng Zhao's writing was a remedy for her madness'.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 梁武帝大清三年 - The year 549 CE during the reign of Emperor Wu of Liang.
    • 侯景 - A general who rebelled against the Liang dynasty, leading to the fall of the capital.
    • 台城 - The imperial palace and capital of the Liang dynasty.
    • 简文 - Emperor Jianwen of Liang, the ruler of the Liang dynasty.
    • 太子大器 - Crown Prince Daqi, the heir apparent to the Liang throne.
    • 《京房列传》 - A biography of Jing Fang, a Han dynasty astrologer and scholar.
    • 王充《论衡》 - A work by Wang Chong, a Han dynasty philosopher and critic.
    • 王子年《拾遗记》 - A collection of historical and mythological stories by Wang Zini.
    • 燕昭王 - King Zhao of Yan, a ruler of the Warring States period.
    • 后汉明帝杨后 - Empress Yang of the Eastern Han dynasty, known for her beauty and mental illness.

    Proper Nouns

    • 梁武帝 - Emperor Wu of Liang.
    • 侯景 - General Hou Jing.
    • 简文 - Emperor Jianwen of Liang.
    • 太子大器 - Crown Prince Daqi.
    • 京房 - Jing Fang, a Han dynasty astrologer.
    • 王充 - Wang Chong, a Han dynasty philosopher.
    • 王子年 - Wang Zini, a writer of the Six Dynasties period.
    • 燕昭王 - King Zhao of Yan.
    • 后汉明帝杨后 - Empress Yang of the Eastern Han dynasty.
    • 孟召 - Meng Zhao, a tutor to Empress Yang.

    In the third year of Emperor Wu of Liang's Dachen era, Hou Jing rebelled and besieged Tai Cheng, cutting off communication with the outside world. Emperor Jianwen and Crown Prince Daqi devised a plan to tie a kite to a message and send it into the air, in an attempt to send an urgent plea for help. Hou Jing's advisors told him, "This must be a form of magical ritual, or else it is a clever device to reach people." He ordered his attendants to shoot it down. When the kite fell, it transformed into birds and flew away, and no one knew where they went.

    The Biography of Jing Fang in the Book of Han states: "Jing Fang, on the eve of his execution, told someone, 'After my death, a guest star will enter the well.'" The entire court mourned him.

    Wang Chong's Lunheng records: "During the Han dynasty, Li Zichang, while governing, wanted to know the truth of a prisoner's situation. He carved a wooden figure of the prisoner, dug a pit in the ground, and placed the wooden figure under interrogation. If the prisoner was guilty, the wooden figure would not move; if the prisoner was innocent, the wooden figure would shake its head. The power of spirit and emotion can establish justice, and it moves the divine in such a way."

    Wang Zini's Shiyiji recounts: "During the reign of King Zhao of Yan, the people of Boyi presented a fragrant tea. If burned, the fragrance would linger on clothing for a month. If passed over the ground, the earth and stones would become fragrant. If passed over decaying wood and rotten grass, they would become lush and beautiful. If used to fumigate dry bones, flesh would regenerate."

    Empress Yang of the Eastern Han dynasty, during the reign of Emperor Ming, was known for her beautiful and colorful face but suffered from a form of mental illness. When she became violent, she would kill people. Only her tutor Meng Zhao, who wrote mournful and sad texts, could calm her. Whenever she read his writings, her madness would subside. People at the time said, "Meng Zhao's writing is a remedy for her madness." [36]

    《玉箱记》曰：前汉刘子光西征过山而渴，无水，子光在山间见一石人，问之曰：“何处有水”石人不答，乃拔剑斩石人。须臾，穷山水出。
    　　齐王奂二子融、琛，同是殷夫人四月二日孪生，又以四月二日同刑于都市。
    　　梁武太清元年，岳阳郡民王保幸种田六顷，悉生嘉禾。
    　　周厉王时，北斗与三台并流，不知其所。厉王没后，两主星复见。
    　　《搜神记》：吴时，葛祚为衡阳太守，先有大查当江损行舟，若祠祭者，查浮可见；不祭者，辄沈，暗覆行舟。祚造大斧数十，明旦往伐之。其夕，汹汹然，波浪振惊，查浮，遂移去，不为江中之患 人立碑，以诵祚之德也。
    　　干宝《搜神记》曰：“零陵太守史满有女，悦书吏，乃密使侍婢取吏食余残水，饮之，遂有孕。十月而生一子，及岁，太守使抱出门，儿匍匐入吏怀，吏推之仆地，化为水。具省前事，太守以女妻吏。 [37]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [37]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 玉箱記 - Title of a historical or anecdotal text, possibly referring to a collection of stories or records.
    • 西征過山而渴 - Literal description of Liu Ziguang's journey westward, crossing a mountain and becoming thirsty.
    • 石人不答 - Literal description of the stone figure not responding.
    • 拔劍斬石人 - Literal description of Liu Ziguang drawing his sword and cutting the stone figure.
    • 窮山水出 - Literal description of the mountain suddenly producing water.
    • 同是殷夫人四月二日孪生 - Literal description of the twins being born on the same day in April to Lady Yīn.
    • 又以四月二日同刑于都市 - Literal description of the twins being executed on the same day in April in the capital city.
    • 悉生嘉禾 - Literal description of all the land producing fine rice.
    • 北斗与三台并流 - Literal description of the Big Dipper and the Three Stars (a constellation) flowing together.
    • 不知其所 - Literal description of not knowing where they went.
    • 两主星复见 - Literal description of the two main stars reappearing.
    • 明旦往伐之 - Literal description of going to chop it down the next day.
    • 其夕，汹汹然，波浪振惊 - Literal description of that night, the waves were turbulent and alarming.
    • 查浮，遂移去 - Literal description of the log floating away and moving off.
    • 不为江中之患 - Literal description of no longer being a problem in the river.
    • 立碑，以诵祚之德也 - Literal description of setting up a stele to praise Ge Zuó's virtue.
    • 悦书吏 - Literal description of the daughter liking a clerk.
    • 乃密使侍婢取吏食余残水，饮之 - Literal description of secretly sending a servant to fetch the clerk's leftover water and drink it.
    • 遂有孕 - Literal description of becoming pregnant.
    • 及岁 - Literal description of by the time she was a year old.
    • 太守使抱出门 - Literal description of the governor having the child taken out of the door.
    • 儿匍匐入吏怀 - Literal description of the child crawling into the clerk's arms.
    • 吏推之仆地 - Literal description of the clerk pushing the child down to the ground.
    • 化为水 - Literal description of turning into water.
    • 具省前事 - Literal description of fully understanding the previous events.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 前汉 - Refers to the Western Han dynasty.
    • 齐王奂 - Refers to King Huàn of Qi, a historical figure.
    • 梁武太清元年 - Refers to the first year of the Taichang era during the reign of Emperor Wu of Liang.
    • 岳阳郡 - Refers to Yueyang, a historical region.
    • 周厉王 - Refers to King Lì of Zhou, a historical figure.
    • 北斗与三台 - Refers to the Big Dipper and the Three Stars, constellations in Chinese astronomy.
    • 吴时 - Refers to the time of the state of Wu.
    • 衡阳 - Refers to Héngyáng, a historical region.
    • 干宝《搜神记》 - Refers to Gan Bǎo's *Search for the Supernatural*, a collection of folktales and supernatural stories.
    • 零陵 - Refers to Línglíng, a historical region.

    Proper Nouns

    • 刘子光 - Liu Ziguang, a historical figure.
    • 融、琛 - The names of King Huàn's two sons.
    • 王保 - Wang Bao, a historical figure.
    • 葛祚 - Ge Zuó, a historical figure.
    • 干宝 - Gan Bǎo, author of *Search for the Supernatural*.
    • 史满 - Shǐ Mǎn, the governor of Línglíng.

    The Yuxiang Ji states: During the Western Han dynasty, Liu Ziguang was on a western campaign and, while crossing a mountain, became thirsty with no water available. In the mountains, Liu Ziguang saw a stone figure and asked it, "Where is there water?" The stone figure did not answer, so he drew his sword and cut the stone figure. Shortly after, water suddenly gushed out from the mountain.

    The two sons of King Huàn of Qi, Róng and Chēn, were born on the same day in April to Lady Yīn. They were also executed on the same day in April in the capital city.

    In the first year of the Taichang era during the reign of Emperor Wu of Liang, Wang Bao, a farmer from Yueyang, had six hectares of land that all produced fine rice.

    During the reign of King Lì of Zhou, the Big Dipper and the Three Stars flowed together, and their whereabouts were unknown. After King Lì's death, the two main stars reappeared.

    The Search for the Supernatural states: During the time of the state of Wu, Ge Zuó served as the governor of Héngyáng. There was a large log in the river that damaged boats. If one sacrificed to the log, it would float and become visible; if one did not sacrifice, it would sink and secretly capsize boats. Ge Zuó made dozens of large axes and went to chop it down the next day. That night, the waves were turbulent and alarming. The log floated away and moved off, no longer being a problem in the river. People set up a stele to praise Ge Zuó's virtue.

    Gan Bǎo's Search for the Supernatural states: "The governor of Línglíng, Shǐ Mǎn, had a daughter who liked a clerk. She secretly sent a servant to fetch the clerk's leftover water and drank it, and she became pregnant. By the time she was a year old, the governor had her taken out of the door. The child crawled into the clerk's arms, and the clerk pushed her down to the ground, where she turned into water. After fully understanding the previous events, the governor married his daughter to the clerk. [37]

    司马懿拜司空日，夜有人扣门请见，自称白虎使者，皆衣白衣，怀中探一物，内懿手中，戒曰：“两世慎勿开，墓中绝。”言讫不见。懿曰：“此或数也。”遂开视之，乃金龙子，长三四寸，背上有铭云：“父子从我受重火。”至武帝受禅，世墓中绝，元帝渡江，都建邺。
    　　《三峡录》云：宋顺帝升明二年，峡人微生亮于溪中钓得一白鱼，长三尺，投置船中，以草覆之。及归，取烹之，见一美女道下，洁白端丽，年可十六七。自称高唐之女，偶化鱼游，为君所得。亮曰：“既为人，能为妻否”女曰：“冥契使然，何为不得”其后三年为亮妻。女曰：“数以足矣，请归高唐。”亮曰：“何时复来”答曰：“情不可忘，有思即复至。”其后一岁三四往，不知所终。 [38]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [38]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 拜司空 - Formal title-granting ceremony for the position of Sikong, one of the Three Excellencies in the Han dynasty.
    • 夜有人扣門請見 - Formal expression meaning 'someone knocked on the door at night to request an audience.'
    • 白虎使者 - A mysterious figure or supernatural being, possibly a guardian or messenger.
    • 皆衣白衣 - All dressed in white robes, indicating a ritual or supernatural appearance.
    • 懷中探一物，內懿手中 - From his chest, he took out an object and placed it in the hand of Sima Yi, describing a mysterious transfer.
    • 兩世慎勿開，墓中絕 - Warning to 'be careful not to open for two generations, for the tomb will be cut off,' indicating a curse or prophecy.
    • 此或數也 - Perhaps this is fate, suggesting an acknowledgment of divine or supernatural intervention.
    • 乃金龍子 - It was a golden dragon offspring, referring to a small, golden dragon-like creature.
    • 背上有銘 - There was an inscription on its back, indicating a message or symbol.
    • 父子從我受重火 - The inscription means 'Father and son will receive the heavy fire from me,' possibly a prophecy or curse.
    • 受禪 - A formal act of transferring the imperial throne, here referring to the abdication of Emperor Xian of Han in favor of Cao Pi (曹丕) in 220 AD.
    • 元帝渡江 - Refers to the migration of the Jin dynasty's emperor Sima Rui (元帝) to the south in 317 AD, marking the beginning of the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 都建邺 - Established Jianye as the capital, referring to the new capital of the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 《三峡录》 - A historical or geographical text that records events and descriptions of the Three Gorges region.
    • 宋顺帝升明二年 - In the second year of the Shengming era of Emperor Shun of Song, referring to the year 478 AD.
    • 峡人微生亮 - A man named Weisheng Liang from the Three Gorges region.
    • 钓得一白鱼 - Caught a white fish, indicating a significant event.
    • 长三尺 - Three feet long, describing the size of the fish.
    • 投置船中，以草覆之 - Placed it in the boat and covered it with grass, indicating careful handling.
    • 及归，取烹之 - When he returned, he took it out to cook it, leading to the transformation.
    • 见一美女道下 - Saw a beautiful woman emerge, indicating a supernatural transformation.
    • 高唐之女 - A woman from Gao Tang, referring to a legendary or supernatural figure.
    • 偶化鱼游 - Accidentally transformed into a fish, indicating a magical or supernatural event.
    • 为君所得 - Was obtained by you, indicating a personal connection.
    • 既为人，能为妻否 - Now that I am human, can I be your wife? indicating a request for a relationship.
    • 冥契使然 - Fate has willed it so, indicating a predestined connection.
    • 数以足矣 - The number of times is enough, indicating a limit to the occurrences.
    • 请归高唐 - Please return to Gao Tang, indicating a request to leave.
    • 何时复来 - When will you come back? indicating a question about future visits.
    • 情不可忘 - The feeling cannot be forgotten, indicating a strong emotional bond.
    • 有思即复至 - If I think of you, you will come back, indicating a promise of return.
    • 一岁三四往 - Three or four times a year, indicating the frequency of visits.
    • 不知所终 - No one knows what happened to her, indicating an unknown fate.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 司马懿 - Sima Yi (179–251 AD), a prominent statesman and military general during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 司空 - One of the Three Excellencies in the Han dynasty, a high-ranking official position.
    • 白虎使者 - A supernatural figure, possibly a guardian or messenger, often associated with protection or divine intervention.
    • 金龙子 - A small, golden dragon-like creature, possibly a symbol of power or a supernatural being.
    • 受禅 - The act of transferring the imperial throne, a significant political event in Chinese history.
    • 元帝渡江 - The migration of the Jin dynasty's emperor Sima Rui to the south, marking the beginning of the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 建邺 - The new capital of the Eastern Jin dynasty, later renamed Jiankang (建康).
    • 《三峡录》 - A historical or geographical text that records events and descriptions of the Three Gorges region.
    • 宋顺帝 - Emperor Shun of Song (466–479 AD), the last emperor of the Liu Song dynasty.
    • 高唐之女 - A legendary or supernatural figure, possibly from a myth or folk tale.
    • 偶化鱼游 - A magical or supernatural event where a person transforms into a fish.
    • 冥契 - Fate or destiny, often used to describe predestined connections or events.
    • 有思即复至 - A promise of return based on thoughts or memories, indicating a strong emotional bond.
    • 不知所终 - An unknown fate, indicating that the person's ultimate destiny is not known.

    Proper Nouns

    • 司马懿 - Sima Yi, a prominent statesman and military general during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 司空 - One of the Three Excellencies in the Han dynasty.
    • 白虎使者 - A supernatural figure, possibly a guardian or messenger.
    • 金龙子 - A small, golden dragon-like creature.
    • 武帝 - Emperor Wu of Wei (曹丕), the first emperor of the Wei dynasty.
    • 元帝 - Emperor Shun of Jin (赵匡胤), the first emperor of the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 建邺 - The new capital of the Eastern Jin dynasty, later renamed Jiankang (建康).
    • 《三峡录》 - A historical or geographical text that records events and descriptions of the Three Gorges region.
    • 宋顺帝 - Emperor Shun of Song (萧道成), the last emperor of the Liu Song dynasty.
    • 微生亮 - A man named Weisheng Liang from the Three Gorges region.
    • 高唐之女 - A legendary or supernatural figure, possibly from a myth or folk tale.
    • 偶化鱼游 - A magical or supernatural event where a person transforms into a fish.
    • 冥契 - Fate or destiny, often used to describe predestined connections or events.
    • 有思即复至 - A promise of return based on thoughts or memories, indicating a strong emotional bond.

    Sima Yi was promoted to the position of Sikong, and that night, someone knocked on his door to request an audience. The person introduced himself as a Baihu messenger, dressed in white robes, and took an object from his chest, placing it in Sima Yi's hand. He warned, "Be careful not to open it for two generations, for the tomb will be cut off." After speaking, the messenger disappeared. Sima Yi remarked, "Perhaps this is fate." He then opened the object to see that it was a golden dragon offspring, about three to four inches long, with an inscription on its back that read, "Father and son will receive the heavy fire from me." When Emperor Wu of Wei received the throne through the act of shouchan (abdication), the Sima family's tomb was said to be cut off. Later, during the reign of Emperor Yuan of Jin, who crossed the river to establish Jiankang as the capital, the Sima family's fortunes declined.

    According to The Records of the Three Gorges, in the second year of the Shengming era of Emperor Shun of Song (478 AD), a man named Weisheng Liang from the Three Gorges region caught a white fish three feet long in a stream. He placed it in his boat and covered it with grass. When he returned home, he took it out to cook it, and a beautiful woman emerged. She was pure and graceful, around sixteen or seventeen years old. She introduced herself as a woman from Gao Tang who had accidentally transformed into a fish and was now in his possession. Weisheng Liang asked, "Now that you are human, can you be my wife?" She replied, "Fate has willed it so, why not?" They were married for three years. The woman then said, "The number of times is enough; please return to Gao Tang." Weisheng Liang asked, "When will you come back?" She answered, "The feeling cannot be forgotten; if I think of you, you will come back." She visited him three or four times a year, and no one knows what happened to her in the end. [38]

    成应元事统云：车胤好学，常聚萤光读书。时值风雨，胤叹曰：“天不遣我成其志业耶！”言讫，有大萤傍书窗，比常萤数倍，读书讫即去。如风雨，即至。
    　　汉黄霸为封溪令，部人陈廉携酒并猩猩以献。霸问：“是何物”人未及应，囊中语曰：“斗酒并仆耳。”霸以其物有灵，开囊放之，猩猩悲啼而去。
    　　梁徐勉为三公，武帝委以国事，每月三两归其家，家畜犬见，吠之。勉叹曰：“吾忧国忘家，以致如是！”
    　　《越绝书》曰：“越王勾践既为吴辱，尝尽礼接士，思以平吴。一日出游，蛙怒，勾践揖之。左右曰：“王揖怒蛙，何也”答曰：“蛙如是怒，可不揖”于是勇士闻之，皆归越而助平吴。 [39]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [39]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 成应元事统云 - Formal introductory phrase meaning 'According to the *Cheng Yingyuan Shi Tong* (Records of Cheng Yingyuan).'
    • 常聚萤光读书 - Literally 'often gather firefly light to read,' referring to the practice of using fireflies as a light source for studying.
    • 天不遣我成其志业耶 - Rhetorical question meaning 'Is it heaven that does not allow me to accomplish my aspirations?'
    • 比常萤数倍 - Means 'several times larger than ordinary fireflies.'
    • 读书讫即去 - Means 'after finishing reading, it leaves.'
    • 斗酒并仆耳 - Response from the bag meaning 'a bowl of wine and I myself.'
    • 吾忧国忘家 - Means 'I worry about the state and forget my family.'
    • 皆有先达，可为师表 - Refers to earlier accomplished figures who can serve as teachers and models.
    • 博学求之 - Means to seek knowledge broadly from many sources.
    • 无不利于事也 - Means 'nothing is unhelpful in matters,' suggesting that all roles or pursuits are valuable.
    • 越王勾践既为吴辱 - Means 'King Goujian of Yue, after being humiliated by Wu.'
    • 尝尽礼接士 - Means 'often treated scholars with utmost courtesy.'
    • 思以平吴 - Means 'thinking of how to defeat Wu.'
    • 王揖怒蛙 - Means 'the king bows to the angry frog.'
    • 蛙如是怒，可不揖 - Means 'if the frog is so angry, how can I not bow?'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 车胤 - A famous scholar from the Eastern Jin Dynasty known for his dedication to learning despite poverty.
    • 萤光 - Refers to the light emitted by fireflies, a symbol of perseverance and hard work.
    • 黄霸 - A notable official during the Han Dynasty, known for his integrity and effective governance.
    • 封溪 - A place name, likely referring to a district or county.
    • 徐勉 - A prominent official during the Southern Liang Dynasty, known for his wisdom and virtue.
    • 武帝 - Refers to Emperor Wu of Liang.
    • 三公 - Refers to high-ranking officials in the imperial court.
    • 越王勾践 - The legendary king of Yue who famously defeated the state of Wu.
    • 吴 - Refers to the state of Wu, a rival of Yue.

    Proper Nouns

    • 车胤 - A historical figure known for his dedication to learning.
    • 萤光 - The light emitted by fireflies.
    • 黄霸 - A historical figure known for his integrity.
    • 封溪 - A place name.
    • 陈廉 - A local person who brought gifts to Huang Ba.
    • 徐勉 - A historical figure known for his wisdom and virtue.
    • 武帝 - Emperor Wu of Liang.
    • 越王勾践 - The legendary king of Yue.
    • 吴 - The state of Wu.

    According to the Cheng Yingyuan Shi Tong: Cai Yun was a diligent scholar who often gathered firefly light to read. One day, during a storm, Cai Yun lamented, "Is it heaven that does not allow me to accomplish my aspirations?" As he finished speaking, a large firefly appeared near the study window, several times larger than ordinary fireflies. After he finished reading, it flew away. Whenever there was a storm, it would appear.

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, Huang Ba served as the magistrate of Fengxi. A local person named Chen Lian brought wine and a talking monkey as gifts. Huang Ba asked, "What is this?" Before anyone could respond, the monkey in the bag spoke, saying, "A bowl of wine and I myself." Huang Ba, believing the creature to be magical, opened the bag and released it, and the monkey wept and left.

    During the Southern Liang Dynasty, Xu Mian served as a high-ranking official. Emperor Wu of Liang entrusted him with state affairs, and he would return home only a few times a month. One day, his dog barked at him upon his return. Xu Mian sighed, "I worry about the state and forget my family, leading to this."

    The Yue Jue Shu states: "After being humiliated by Wu, King Goujian of Yue treated scholars with utmost courtesy, thinking of how to defeat Wu. One day, while out, a frog became angry, and Goujian bowed to it. His attendants asked, 'Why does the king bow to an angry frog?' Goujian replied, 'If the frog is so angry, how can I not bow?' Upon hearing this, the brave men all returned to Yue and helped him defeat Wu." [39]

    《搜神记》曰：冯棱妻死，棱器之恸，乃叹曰：“奈何不生一子而死！”俄而，妻复苏。后孕十月，产讫而死。
    　　始皇二十八年登封泰山，至半，忽大风雨雷电，路傍有五松树，荫翳数亩，乃封为五大夫。忽闻松上有人言曰：“无道德，无仁礼，而天下妄命。帝何以封”左右咸闻，始皇不乐，乃归，崩于沙丘。
    　　汉高祖每战，新当矢石，前后被七十二箭，或言灭七十二黑子。
    　　柳积，字德封。勤苦为学，夜燃木叶以代灯火。中夕，闻窗外有呼者，积出见之，有五六丈夫各负一囊，倾于屋下，如榆荚。语曰：“与君为书粮，勿忧业不成。”明旦起视，皆汉古钱，计得一百七十千，乃终其业。宋明帝时，官至东宫舍人。
    　　后汉刘圣公初得玺绶之夕，有流星下降，如绳绕圣公。明日为刘盆子将谢禄缢杀之，亦绕星之象。
    　　曹操无道，置发丘中郎、谋金校尉数十员。天下人冢墓，无问新旧，发掘时，骸骨横暴草野，人皆悲伤。其凶酷残忍如此。 [40]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [40]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 登封泰山 - A ritual act of ascending Mount Tai to offer sacrifices to heaven, symbolizing the emperor's legitimacy.
    • 封為五大夫 - To confer the rank of 'Five Great Officers' (五大夫), a title of nobility in the Zhou dynasty.
    • 無道德，無仁禮 - A critical statement condemning a lack of virtue and proper conduct.
    • 天下妄命 - To rule the world without just cause or moral authority.
    • 帝何以封 - A rhetorical question asking why the emperor bestowed titles without merit.
    • 新當矢石 - To face arrows and stones, meaning to be in the midst of battle.
    • 被七十二箭 - To be hit by seventy-two arrows, a specific detail emphasizing the severity of the injury.
    • 滅七十二黑子 - To remove seventy-two black spots, a metaphorical reference to the arrows, possibly suggesting healing or protection.
    • 勤苦為學 - To study diligently and with great effort.
    • 夜燃木葉以代灯火 - To burn wood leaves at night as a substitute for oil lamps, indicating poverty and resourcefulness.
    • 忽聞窗外有呼者 - To suddenly hear someone calling from outside the window.
    • 傾於屋下，如榆荚 - To pour out like elm seeds, a vivid description of the money falling.
    • 與君為書粮 - To provide you with provisions for your studies, emphasizing the generosity of the supernatural beings.
    • 博學求之 - To seek knowledge broadly and deeply.
    • 計得一百七十千 - To calculate that the amount was one hundred and seventy thousand coins.
    • 崩于沙丘 - To die at Shaoqiu (沙丘), a place associated with Qin Shi Huang's death.
    • 劉聖公 - Liu Shengong, a rebel leader during the late Western Han dynasty.
    • 得璽綬之夕 - The night when he obtained the imperial seal and ribbon, symbolizing his claim to the throne.
    • 流星下降，如繩繞聖公 - A meteor fell and wrapped around Liu Shengong, a portentous event.
    • 劉盆子將謝禄缢殺之 - Liu Penge's general Xie Lu strangled Liu Shengong, leading to his death.
    • 曹操無道 - Cao Cao was unjust or tyrannical.
    • 置發丘中郎、謀金校尉 - To appoint officials such as 'Faqiu Zhonglang' (發丘中郎) and 'Moujin Xiaowei' (謀金校尉), likely referring to officials tasked with excavating graves and seizing treasures.
    • 骸骨横暴草野 - Bones were scattered across the fields, a grim image of desecration.
    • 人皆悲伤 - People were all saddened, indicating widespread grief.
    • 凶酷残忍 - Cruel and ruthless, describing Cao Cao's harsh policies.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 《搜神記》 - A collection of supernatural stories and legends, often used to illustrate moral lessons or historical events.
    • 五大夫 - A noble title in the Zhou dynasty, indicating high social status.
    • 始皇二十八年 - The twenty-eighth year of Qin Shi Huang's reign, corresponding to 219 BCE.
    • 劉聖公 - A historical figure, known for his rebellion against the Western Han dynasty.
    • 崩于沙丘 - A significant event in Chinese history, marking the end of Qin Shi Huang's reign.

    Proper Nouns

    • 《搜神記》 - Classical anthology of folktales and legends, attributed to Gan Bao (干寶) of the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 紹興 - Era name of the Southern Song; rendered as Shaoxing.
    • 劉聖公 - Liu Shengong, a rebel leader during the late Western Han dynasty.
    • 劉盆子 - Liu Penge, a general who strangled Liu Shengong.
    • 曹操 - Cao Cao, a historical figure known for his policies and actions.

    Final Translation

    The Records of the Supernatural states: When Feng Ling's wife died, Feng Ling was deeply grieved and lamented, saying, "Why didn't she bear a son before dying!" Soon after, his wife revived. She later became pregnant for ten months and gave birth, but then died.

    In the twenty-eighth year of Qin Shi Huang's reign, he ascended Mount Tai for the ritual of offering sacrifices to heaven. Halfway up, there was a sudden storm with rain, lightning, and thunder. Beside the road, there were five pine trees, their shade covering several acres. Qin Shi Huang then bestowed upon them the rank of "Five Great Officers." Suddenly, someone was heard speaking from the top of the pines: "Lacking virtue and propriety, and ruling the world without just cause. Why did the emperor bestow titles?" Everyone present heard this, and Qin Shi Huang was displeased. He returned, and soon after, he died at Shaoqiu.

    Emperor Gaozu of Han often faced battles, where he was exposed to arrows and stones. He was hit by seventy-two arrows, or so it was said, that seventy-two black spots were removed from him.

    Liu Ji, whose courtesy name was De Feng, studied diligently and worked hard. At night, he burned wood leaves to light his way, as he had no oil lamps. One night, he heard someone calling from outside his window. He went out to see and found five or six men, each carrying a sack, pouring something like elm seeds onto the ground. They said, "We are providing you with provisions for your studies, so do not worry about your work not being completed." The next morning, when he looked, it was all ancient Han coins, amounting to one hundred and seventy thousand. With this, he completed his studies. During the reign of Emperor Mingdi of Song, he held the position of Eastern Palace Attendant.

    In the late Western Han dynasty, Liu Shengong, upon obtaining the imperial seal and ribbon, saw a meteor fall and wrap around him like a rope. The next day, Liu Penge's general Xie Lu strangled him, just as the meteor had predicted.

    Cao Cao was unjust, appointing officials such as "Faqiu Zhonglang" and "Moujin Xiaowei." Regardless of whether they were new or old, he ordered the excavation of tombs across the land. Bones were scattered across the fields, and people were all saddened by this. His cruelty and ruthlessness were evident. [40]

    成应元事统云：刘牧，字子仁，尝居南山野中，喜山鸟之啼，爱风松之韵，植果种蔬。野人侮之，多伐树践囿。牧曰：“我不负人，人何负我”俄有二虎，近其居，为见牧则摇尾，牧曰：“汝来护我也”虎辄俯首。历数年，牧卒，虎乃去。
    　　蚩尤是古之帝者，兄弟八十一人。皆铜头铁额，食沙啖石，然卒为黄帝所灭也。
    　　《搜神记》曰：宋康王以韩朋妻美而夺之，使朋筑青凌台，然后杀之。其妻请临丧，遂投身而死。王令分埋台左右。期年，各生一梓树，及大，树枝条相交。有二鸟哀鸣其上。因号之曰“相思树”。
    　　黄帝斩蚩尤，冢在高平寿长县，高七丈。时人常十月祠之，有赤气如匹绛，时人谓之“蚩尤旗”。
    　　《西京杂记》：弘成子少时，好学，尝有人过门授一文石，大如燕卵。吞之遂明悟，而更聪敏，为天下通儒。又五鹿充宗受学成子，成子一日病，乃吐此石。充宗受而吞之，又为名儒。 [41]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [41]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 成应元事统云 - Title or introductory phrase indicating that what follows is a story or record from the *Cheng Yingyuan Shitong* (《成应元事统》), a historical or anecdotal text.
    • 植果种蔬 - Literal phrase meaning 'planting fruits and vegetables,' used to describe agricultural activities.
    • 汝来护我也 - Colloquial phrase meaning 'you come to protect me,' used in the context of a human-tiger relationship.
    • 为见牧则摇尾 - Phrase describing the tigers' behavior, meaning 'when they see me, they wag their tails.'
    • 蚩尤是古之帝者 - Phrase stating that Chiyou was an ancient emperor.
    • 兄弟八十一人 - Literal phrase meaning 'eighty-one brothers,' referring to the large number of Chiyou's followers.
    • 铜头铁额 - Metaphorical phrase meaning 'copper head and iron forehead,' describing the physical appearance of Chiyou and his followers.
    • 食沙啖石 - Phrase meaning 'eating sand and gnawing on stones,' describing the unusual diet of Chiyou and his followers.
    • 为黄帝所灭也 - Phrase meaning 'were ultimately defeated by the Yellow Emperor,' indicating the historical outcome.
    • 《搜神记》曰 - Introductory phrase indicating that what follows is a quote from the *Shenxian Ji* (《搜神记》), a collection of supernatural stories.
    • 宋康王以韩朋妻美而夺之 - Phrase meaning 'King Song of Kang took Han Peng's wife because she was beautiful,' describing a historical event.
    • 使朋筑青凌台 - Phrase meaning 'he made Han Peng build the Qingling Terrace,' indicating a task given to Han Peng.
    • 然后杀之 - Phrase meaning 'and then killed him,' describing the tragic fate of Han Peng.
    • 遂投身而死 - Phrase meaning 'she threw herself into the grave and died,' describing the wife's sacrifice.
    • 有赤气如匹绛 - Phrase meaning 'there was a red vapor like a piece of red silk,' describing a supernatural phenomenon.
    • 时人谓之“蚩尤旗” - Phrase meaning 'people at that time called it the 'Chiyou Flag,' indicating a cultural interpretation of the phenomenon.
    • 《西京杂记》 - Title of a historical text, *Xijing Zaji* (《西京杂记》), which contains various anecdotes and historical notes.
    • 弘成子少时 - Phrase meaning 'when Hong Chengzi was young,' introducing a character and time period.
    • 好学 - Phrase meaning 'loved learning,' describing a character trait.
    • 尝有人过门授一文石 - Phrase meaning 'once a person passed by the door and gave him a piece of jade,' describing a mysterious event.
    • 吞之遂明悟 - Phrase meaning 'after swallowing it, he became enlightened,' indicating a transformative experience.
    • 为天下通儒 - Phrase meaning 'became a renowned scholar,' describing the character's achievement.
    • 五鹿充宗受学成子 - Phrase meaning 'Wu Lu Chongzong received teachings from Chengzi,' introducing a new character and relationship.
    • 成子一日病 - Phrase meaning 'one day Chengzi fell ill,' indicating a change in the narrative.
    • 乃吐此石 - Phrase meaning 'he then spat out the stone,' describing a physical action.
    • 充宗受而吞之 - Phrase meaning 'Chongzong took it and swallowed it,' indicating the transfer of the stone.
    • 又为名儒 - Phrase meaning 'and also became a famous scholar,' describing the new character's achievement.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 成应元事统 - A historical or anecdotal text that records various stories and events.
    • 刘牧 - A historical figure whose story is being recounted.
    • 南山野中 - A specific location in the south mountain wilderness, likely a place of retreat or seclusion.
    • 山鸟之啼，风松之韵 - Natural sounds and rhythms that describe the environment.

    The Cheng Yingyuan Shitong records: Liu Mu, courtesy name Ziren, once lived in the wilds of the South Mountain. He delighted in the songs of mountain birds and the rustling of wind through the pines, planting fruits and vegetables. The villagers mocked him, often cutting down trees and trampling his garden. Liu Mu said, "I have not wronged others; why should they wrong me?" Soon, two tigers approached his dwelling. When they saw Liu Mu, they wagged their tails. He said, "You come to protect me." The tigers bowed their heads. After several years, Liu Mu passed away, and the tigers departed.

    Chiyou was an ancient emperor, with eighty-one brothers. They all had copper heads and iron foreheads, eating sand and gnawing on stones, but were ultimately defeated by the Yellow Emperor.

    According to the Shenxian Ji: King Song of Kang took Han Peng's wife because she was beautiful and made Han Peng build the Qingling Terrace, then killed him. His wife requested to attend his funeral and threw herself into the grave, dying there. The king ordered her to be buried on either side of the terrace. After a year, each side grew a paulownia tree, which grew large and their branches intertwined. Two birds mourned sadly on top of them. People called this tree the "Tree of Longing."

    The Yellow Emperor defeated Chiyou, and his tomb is located in Shouchang County, Gaoping, standing seven zhang high. People often held ceremonies there in October, seeing a red vapor like a piece of red silk, which they called the "Chiyou Flag."

    The Xijing Zaji states: When Hong Chengzi was young, he loved learning. Once, a person passed by his door and gave him a piece of jade, as big as a swallow's egg. After swallowing it, he became enlightened and even more intelligent, becoming a renowned scholar throughout the land. Wu Lu Chongzong also received teachings from Chengzi. One day, Chengzi fell ill and spat out the stone. Chongzong took it and swallowed it, and he too became a famous scholar. [41]

    晋陶侃微时，丁父艰，将葬，忽失牛，不知所在。遇一老父，谓曰：“前有一牛眠圩中，其地若葬，位极人臣。”又指一山云：“亦其次，当世出二千石。”言讫不见。侃寻牛得之，因改葬地。
    　　汉太尉杨震以忠贞见黜，及还洛，叹曰：“吾居上司，疾奸臣樊丰之狡不能诛，知帑藏空虚而不能富。”因饮鸩而卒。门人冤之，天子嘉之。改葬日，有大鸟翼一丈三尺，集于柩前，低头垂泪。葬毕，乃飞去。时人以为忠贞所感。
    　　《会稽记》：上虞兰室山，葛玄所隐之处，有隐几化为鹿。鹿鸣，即县令有罪。
    　　司马郊，字子都，隐居华山向五十年，禽兽日游目前，有如家驯。每灌园，不食菜心，以其伤生意。及四时山果熟，果大，大鸟衔，果小，小鸟衔，俱送郊斋中，不知纪极。叹曰：“禽鸟送我果甚多，但可日料三十颗。”异日如戒。比三十年，及郊卒，百禽聚于庭，悲鸣累日而去。 [42]

    During his humble beginnings, Tao Kan experienced the loss of his father and was preparing for his burial when he suddenly lost his ox and did not know its whereabouts. He encountered an old man who said to him, "There is a cow sleeping in the field; if you were to bury your father there, you would reach the highest rank among ministers." The old man also pointed to a mountain and said, "That place is also suitable, and it would produce a governor or magistrate in this world." After he finished speaking, the old man disappeared. Tao Kan found his ox and changed the burial site accordingly.

    Yang Zhen, the Grand Commandant of the Han Dynasty, was dismissed due to his loyalty and integrity. Upon returning to Luoyang, he lamented, "I held a high position, yet I could not punish the cunning evil minister Fan Feng, nor could I fill the empty treasury." He then drank poison and died. His disciples mourned him, and the emperor praised him. On the day of his reburial, a large bird with a wingspan of one zhang and three chi appeared, gathered at the front of the coffin, lowered its head, and shed tears. After the burial was completed, the bird flew away. People believed this was a sign of Yang Zhen's loyalty and integrity.

    According to The Record of Kuaiji: In Shangyu, there is Lan Shi Mountain, where Ge Xuan lived in seclusion. There was a table that transformed into a deer. When the deer cried, it indicated that the county magistrate had committed a crime.

    Si Ma Xiao, also known as Zi Du, lived in seclusion on Mount Hua for more than fifty years. Wild animals roamed around him daily, as if they were tame. Whenever he watered the garden, he did not eat the hearts of vegetables because he believed it would harm the life force. When the fruits of the mountain ripened, large fruits were carried by big birds, and small fruits were carried by small birds, all delivered to Si Ma Xiao's study without limit. He remarked, "The birds deliver me so many fruits, but I can only eat thirty pieces per day." On another day, he followed this rule. After thirty years, when Si Ma Xiao died, a hundred birds gathered in the courtyard, mourned for several days, and then flew away. [42]

    魏陈思王曹植与文帝不叶。文帝即位，尝欲害之，又以思王太后之爱，不敢肆心。因召植游华林园，饮酒酣醉之，密遣左右缢杀。使者以弓弦三缢不死，而弦皆顿绝，植即惊觉。左右走白帝，帝自是后不敢害植。
    　　《华阳国志》：夜郎者，有一女子浣服水滨，忽见三节大竹筒至女前，闻竹中儿啼，剖而视之，得一男。收养及长，甚有武才，自立为夜郎侯，以竹为姓。
    　　淄川有女曰颜文姜，事姑孝谨，樵薪之外，复汲山泉以供姑饮。一旦，缉笼之下，忽涌一泉，清泠可爱。时人谓之“颜娘泉”，至今利物。
    　　汉杨仆为楼船将军，自以功高，耻为关外人，请以家财移关于新安，有诏从之。 [43]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [43]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 魏陈思王曹植 - Cao Zhi, a prince and poet of the Cao Wei state.
    • 不叶 - Means 'not in harmony' or 'at odds,' referring to the strained relationship between Cao Zhi and his brother, Cao Pi.
    • 文帝即位 - Means 'Emperor Wen ascended the throne,' referring to Cao Pi becoming the emperor.
    • 尝欲害之 - Means 'once intended to harm him,' indicating Cao Pi's desire to eliminate Cao Zhi.
    • 不敢肆心 - Means 'dared not act without restraint,' reflecting Cao Pi's hesitation due to their mother's favoritism toward Cao Zhi.
    • 饮酒酣醉之 - Means 'drinking to intoxication,' describing how Cao Pi got Cao Zhi drunk.
    • 密遣左右缢杀 - Means 'secretly ordered his attendants to strangle him,' referring to the plot to kill Cao Zhi.
    • 弓弦三缢不死 - Means 'three times strangled with a bowstring but did not die,' describing the failed attempt.
    • 弦皆顿绝 - Means 'the bowstrings all broke suddenly,' indicating the failure of the assassination attempt.
    • 惊觉 - Means 'awakened in alarm,' describing Cao Zhi's reaction upon realizing what happened.
    • 走白帝 - Means 'ran to inform the emperor,' referring to the attendants' immediate report to Cao Pi.
    • 自是后不敢害植 - Means 'from then on dared not harm Cao Zhi,' indicating Cao Pi's subsequent caution.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 魏陈思王曹植 - Cao Zhi, a renowned poet and prince, is known for his literary contributions and tragic fate under his brother's rule.
    • 文帝即位 - Cao Pi, the elder brother of Cao Zhi, became the first emperor of the Cao Wei state.
    • 华林园 - A famous imperial garden in Luoyang, often used for leisure and entertainment by the royal family.
    • 夜郎 - A kingdom in southwestern China during the Han dynasty, known for its unique customs and legends.
    • 颜文姜 - A legendary figure in Chinese folklore, known for her filial piety and the miraculous spring that appeared due to her devotion.
    • 杨仆 - A Han dynasty general and statesman, known for his military campaigns and political influence.
    • 楼船将军 - A high-ranking military officer responsible for naval operations.
    • 关外人 - Refers to people living outside the frontier regions, often associated with lower social status.
    • 新安 - A place name in present-day Henan province, where Cao Pi allowed Elang to relocate the border post.

    Proper Nouns

    • 魏陈思王曹植 - Cao Zhi, a prince and poet of the Cao Wei state.
    • 文帝 - Cao Pi, the first emperor of the Cao Wei state.
    • 华林园 - The Hualin Garden, a royal garden in Luoyang.
    • 夜郎 - A kingdom in southwestern China during the Han dynasty.
    • 颜文姜 - A legendary figure in Chinese folklore.
    • 杨仆 - A Han dynasty general and statesman.
    • 楼船将军 - A high-ranking military officer responsible for naval operations.
    • 关外人 - People living outside the frontier regions.
    • 新安 - A place name in present-day Henan province.

    Cao Zhi, the Prince of Wei and a renowned poet, was at odds with Cao Pi, the future Emperor Wen of Wei. After Cao Pi ascended the throne, he once intended to harm Cao Zhi but dared not act without restraint due to their mother's favoritism toward Cao Zhi. Cao Pi then invited Cao Zhi to visit the Hualin Garden, where he drank him to intoxication and secretly ordered his attendants to strangle him. The attendants tried three times to strangle Cao Zhi with a bowstring, but he did not die, and the bowstrings all broke suddenly. Cao Zhi then awoke in alarm. His attendants ran to inform the emperor, and from then on, Cao Pi dared not harm Cao Zhi.

    According to the Huayang Guozhi: In the kingdom of Yelang, there was a woman who was washing clothes by the water's edge. Suddenly, she saw a three-jointed bamboo tube come before her. Hearing a baby cry inside the bamboo, she cut it open and found a boy. She raised him, and when he grew up, he showed great martial talent. He declared himself the Lord of Yelang and took the surname Zhu.

    In Zichuan, there was a woman named Yan Wenjiang. She was filial and diligent in serving her mother-in-law, going beyond just chopping firewood to also fetch mountain spring water for her mother-in-law to drink. One day, under the basket she was weaving, a spring suddenly gushed forth, clear and refreshing. The people of the time called it "Yan Lady's Spring," and it continues to benefit people to this day.

    During the Han dynasty, Yang Pu served as the General of Naval Forces. He considered himself highly accomplished and was ashamed to be considered an outsider. He requested to use his personal wealth to relocate the border post to Xin'an, and the emperor granted his request. [43]

    《列子》曰：“韩娥过齐雍门，鬻歌假食，既毕，而余响绕梁三日不绝。娥因曼声哀哭，一里老幼悲愁垂涕相对，三日不食。复作长歌，于是雍门之人欣跃舞不止，乃厚赂遣之。
    　　项籍开始皇墓，探取珠宝，其余不尽取者，有金雁飞出墓外，为罗者所获。
    　　汉武帝元封中，浮圻国贡兰金之泥。其金生于汤泉。盛夏之日，波浪常沸，飞鸟不敢过，居人不敢渡。国人于水边，见有此泥，取为器物，色若紫磨金，其滑者如泥。贡于汉，帝取之，常封亟匣以辟邪魅。卫青、张骞皆蒙此泥封玺绶。帝既崩，紫泥遂绝。
    　　石虎于太武殿前造楼，高四十丈，以珠为帘，五色玉为佩。每风至，即惊触似音乐在空。过者皆仰视，爱之。又屑诸异香如粉，撒楼上，风吹四散，谓之“芳尘”。
    　　《吕氏春秋》曰：有人臭者，父母、兄弟、妻子、道路皆恶之，此人无所容是，乃之海上。海上有人悦其臭，昼夜随之，不能抛舍。 [44]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [44]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 鬻歌假食 - Singing for sale and eating for hire, referring to a form of entertainment where performers sing and receive payment.
    • 曼声哀哭 - Singing in a prolonged, mournful tone, describing a type of vocal performance.
    • 欣跃舞不止 - Continuous joy and dancing, indicating a strong emotional response.
    • 厚赂遣之 - Sending them away with generous gifts, showing the high regard for the performer.
    • 探取珠宝 - Digging up and taking away jewels, describing the act of looting a tomb.
    • 有金雁飞出墓外 - A golden goose flying out of the tomb, a supernatural event.
    • 为罗者所获 - Was caught by the netters, indicating that the golden goose was captured.
    • 兰金之泥 - Purple gold mud, a rare and valuable substance.
    • 其金生于汤泉 - The gold is born in the hot springs, describing the origin of the gold.
    • 波浪常沸 - The waves are always boiling, indicating the intense heat of the hot springs.
    • 飞鸟不敢过，居人不敢渡 - Birds and residents are afraid to cross, describing the extreme conditions of the hot springs.
    • 屑诸异香如粉 - Grinding various exotic fragrances into powder, describing the preparation of aromatic powders.
    • 撒楼上，风吹四散，谓之‘芳尘’ - Scattering the powder on the building, where the wind disperses it, creating a fragrant dust.
    • 有人臭者 - A person with a bad smell, describing someone with an unpleasant odor.
    • 父母、兄弟、妻子、道路皆恶之 - Parents, brothers, wives, and children, as well as passersby, all hate him, indicating the widespread dislike of the person.
    • 此人无所容是 - This person has no place to be, indicating that the person is socially isolated.
    • 乃之海上 - He went to the sea, describing the person's destination.
    • 海上有人悦其臭 - There were people on the sea who liked his smell, indicating that some people found the person's odor appealing.
    • 昼夜随之，不能抛舍 - They followed him day and night, unable to leave, describing the persistent attachment of these people.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 《列子》 - A collection of fables and anecdotes attributed to Lie Yukou, a philosopher from the Warring States period.
    • 韩娥 - A legendary singer from the State of Qi during the Warring States period.
    • 项籍 - Xiang Yu, a prominent warlord during the late Qin dynasty.
    • 汉武帝 - Emperor Wu of Han, a powerful emperor of the Han dynasty.
    • 浮圻国 - A mythical kingdom, possibly a reference to a distant or exotic land.
    • 汤泉 - Hot springs, often associated with healing properties in Chinese culture.
    • 石虎 - Shi Hu, a ruler of the Later Zhao dynasty.
    • 太武殿 - A grand hall, possibly a reference to a significant architectural structure.
    • 卫青 - Wei Qing, an important general and explorer during the Han dynasty.
    • 张骞 - Zhang Qian, an important general and explorer during the Han dynasty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 《列子》 - The *Liezi*.
    • 韩娥 - Han E.
    • 项籍 - Xiang Yu.
    • 汉武帝 - Emperor Wu of Han.
    • 浮圻国 - Fuqi Kingdom.
    • 兰金之泥 - Purple Gold Mud.
    • 汤泉 - Hot Springs.
    • 石虎 - Shi Hu.
    • 太武殿 - Taiwu Hall.
    • 卫青 - Wei Qing.
    • 张骞 - Zhang Qian.

    《Liezi》states: "Han E passed through the gate of Qi, selling her songs and food. After she finished, her lingering voice echoed around the beam for three days without ceasing. Han E then sang in a prolonged, mournful tone, causing the old and young in the surrounding area to weep and cry, unable to eat for three days. She then sang a long song, and the people of the gate of Qi rejoiced and danced continuously, so they sent her away with generous gifts.

    Xiang Yu began digging into the tomb of the First Emperor, searching for jewels. Those that were not fully taken, a golden goose flew out of the tomb, and was caught by the netters.

    During the reign of Emperor Wu of Han in the Yuanfeng period, the country of Fuqi presented purple gold mud. The gold was born in the hot springs. On hot summer days, the waves were always boiling, and birds dared not fly over, nor did the residents dare to cross. The people by the water saw this mud and took it to make objects, which were colored like polished purple gold, and the smooth ones were as soft as mud. It was presented to the Han dynasty, and the emperor took it, always sealing and storing it in a box to ward off evil spirits. Wei Qing and Zhang Qian were both honored with this mud to seal their seals and ribbons. After the emperor died, the purple mud was no longer available.

    Shi Hu built a tower in front of the Taiwu Hall, forty zhang high, with pearls as curtains and five-colored jade as pendants. Whenever the wind came, it would shock and touch like music in the air. Passersby all looked up and loved it. He also ground various exotic fragrances into powder and scattered them on the building, where the wind dispersed them, creating a fragrant dust called 'fragrant dust.'

    The Lüshi Chunqiu states: There was a person with a bad smell, and his parents, brothers, wives, and children, as well as passersby, all hated him. This person had no place to be, so he went to the sea. There were people on the sea who liked his smell, and they followed him day and night, unable to leave." [44]

    晋文公时，有蛇当道而横。文公以为不祥，反政修德，令吏守蛇。守吏夜梦有人杀蛇，曰：“何以当圣人道”觉而见蛇巳坏矣。
    　　《庄子》云：齐桓公出游于泽，泽畔见一物，其大如毂，其长如猿，紫衣而朱冠，见人则捧其首。公谓管仲曰：“此其怪乎”仲曰：“此委蛇也，见者必霸。”公后果霸，其国为五霸之首。
    　　魏公子无忌视事，忽有一逐鸠，鸠入公子案下，遂去。令捕，取数百，列于庭下，问之：逐鸠者当伏翅。有伏罪于地，乃杀之，而放其群。
    　　后汉卢景初生，项有一丛白毛，数之得四十九茎。后四十九年卒。
    　　后汉裴安祖，常息大树下，有鸷鸟逐一雄雉，雉急投安祖，忽触树而死。安祖哀之，置于荫地，俄顷复生，乃飞去。因寝，见一人衣冠甚伟，拜谢安祖，曰：“荷君保全，故此伸谢。”安祖年八十而卒。 [45]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [45]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 晋文公时 - Temporal phrase meaning 'during the reign of Duke Wen of Jin.'
    • 反政修德 - Means 'to return to governance and cultivate virtue,' indicating a shift in policy or personal conduct.
    • 令吏守蛇 - Means 'to order officials to guard the snake,' suggesting a directive to protect or monitor the snake.
    • 觉而见蛇巳坏矣 - Means 'awakening and seeing the snake already broken,' indicating a sudden realization of the snake's state.
    • 《庄子》云 - Means 'The *Zhuangzi* says,' introducing a quote or reference to the *Zhuangzi*.
    • 齐桓公出游于泽 - Means 'Duke Huan of Qi went out touring in a marsh,' describing a historical event.
    • 见者必霸 - Means 'those who see it will become hegemonic,' indicating a prophecy or prediction.
    • 魏公子无忌视事 - Means 'Marquis Wuji of Wei was in office,' indicating a historical figure's role.
    • 忽有一逐鸠 - Means 'suddenly a hawk chased a dove,' describing a sudden event.
    • 鸠入公子案下 - Means 'the dove entered under Marquis Wuji's desk,' indicating the dove's hiding place.
    • 有伏罪于地 - Means 'a hawk confessed guilt on the ground,' indicating the hawk's admission.
    • 卢景初生，项有一丛白毛 - Means 'Lu Jingzhu was born with a cluster of white hair on his neck,' describing a birthmark.
    • 裴安祖，常息大树下 - Means 'Pei Anzu often rested under a big tree,' indicating a habitual action.
    • 荷君保全 - Means 'to repay your protection,' indicating gratitude.
    • 年八十而卒 - Means 'died at the age of eighty,' indicating the age of death.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋文公 - Duke Wen of Jin, a prominent ruler of the State of Jin during the Spring and Autumn period.
    • 齐桓公 - Duke Huan of Qi, a famous ruler of the State of Qi during the Spring and Autumn period.
    • 魏公子无忌 - Marquis Wuji of Wei, a historical figure from the State of Wei.
    • 《庄子》 - The *Zhuangzi*, a foundational text of Daoism.
    • 卢景初 - A historical figure known for his birthmark.
