    • 裴安祖 - A historical figure known for his encounter with a dove and a hawk.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • This phrase describes a snake blocking the path, which is straightforward but requires careful translation to maintain the imagery.
    • This sequence of actions must be read as a coherent policy change and its implementation.
    • This dream sequence must be translated to convey the dreamer's experience and the subsequent realization.
    • This detailed description of a mythical creature must be translated to preserve the vivid imagery.
    • This dialogue must be translated to convey the exchange and the prophecy.
    • This sequence of events must be translated to maintain the narrative flow.
    • This detailed account of the hawk's capture and the subsequent actions must be translated to preserve the sequence of events.
    • This description of a birthmark must be translated to maintain the specific detail.
    • This sequence of events must be translated to maintain the narrative flow.
    • This sequence of events must be translated to maintain the narrative flow.
    • This dream sequence must be translated to convey the dreamer's experience and the subsequent realization.
    • This statement must be translated to indicate the age of death.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋文公 - Duke Wen of Jin.
    • 齐桓公 - Duke Huan of Qi.
    • 魏公子无忌 - Marquis Wuji of Wei.
    • 《庄子》 - The *Zhuangzi*.
    • 卢景初 - Lu Jingzhu.
    • 裴安祖 - Pei Anzu.

    During the reign of Duke Wen of Jin, a snake blocked the path. Duke Wen considered this an ill omen and thus changed his governance to cultivate virtue, ordering officials to guard the snake. One of the guards had a dream in which a person killed the snake, saying, "Why should a snake obstruct the path of a sage?" Upon waking, he found the snake already broken.

    The Zhuangzi records: During the reign of Duke Huan of Qi, he went out touring in a marsh. At the edge of the marsh, he saw a creature as large as a wheel and as long as an ape, dressed in purple and wearing a red cap. When it saw people, it would bow its head. Duke Huan said to Guan Zhong, "Is this creature a monster?" Guan Zhong replied, "This is a wēiyí (委蛇), and whoever sees it will become hegemonic." Indeed, Duke Huan later became a hegemon, and his state became the first of the Five Hegemons.

    Marquis Wuji of Wei was in office when suddenly a hawk chased a dove. The dove fled and hid under Marquis Wuji's desk, and the hawk flew away. He ordered the capture of hawks, catching hundreds of them and arranging them in the courtyard. He asked them, "The one who chased the dove should confess." One hawk confessed its guilt on the ground, and it was then killed, while the rest were released.

    During the Later Han dynasty, Lu Jingzhu was born with a cluster of white hair on his neck, numbering forty-nine strands. He died forty-nine years later.

    Also during the Later Han dynasty, Pei Anzu often rested under a big tree. One day, a fierce bird chased a rooster, which, in panic, flew into Pei Anzu's arms and suddenly hit a tree and died. Pei Anzu mourned the rooster, placed it in a shaded area, and soon after, the rooster revived and flew away. That night, he had a dream in which a man with a grand appearance bowed to thank Pei Anzu, saying, "I repay your protection, so I come to express my gratitude." Pei Anzu died at the age of eighty. [45]

    后汉郑宏为临淮太守，行春，有二白鹿夹车而行。宏异之，主簿黄国曰：“三公车旁画鹿，君必为相。”后位至太尉。
    　　宋长沙王道怜子义庆，在广陵卧病，食粥之次，忽有白虹入室，就食其粥。义庆掷器于阶，虹遂作风雨声，响撼庭户，良久不见。
    　　《列女传》：“陶答子相陶，其政不修而家益富。其妻抱子而泣，姑问：“泣何也”曰：“妾闻南山有玄豹，雾雨十日，不下食，欲为泽其身而有文章也，故有威而远害。今夫子不修德而家益厚，祸将至矣。”期年，而答子见诛。
    　　伪蜀李势宫人张氏，有妖容，势宠之。一旦化为大斑蛇，长丈余，送于苑中。夜复来寝床下。势惧，遂杀之。后有郑美人，势亦宠爱，化为雌虎，一夕，食势姬三人。未几，势为桓温所杀。 [46]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [46]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 行春 - Ritual performed by local officials during spring, promoting agriculture and social harmony.
    • 三公车旁画鹿 - Ancient omen where a deer appears beside the carriage of a high-ranking official, symbolizing good fortune or a significant change in status.
    • 为相 - To become a chancellor or prime minister, a high-ranking position in the government.
    • 化为大斑蛇 - To transform into a large spotted snake, associated with divine or demonic intervention.
    • 饭牛牧羊 - To feed cattle and herd sheep, a humble occupation emphasizing the importance of all social roles.
    • 皆有先达，可为师表 - All have predecessors who can serve as role models, emphasizing the value of learning from those who came before.
    • 博学求之，无不利于事也 - Broadly seek knowledge from many sources, and nothing will be unhelpful in matters, suggesting that learning from various sources is beneficial.
    • 期年而答子见诛 - After a year, Táo Dázǐ was killed, indicating a swift and severe punishment.
    • 化为雌虎，一夕，食势姬三人 - Transformed into a female tiger, one night, she ate three of Rìshì's concubines, highlighting the supernatural and dangerous nature of the event.
    • 后有郑美人，势亦宠爱，化为雌虎 - Later, there was a lady named Zheng, whom Rìshì also favored, and she too transformed into a female tiger, reinforcing the recurring theme of transformation and danger.
    • 未几，势为桓温所杀 - Shortly after, Rìshì was killed by Huán Wēn, indicating the ultimate downfall of the ruler.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 后汉郑宏为临淮太守 - Véng Hóng served as the governor of Lín Huái during the Eastern Han dynasty, a period known for its political and social reforms.
    • 宋长沙王道怜子义庆 - Yì Qìng was the son of Cáo Dào Lián, a prince of the Song dynasty, a period marked by cultural and literary achievements.
    • 陶答子相陶 - Táo Dázǐ served as the chancellor of the state of Táo, a historical figure known for his political and administrative roles.
    • 伪蜀李势宫人张氏 - Rì Shì, a ruler of the pseudo-Song state, had a concubine named Zhang who transformed into a snake, a supernatural event often associated with divine retribution.
    • 桓温 - Huán Wēn, a powerful military leader who eventually overthrew Rì Shì, a key figure in the transition of power during the late Western Jin and early Eastern Jin dynasties.

    Proper Nouns

    • 后汉 - Eastern Han dynasty.
    • 郑宏 - Véng Hóng.
    • 临淮 - Lín Huái, a region in the Eastern Han dynasty.
    • 宋 - Song dynasty.
    • 长沙王 - Prince of Cáo.
    • 道怜 - Cáo Dào Lián.
    • 义庆 - Yì Qìng.
    • 广陵 - Guāng Líng, a region in the Song dynasty.
    • 陶 - Táo, a state during the Warring States period.
    • 陶答子 - Táo Dázǐ.
    • 伪蜀 - Pseudo-Song state.
    • 李势 - Rì Shì.
    • 张氏 - Zhang, a concubine of Rì Shì.
    • 桓温 - Huán Wēn.
    • 郑美人 - Zheng, a concubine of Rì Shì.
    • 势 - Rì Shì, the ruler of the pseudo-Song state.

    During the Eastern Han dynasty, Véng Hóng served as the governor of Lín Huái. While performing the spring ritual, two white deer appeared alongside his carriage. Véng Hóng found this strange, and his subordinate, Huáng Guó, said, "When a deer appears beside the carriage of a high-ranking official, it is an omen that the official will become a chancellor." Later, Véng Hóng indeed rose to the position of Taivèi.

    In the Song dynasty, Yì Qìng, the son of Prince Cáo Dào Lián, was ill in Guāng Líng. One day, while eating porridge, a white rainbow entered the room and tried to eat his porridge. Yì Qìng threw the bowl down the steps, and the rainbow then made a sound like wind and rain, shaking the courtyard gates. After a long time, the rainbow disappeared.

    According to the Lì Nǚzhuàn (Biographies of Exemplary Women): "Táo Dázǐ served as the chancellor of Táo, but his administration was neglected, and his family became increasingly wealthy. His wife, holding her child, wept. Her mother-in-law asked, 'Why are you crying?' She replied, 'I have heard that the black leopard of the South Mountain, when it rains for ten days, does not eat, in order to make its fur glossy and beautiful, thus gaining its majestic appearance and avoiding harm. Now, my husband neglects virtue but his family grows richer, and disaster will surely come.' After a year, Táo Dázǐ was killed."

    In the pseudo-Song state, Rì Shì had a concubine named Zhang who had a beautiful but ominous appearance, and Rì Shì favored her. One day, she transformed into a large spotted snake, over ten feet long, and was sent to the imperial garden. That night, she returned and slept under his bed. Rì Shì was terrified and had her killed. Later, there was a lady named Zheng, whom Rì Shì also favored, and she too transformed into a female tiger. One night, she ate three of Rì Shì's concubines. Soon after, Rì Shì was killed by Huán Wēn. [46]

    吴道子善画神—元中，将军裴居母丧，诣道子，请于东都天宫寺图神鬼数壁，以资冥助。答曰：“废画巳久，若将军有意，为吾缠结舞剑一曲，庶因猛励，获通幽冥。”于是脱去衰服，若常时妆饰，走马如飞，左旋右抽，掷剑入云，高数十丈，若电光下射，引手执鞘承之，剑透空而下。观者数千人，无不悚栗。道子于是援毫图壁，俄顷之际，魔魅化出，飒然风起，为天下之壮观。道子平生所画，得意无出于是。
    　　晋王恺有牛，号“八百里”，常莹其是角。王武子戏与射赌，以金敌之，偶中的，谓左右曰：“可生采其心作炙。”至，食一脔而止。
    　　蜀将姜维既死，剖其腹，视其胆如斗大。
    　　左思构《三都赋》，门庭墙溷皆置纸笔，十年乃就。
    　　汉张仓年老而无齿，饮人乳，过百余岁终。常感王陵，母卒后奉陵妻，朝夕侍诺，如事其母。 [47]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [47]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 吳道子善畫神 - Idiomatic expression emphasizing Wu Daozi's expertise in painting the divine.
    • 請於东都天宫寺图神鬼数壁 - Idiomatic phrase indicating the act of painting multiple walls with divine and ghostly figures.
    • 废画已久 - Idiomatic phrase indicating a long hiatus in Wu Daozi's painting career.
    • 为吾缠结舞剑一曲 - Idiomatic phrase describing a specific performance combining dance and swordplay.
    • 庶因猛励，获通幽冥 - Idiomatic phrase expressing a hopeful intention to achieve a supernatural goal.
    • 脱去衰服 - Idiomatic phrase referring to the act of removing formal mourning attire.
    • 走马如飞 - Idiomatic phrase vividly describing swift horse riding.
    • 左旋右抽 - Idiomatic phrase describing the intricate movements of swordplay.
    • 掷剑入云 - Idiomatic phrase describing the dramatic act of throwing a sword into the clouds.
    • 若电光下射 - Idiomatic phrase using a metaphor for the speed and intensity of the sword's descent.
    • 引手执鞘承之 - Idiomatic phrase describing the precise action of catching the sword.
    • 魔魅化出 - Idiomatic phrase indicating the appearance of supernatural entities.
    • 飒然风起 - Idiomatic phrase describing a sudden, powerful gust of wind.
    • 为天下之壮观 - Idiomatic phrase emphasizing the grandeur and significance of the event.
    • 得意无出于是 - Idiomatic phrase expressing Wu Daozi's complete satisfaction with the result.
    • 常莹其角 - Idiomatic phrase describing the care taken to maintain the cow's horns.
    • 以金敌之 - Idiomatic phrase indicating a wager involving gold.
    • 偶中的 - Idiomatic phrase describing a fortunate outcome in the bet.
    • 可生采其心作炙 - Idiomatic phrase suggesting a gruesome method of preparing the cow.
    • 食一脔而止 - Idiomatic phrase describing a limited indulgence in eating the cow's meat.
    • 视其胆如斗大 - Idiomatic phrase describing the size of Jiang Wei's gallbladder.
    • 门庭墙溷皆置纸笔 - Idiomatic phrase describing Zuo Si's dedication to writing.
    • 过百余岁终 - Idiomatic phrase describing Zhang Cang's longevity.
    • 如事其母 - Idiomatic phrase describing Zhang Cang's filial piety and gratitude.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 吳道子 - Famous Chinese painter of the Tang dynasty, known for his contributions to Buddhist art.
    • 元中 - The Yuan dynasty (1271–1368), a period in Chinese history when the Mongols ruled China.
    • 东都天宫寺 - A temple in the East Capital, likely referring to Luoyang, which was the capital of the Eastern Han dynasty.
    • 裴 - A general during the Yuan dynasty, known for his military exploits.
    • 王恺 - A prince of the Jin dynasty, known for his wealth and extravagance.
    • 八百里 - A legendary horse or cow, often associated with extraordinary qualities.
    • 姜维 - A general of the Shu Han state during the Three Kingdoms period, known for his loyalty and military prowess.
    • 左思 - A writer of the Jin dynasty, best known for his work *The Three Capitals Fu*.
    • 张仓 - A historical figure from the Han dynasty, known for his longevity and filial piety.

    Proper Nouns

    • 吳道子 - Famous Chinese painter of the Tang dynasty.
    • 裴 - General during the Yuan dynasty.
    • 王恺 - Prince of the Jin dynasty.
    • 八百里 - Legendary horse or cow.
    • 姜维 - General of the Shu Han state during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 左思 - Writer of the Jin dynasty.
    • 张仓 - Historical figure from the Han dynasty.

    Wu Daozi was skilled in painting the divine. During the Yuan period, General Pei Ji, while in mourning for his mother, went to Wu Daozi and requested that several walls at the East Capital Tian Gong Temple be painted with gods and ghosts to aid in the afterlife. Wu Daozi replied, "I have stopped painting for a long time. If General you wish, perform for me a dance of entangled swordplay, perhaps through your fierce effort, I may gain access to the underworld." Pei Ji then took off his mourning clothes, dressed as he usually did, and rode his horse like flying, spinning left and pulling right, throwing his sword into the clouds, which soared dozens of feet high, like lightning shooting down. Pei Ji extended his hand to catch the hilt, and the sword plunged through the air and descended. Thousands of spectators were filled with awe. Wu Daozi then picked up his brush and painted the walls, and in the blink of an eye, evil spirits emerged, a sudden wind arose, creating a spectacular sight in the world. This was the most satisfying painting Wu Daozi had ever done.

    Prince Wang Kai of the Jin dynasty had a cow named "Eight Hundred Li," which he always polished its horns. Wang Wu Zi once bet against it with gold, and by chance, he hit it. He told his attendants, "It is possible to cut out its heart and roast it." When they did, he ate a piece and stopped.

    After General Jiang Wei of Shu died, his abdomen was cut open, and his gallbladder was found to be as large as a basket.

    Zuo Si composed the Three Capitals Fu, placing paper and ink in his doorways, walls, and latrines, and it took him ten years to complete it.

    Han Zhang Cang, old and toothless, drank human milk and lived over a hundred years. He often felt grateful to Wang Ling, and after his mother's death, he served Wang Ling's wife, attending to her day and night as if she were his mother. [47]

    唐德宗朝有阳城者，华阴人也。其弟域。兄弟雍睦，坐卧相随，皆不娶妻。朝廷以谏议大夫征起。性嗜酒，常枕以江石，每用质于酒家，有得三数斛者。料钱入室，即复赎之。
    　　唐文宗朝宰相路随，志行清俭，常闭门不见宾客。状貌或似其先人，以此未尝视镜。又感其父没蕃，终身不肯西坐，其寝西首。
    　　晋桓玄贪秽，金玉不离其手。
    　　吴隐之兄坦之，葬母设祭，每祭，恸绝，至第七祭，呕血而死。
    　　要离羸瘦极，每出，遇顺风即行，逆风即倒。
    　　汉成帝赵飞燕身轻，能为掌上舞。
    　　唐高开道，箭在脑中，使医凿骨取出镞，与客饮酒，谈笑如常。
    　　唐天后朝，宰相娄师德温恭谨慎，未尝与人有毫发之隙。弟授代州刺史，临行戒曰：“吾甚爱汝，慎勿与人相竞。”弟答曰：“人唾面，亦拭之而去。”兄曰：“只此不可。凡唾汝面者，其人怒也。拭之，是逆其心。何不待其自乾”其于保身远害，皆如此类也。 [48]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [48]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 華陰人也 - Indicates that Yang Cheng is from Huayin, a place name in Shaanxi Province.
    • 坐臥相隨 - Means 'sit and sleep together,' indicating close sibling relationship.
    • 皆不娶妻 - Means 'both did not marry,' highlighting their unique lifestyle.
    • 性嗜酒 - Means 'has a strong liking for alcohol.'
    • 常枕以江石 - Means 'often uses a stone from the Yangtze River as a pillow,' suggesting a habit.
    • 每用質於酒家 - Means 'often uses it as collateral for alcohol,' indicating a practice.
    • 料錢入室 - Means 'when the salary comes in,' referring to the time when he receives his pay.
    • 即復贖之 - Means 'he immediately redeems it,' indicating a consistent action.
    • 志行清儉 - Means 'has a clear and frugal character.'
    • 常閉門不見賓客 - Means 'often keeps the door closed and does not see guests,' suggesting a reclusive lifestyle.
    • 狀貌或似其先人 - Means 'appearance is similar to his ancestors,' indicating a resemblance.
    • 以此未嘗視鏡 - Means 'for this reason, he never looks in a mirror,' suggesting a lack of vanity.
    • 感其父沒蕃 - Means 'because his father died in a foreign land,' indicating a personal loss.
    • 終身不肯西坐 - Means 'never sits facing west for his entire life,' indicating a ritual practice.
    • 其寢西首 - Means 'he sleeps with his head facing west,' suggesting a specific sleeping arrangement.
    • 晉桓玄貪穢 - Means 'Jin's Emperor Huan Xuan was greedy and corrupt.'
    • 金玉不離其手 - Means 'gold and jade were never far from his hands,' indicating wealth and luxury.
    • 吳隱之兄坦之 - Refers to Wu Yin's brother Tan Zhi.
    • 葬母設祭 - Means 'held a funeral and set up a memorial service for his mother.'
    • 每祭，慟絕 - Means 'each time he offered sacrifices, he wept until he fainted,' indicating deep grief.
    • 至第七祭，嘔血而死 - Means 'on the seventh sacrifice, he vomited blood and died,' indicating extreme sorrow.
    • 要離羸瘦極 - Means 'Yao Li was extremely thin and weak.'
    • 每出，遇順風即行，逆風即倒 - Means 'when he went out, if the wind was favorable, he could walk; if the wind was against him, he would fall,' indicating his frailty.
    • 漢成帝趙飛燕身輕 - Means 'Emperor Cheng of Han's Zhao Feiyan was very light.'
    • 能為掌上舞 - Means 'could dance on the palm of her hand,' indicating her grace and lightness.
    • 唐高開道 - Refers to Tang's Emperor Gao Kai Dao.
    • 箭在腦中 - Means 'had an arrow stuck in his head.'
    • 使醫鑿骨取出鋒 - Means 'had a doctor drill into his skull to remove the arrowhead,' indicating a medical procedure.
    • 與客飲酒，談笑如常 - Means 'drank with guests and talked and laughed as usual,' indicating his resilience.
    • 唐天后朝 - Refers to the reign of Empress Wu Zetian.
    • 宰相婁師德溫恭謹慎 - Means 'Prime Minister Lou Shide was warm, respectful, and cautious.'
    • 未嘗與人有毫髮之隙 - Means 'never had any minor disputes with anyone,' indicating his peaceful nature.
    • 弟授代州刺史 - Means 'his younger brother was appointed Governor of Dazhou.'
    • 臨行戒曰 - Means 'on the eve of departure, he warned him,' indicating a paternal advice.
    • 吾甚愛汝，慎勿與人相競 - Means 'I love you very much, be careful not to compete with others,' indicating a concern for his brother.
    • 人唾面，亦拭之而去 - Means 'if someone spits on your face, wipe it off and leave,' indicating a response to rudeness.
    • 只此不可 - Means 'this is not acceptable,' indicating a correction.
    • 凡唾汝面者，其人怒也 - Means 'those who spit on your face are angry,' indicating the reason for the action.
    • 拭之，是逆其心 - Means 'wiping it off is against their will,' indicating the consequence.
    • 何不待其自乾 - Means 'why not let it dry naturally,' indicating a better response.
    • 其於保身遠害，皆如此類也 - Means 'in terms of preserving oneself and avoiding harm, all such cases are like this,' indicating a general principle.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 唐德宗朝 - Refers to the reign of Emperor Dezong of Tang.
    • 唐文宗朝 - Refers to the reign of Emperor Wenzong of Tang.
    • 路隨 - Refers to Lu Sui, a prime minister during the Tang dynasty.
    • 吳隱之 - Refers to Wu Yin, a historical figure.
    • 要離 - Refers to Yao Li, a historical figure.
    • 漢成帝 - Refers to Emperor Cheng of Han.
    • 趙飛燕 - Refers to Zhao Feiyan, a famous concubine of Emperor Cheng of Han.
    • 唐高開道 - Refers to Tang's Emperor Gao Kai Dao.
    • 唐天后朝 - Refers to the reign of Empress Wu Zetian.
    • 婁師德 - Refers to Lou Shide, a prime minister during the Tang dynasty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 唐德宗 - Emperor Dezong of Tang.
    • 陽城 - Yang Cheng, a historical figure.
    • 華陰 - A place name in Shaanxi Province.
    • 唐文宗 - Emperor Wenzong of Tang.
    • 路隨 - Lu Sui, a prime minister during the Tang dynasty.
    • 吳隱之 - Wu Yin, a historical figure.
    • 要離 - Yao Li, a historical figure.
    • 漢成帝 - Emperor Cheng of Han.
    • 趙飛燕 - Zhao Feiyan, a famous concubine of Emperor Cheng of Han.
    • 唐高開道 - Tang's Emperor Gao Kai Dao.
    • 唐天后 - Empress Wu Zetian.
    • 婁師德 - Lou Shide, a prime minister during the Tang dynasty.

    During the reign of Emperor Dezong of Tang, there was a man named Yang Cheng from Huayin. His younger brother was named Yu. The brothers were harmonious and often sat and slept together, and neither of them married. The court summoned Yang Cheng to serve as an Advisor to the Imperial Censorate. He had a strong liking for alcohol and often used a stone from the Yangtze River as a pillow. He would use this stone as collateral for alcohol, sometimes obtaining several hundred liters. When his salary came in, he would immediately redeem the stone.

    During the reign of Emperor Wenzong of Tang, the Prime Minister Lu Sui was known for his clear and frugal character. He often kept the door closed and did not see guests. His appearance was similar to that of his ancestors, and for this reason, he never looked in a mirror. Additionally, he was deeply affected by his father's death in a foreign land, and he never sat facing west for his entire life; he slept with his head facing west.

    Emperor Huan Xuan of Jin was known for his greed and corruption, and gold and jade were never far from his hands.

    Wu Yin's brother Tan Zhi held a funeral and set up a memorial service for his mother. Each time he offered sacrifices, he wept until he fainted. On the seventh sacrifice, he vomited blood and died.

    Yao Li was extremely thin and weak. When he went out, if the wind was favorable, he could walk; if the wind was against him, he would fall.

    Emperor Cheng of Han's concubine Zhao Feiyan was very light and could dance on the palm of her hand.

    Tang's Emperor Gao Kai Dao had an arrow stuck in his head. He had a doctor drill into his skull to remove the arrowhead, and he drank with guests and talked and laughed as usual.

    During the reign of Empress Wu Zetian, the Prime Minister Lou Shide was warm, respectful, and cautious. He never had any minor disputes with anyone. His younger brother was appointed Governor of Dazhou, and on the eve of departure, he warned him: "I love you very much, be careful not to compete with others." His younger brother replied: "If someone spits on your face, wipe it off and leave." Lou Shide said: "This is not acceptable. Those who spit on your face are angry. Wiping it off is against their will. Why not let it dry naturally?" In terms of preserving oneself and avoiding harm, all such cases are like this. [48]

    苍梧王酷暴好杀，尝自持刀槊行，见人即击刺死之。若一日不杀人，即惨而不乐。
    　　何宴常服妇人之衣。
    　　晋陆云，字士龙。家在吴，久不得家信。有犬黄耳，云摩其背，谓曰：“与吾达一书至家，得否”其犬即摇尾，因以竹筒盛书，置之犬项。旬日达家，得报而还。
    　　陈正为太官，进炙，有发贯炙。光武令斩正，正曰：“臣有三罪，请言毕而后死。”曰：“山出炭，炎焰不能焦发，臣罪一也；匣出佩刀，日砥砺，不能断发，臣罪二也；臣与庖人六目同视之，曾不如黄门两目，臣罪三也。”光武乃罪黄门而释正。
    　　汉武帝自甘泉至渭桥，有女浴于渭水者，乳长七尺。上怪问之，答曰：“后第七车当知我。”时侍中张宽在第七车，使问之，宽曰：“祭天星，斋不严，即此女见。” [49]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [49]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 酷暴好殺 - Compound adjective meaning 'cruel and fond of killing'.
    • 自持刀槊行 - Literally 'carrying a sword and spear himself', implying personal and direct involvement in violence.
    • 見人即擊刺死之 - Means 'sees a person and immediately strikes and kills them', indicating a habitual and immediate act of violence.
    • 若一日不杀人，即惨而不乐 - Conditional clause meaning 'if he does not kill anyone in a day, he becomes sad and unhappy'.
    • 竹筒盛書 - A bamboo tube containing a letter, describing the method of delivery.
    • 置之犬項 - Put it around the dog's neck, indicating how the letter is attached.
    • 旬日達家 - Within ten days, the letter reaches home.
    • 匣出佩刀，日砥砺，不能斷發 - Another metaphorical explanation, suggesting that a sword kept in a sheath and sharpened daily cannot cut hair.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 蒼梧王 - Refers to a historical figure, likely a king or ruler.
    • 何宴 - A historical figure, possibly a courtier or official.
    • 晉陸雲 - 'Jin Lu Yun', a historical figure from the Jin Dynasty.
    • 黃耳 - 'Huang'er', a named dog with a unique ability.
    • 陳正 - 'Chen Zheng', a historical figure serving in the imperial kitchen.
    • 光武 - 'Emperor Guangwu', a historical figure, likely referring to Emperor Guangwu of the Eastern Han Dynasty.
    • 漢武帝 - 'Emperor Wu of Han', a historical figure, likely referring to Emperor Wu of the Han Dynasty.
    • 甘泉 - 'Ganquan', a historical place, likely a palace or temple.
    • 渭橋 - 'Weiqiao', a historical place, likely a bridge over the Wei River.
    • 侍中張寬 - 'Zhang Kuan, an attendant in the imperial carriage', a historical figure.

    Proper Nouns

    • 蒼梧王 - A historical figure, likely a king or ruler.
    • 何宴 - A historical figure, possibly a courtier or official.
    • 晉陸雲 - 'Jin Lu Yun', a historical figure from the Jin Dynasty.
    • 黃耳 - 'Huang'er', a named dog with a unique ability.
    • 陳正 - 'Chen Zheng', a historical figure serving in the imperial kitchen.
    • 光武 - 'Emperor Guangwu', a historical figure, likely referring to Emperor Guangwu of the Eastern Han Dynasty.
    • 漢武帝 - 'Emperor Wu of Han', a historical figure, likely referring to Emperor Wu of the Han Dynasty.
    • 甘泉 - 'Ganquan', a historical place, likely a palace or temple.
    • 渭橋 - 'Weiqiao', a historical place, likely a bridge over the Wei River.
    • 侍中張寬 - 'Zhang Kuan, an attendant in the imperial carriage', a historical figure.

    The Cruel King of Cangwu

    The King of Cangwu was cruel and fond of killing. He would often carry a sword and spear with him, and upon seeing anyone, he would immediately strike and kill them. If he did not kill anyone in a day, he would become sad and unhappy.

    He Yan often wore women's clothing.

    Jin Lu Yun, courtesy name Shilong, had his home in Wu, and he had not received any letters from home for a long time. He had a dog named Huang'er. Lu Yun patted the dog's back and said, "Can you deliver a letter to my home for me?" The dog wagged its tail, and Lu Yun placed the letter in a bamboo tube and put it around the dog's neck. Within ten days, the letter reached home, and he received a reply.

    Chen Zheng served as a high-ranking official in the imperial kitchen. He presented grilled meat, but there was hair through it. Emperor Guangwu ordered Chen Zheng to be beheaded. Chen Zheng said, "I have three crimes, please let me speak them all before I die." He continued, "Charcoal from the mountains cannot burn hair, that is my first crime; a sword kept in a sheath and sharpened daily cannot cut hair, that is my second crime; I and the cook looked at it with six eyes, yet it was not even noticed by the eunuchs' two eyes, that is my third crime." Emperor Guangwu then punished the eunuchs and released Chen Zheng.

    Emperor Wu of Han traveled from Ganquan to Weiqiao. There was a woman bathing in the Wei River who had breasts that were seven feet long. The emperor found this strange and asked about it. She replied, "The person in the seventh carriage should know me." At that time, Zhang Kuan, an attendant in the imperial carriage, was in the seventh carriage. The emperor sent someone to ask Zhang Kuan, who said, "The ritual for worshipping the stars was not performed with proper solemnity, and that is why this woman appeared." [49]

    梁沈约家书藏十二万卷，然心僻恶，闻人一善，如万箭攒心。
    　　唐富人王元宝，玄宗问其家财多少，对曰：“臣请以一缣系陛下南山一树，南山树尽，臣缣未穷。”时人谓钱为王老，以有元宝字也。
    　　玄宗御含元殿，望南山，见一白龙横亘山上，问左右，曰：“不见。”急召元宝。问之，元宝曰：“见一白物横在山顶，不辨于状。”左右贵人启曰：“何臣等不见，元宝独见之也”帝曰：“我闻至富敌至贵。朕天下之主，而元宝天下之富，故耳。”
    　　玄宗幸蜀之时，至东泰山，内臣高力士拢马请下，东北陈四拜，奏曰：“陛下出幸忽遽，不得亲辞九庙。此山最高，可望秦中。”玄宗悲感恸极，左右不胜哀咽。
    　　陆贾得南越王赵佗所赠，橐中装万金。归分五男，各令散居。而贾携侍儿竟乐游于五子之家，每止十日，极其滋味承奉，其玉剑珠宝，随身皆赐之，虽非训导，亦为达见。 [50]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [50]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 家书藏十二万卷 - Refers to a vast personal library.
    • 心僻恶 - Compound term meaning 'a perverse heart' or 'a heart inclined to evil.'
    • 闻人一善，如万箭攒心 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'to feel as if a thousand arrows are piercing the heart when one hears of someone's goodness,' indicating extreme jealousy or resentment.
    • 臣请以一缣系陛下南山一树 - Hyperbolic expression meaning 'I beg to tie one piece of silk to one tree on the southern mountain,' used to exaggerate wealth.
    • 至富敌至贵 - Means 'extreme wealth rivals extreme nobility,' suggesting that wealth can match the status of high-ranking officials.
    • 竟乐游于五子之家 - Means 'to enjoy leisurely visits to the homes of his five sons,' indicating a life of luxury and indulgence.
    • 极其滋味承奉 - Means 'to savor every detail of the service,' emphasizing the luxurious lifestyle.
    • 虽非训导，亦为达见 - Means 'although not a formal teaching, it is also a wise insight,' suggesting practical wisdom.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 梁沈约 - Refers to Shen Yue, a prominent literary figure of the Liang dynasty.
    • 唐富人王元宝 - Refers to Wang Yuanbao, a wealthy merchant during the Tang dynasty.
    • 玄宗 - Refers to Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, a significant ruler known for his cultural patronage.
    • 东泰山 - Refers to Mount Dong, a location where Emperor Xuanzong sought refuge during the An Lushan Rebellion.
    • 高力士 - Refers to Gao Lishi, a trusted eunuch and minister during the Tang dynasty.
    • 九庙 - Refers to the ancestral temples, symbolizing the imperial lineage and heritage.
    • 南越王赵佗 - Refers to Zhao Tuo, the first ruler of Nanyue, a kingdom in southern China.

    Proper Nouns

    • 梁沈约 - Shen Yue, a literary figure of the Liang dynasty.
    • 唐富人王元宝 - Wang Yuanbao, a wealthy merchant of the Tang dynasty.
    • 玄宗 - Emperor Xuanzong of Tang.
    • 东泰山 - Mount Dong.
    • 高力士 - Gao Lishi, a trusted eunuch and minister.
    • 九庙 - Ancestral temples.
    • 南越王赵佗 - Zhao Tuo, the first ruler of Nanyue.

    Shen Yue, a literary figure of the Liang dynasty, had a vast collection of twelve thousand scrolls of family letters, but his heart was perverse. When he heard of someone's goodness, it felt as if a thousand arrows were piercing his heart.

    Wang Yuanbao, a wealthy merchant of the Tang dynasty, was asked by Emperor Xuanzong about his wealth. He replied, "I beg to tie one piece of silk to one tree on the southern mountain; even when all the trees are tied, my wealth will not be exhausted." People at the time called money "Wang Lao" because of the character "元" (yuan) in his name.

    Emperor Xuanzong, while on the Hall of Containing the Heavenly Light, looked at the southern mountain and saw a white dragon stretching across the mountain. He asked his attendants if they saw it, and they said no. He then urgently summoned Wang Yuanbao and asked him. Wang Yuanbao said, "I saw a white object stretching across the top of the mountain, but I couldn't determine what it was." The imperial attendants then said, "Why don't we see it, but Wang Yuanbao does?" The emperor replied, "I have heard that extreme wealth rivals extreme nobility. I am the ruler of the empire, and Wang Yuanbao is the wealthiest man in the empire."

    During Emperor Xuanzong's journey to Sichuan, he reached Mount Dong. The eunuch Gao Lishi pulled the emperor's horse and asked him to dismount. Gao Lishi then bowed four times to the northeast and said, "Your Majesty, you left in haste and did not have the chance to personally bid farewell to the ancestral temples. This mountain is the highest, and from here you can see the region of Qin." Emperor Xuanzong was deeply moved and felt an overwhelming sense of sorrow, and his attendants could not help but weep.

    Lu Jia, after receiving a large sum of money from Zhao Tuo, the first ruler of Nanyue, divided the wealth among his five sons and instructed them to live separately. However, Lu Jia himself enjoyed leisurely visits to the homes of his five sons, staying for ten days each time and savoring every detail of the service. He also gave his jade swords and jewelry to his sons, and although this was not formal teaching, it was also a wise insight. [50]

    魏鲍子都暮行于野，见书生卒然心痛，下马为摩其心。有顷，书生卒。子都视其囊中，有素书一帙、金十饼。乃卖二饼，葬书生。其余枕之项下，置素书腹上，而退。其后数年，子都行，有一骏马逐之。既而有认马者，谓子都为盗，因问儿所在。子都具言。于是相随往开墓，取其儿归葬，金八饼在项下，素书在腹上。举家诣官，称子都之德。由是子都声名大振。
    　　魏毛，字孝先。为尚书，人无敢以好衣食见者。武帝叹曰：“吾不及毛尚书能移风俗”。
    　　窦婴征七国时，得赐千金。置之庑下，任人所取，不入私室。
    　　宋昭王出亡，谓其御者曰：“吾知所以亡者。”御者曰：“何以知之”昭王曰：“吾被服而立，左右皆曰：‘君丽者也’；发言举事，左右皆曰：‘君圣者也。’吾内外不见其过，安得不亡乎”于是改行易操，后三年，美行于宋，宋人迎之，复位，谥曰昭。 [51]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [51]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 卒然心痛 - Sudden heartache, describing the scholar's sudden illness.
    • 摩其心 - Massaged his heart, indicating the act of trying to relieve the scholar's pain.
    • 卒 - Died, marking the end of the scholar's life.
    • 卖二饼 - Sold two pieces, indicating the action of selling part of the gold.
    • 葬书生 - Buried the scholar, showing the act of giving the scholar a proper burial.
    • 其余枕之项下 - The rest were placed under his neck, indicating the placement of the remaining gold.
    • 置素书腹上 - Placed the scroll on his abdomen, showing the careful handling of the scholar's possessions.
    • 骏马逐之 - A fine horse chased him, describing the unexpected event.
    • 认马者 - The person who recognized the horse, indicating the horse's owner.
    • 称子都之德 - Praised Zidu's virtue, highlighting the positive impact of Zidu's actions.
    • 声名大振 - His reputation greatly increased, showing the result of the praise.
    • 好衣食见者 - Those who come to see with good clothes and food, indicating people who come to visit with gifts.
    • 征七国 - Campaign against the Seven States, referring to a significant military campaign.
    • 赐千金 - Was granted a thousand gold pieces, indicating a large reward.
    • 置之庑下 - Placed them under the eaves, showing where the gold was stored.
    • 任人所取 - Allowed people to take what they wanted, indicating the distribution of the gold.
    • 不入私室 - Did not enter his private room, showing the transparency of his actions.
    • 内外不见其过 - Neither inside nor outside did he see his faults, indicating a lack of self-awareness.
    • 安得不亡乎 - How could he not perish, a rhetorical question leading to a realization.
    • 改行易操 - Changed his behavior and manner, showing the king's transformation.
    • 美行于宋 - Became renowned for his good deeds in Song, indicating the positive changes.
    • 谥曰昭 - Posthumously given the title Zhao, showing the honor bestowed upon him.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 魏鲍子都 - Refers to Bao Zidu, a historical figure from the Wei dynasty.
    • 魏毛孝先 - Refers to Mao Xiaoxian, a historical figure from the Wei dynasty.
    • 尚书 - Refers to a high-ranking official in the imperial court.
    • 武帝 - Refers to Emperor Wu of the Han dynasty, a significant historical figure.
    • 窦婴 - Refers to Dou Ying, a historical figure who played a key role in the campaign against the Seven States.
    • 宋昭王 - Refers to King Zhao of Song, a historical figure who went into exile and later returned to power.

    Proper Nouns

    • 魏鲍子都 - Bao Zidu, a historical figure from the Wei dynasty.
    • 魏毛孝先 - Mao Xiaoxian, a historical figure from the Wei dynasty.
    • 武帝 - Emperor Wu of the Han dynasty.
    • 窦婴 - Dou Ying, a historical figure.
    • 宋昭王 - King Zhao of Song, a historical figure.

    During the twilight hours, Bao Zidu of the Wei dynasty was walking in the wilderness when he encountered a scholar who suddenly fell ill with a heartache. Bao Zidu dismounted his horse and massaged the scholar's chest. After a short while, the scholar passed away. Bao Zidu then examined the scholar's bag and found within it a scroll of white paper and ten pieces of gold. He sold two pieces of gold and buried the scholar. The remaining gold was placed under the scholar's neck, and the scroll was laid on his abdomen before Bao Zidu departed. Several years later, as Bao Zidu was traveling, he was chased by a fine horse. Soon after, the owner of the horse recognized it and accused Bao Zidu of theft, asking about the whereabouts of his child. Bao Zidu explained everything. They then accompanied him to the grave, where they found the child, along with eight pieces of gold placed under his neck and the scroll on his abdomen. The entire family went to the authorities to commend Bao Zidu's virtue. As a result, Bao Zidu's reputation grew significantly.

    Mao Xiaoxian of the Wei dynasty, whose courtesy name was Xiaoxian, served as a high-ranking official known as a Shangshu. No one dared to visit him with gifts of fine clothes and food. Emperor Wu of the Han dynasty remarked, "I am not as capable as Shangshu Mao Xiaoxian in changing the customs of the people."

    When Dou Ying of the Han dynasty was involved in the campaign against the Seven States, he was granted a thousand pieces of gold. He placed them under the eaves and allowed people to take what they wanted, without bringing them into his private quarters.

    King Zhao of Song went into exile and told his charioteer, "I know why I have perished." The charioteer asked, "How do you know this?" King Zhao replied, "When I stood dressed in fine clothes, those around me said, 'You are beautiful.' When I spoke and acted, those around me said, 'You are wise.' Neither inside nor outside did I see my faults—how could I not perish?" Realizing his mistakes, he changed his behavior and manner. Three years later, he became renowned for his good deeds in Song, and the people of Song welcomed him back to power, posthumously giving him the title Zhao. [51]

    晋王戎，字仲。性鄙吝。家有绿李，子熟时惠人，必钻破其核，恐他人种植之。
    　　王澄出为荆州刺史，送者盈路，见路傍树有一鹊巢，乃自解衣上树，探弄鹊雏，傍若无人。
    　　王右军，永和九年曲水会，用鼠须笔蚕茧纸为《兰亭记叙》，平生之札，最为得意。其后虽书数百本，无一得及者。太宗令御史萧翼密购得之，爵赏之外，别费亿万。太宗临崩，谓高宗曰：“以《兰亭》殉吾，孝也。”遂随梓宫入陵。
    　　汉张骞奉使大月氐，往返一亿三万里，得葡萄、涂林、安石榴，植之于中国。
    　　汉和忆为畿令，常有一虎害人，亿令设槛，得二虎。亿曰：“害人者低头。”一虎低头，亿杀之，其一虎放去。自是猛兽皆出境，吏人以为神君。
    　　后汉郑玄居山东，有疑，莫知所问，遂往入关诣马融。三年不得见。一日融大会，遂见之。登楼问其疑，数十段皆决。语毕，遂归。融谓门人曰：“玄既归，吾道东矣。” [52]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [52]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 性鄙吝 - Means 'frugal to the point of stinginess' or 'mean-spirited.' Describes a person's character as being excessively frugal or miserly.
    • 必钻破其核 - Literally means 'must drill through its core.' Metaphorical expression indicating extreme caution to prevent others from planting the seeds.
    • 傍若无人 - Means 'as if no one were around,' describing a state of being completely oblivious to one's surroundings.
    • 用鼠须笔蚕茧纸 - Refers to using mouse whisker brushes and silk cocoon paper, which were high-quality materials for writing.
    • 平生之札，最为得意 - Means 'the best work of his life,' emphasizing the significance of the *Lanting* (Orchid Pavilion) inscription.
    • 爵赏之外，别费亿万 - Means 'beyond the titles and rewards, additional costs in the millions,' indicating the immense value placed on the *Lanting* (Orchid Pavilion) inscription.
    • 以《兰亭》殉吾，孝也 - Means 'to bury the *Lanting* (Orchid Pavilion) with me, is filial piety,' expressing the emperor's wish to be buried with the *Lanting* (Orchid Pavilion) inscription.
    • 得葡萄、涂林、安石榴 - Refers to obtaining grapes, *tulin* (likely a type of fruit), and pomegranates, which were introduced to China from the West.
    • 害人者低头 - Means 'the one that harms people will lower its head,' indicating that the more aggressive tiger will submit to authority.
    • 吾道东矣 - Means 'my way has spread to the east,' suggesting that Vajrayana Buddhism (or another form of Buddhism) has spread to the east.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋王戎 - Refers to Wang Rong, a prominent figure during the Western Jin Dynasty.
    • 王澄 - Refers to Wang Cheng, a scholar and official during the Western Jin Dynasty.
    • 王右军 - Refers to Wang Xizhi, known as the 'Sage of Calligraphy,' who lived during the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 永和九年 - Refers to the year 353 CE, when the famous *Lanting* (Orchid Pavilion) gathering took place.
    • 鼠须笔蚕茧纸 - Refers to high-quality writing materials: mouse whisker brushes and silk cocoon paper.
    • 《兰亭记叙》 - Refers to the *Lanting* (Orchid Pavilion) inscription, a famous calligraphy work by Wang Xizhi.
    • 太宗 - Refers to Emperor Taizong of Tang, who reigned from 626 to 649 CE.
    • 汉张骞 - Refers to Zhang Qian, a Han Dynasty diplomat and explorer.
    • 大月氐 - Refers to the Great Yuezhi, an ancient nomadic people.
    • 涂林 - Likely refers to a type of fruit, possibly a variant of pomegranate or another fruit.
    • 汉和忆 - Refers to He Yi, a figure from the Han Dynasty.
    • 畿令 - Refers to a local official responsible for maintaining order.
    • 害人者低头 - Refers to a method of distinguishing between two tigers, one of which was known to harm people.
    • 郑玄 - Refers to Zheng Xuan, a renowned scholar of the Eastern Han Dynasty.
    • 马融 - Refers to Ma Rong, another prominent scholar of the Eastern Han Dynasty.
    • 先达 - Refers to earlier accomplished figures or predecessors who can be learned from.
    • 可为师表 - Means 'can serve as a teacher and model,' stressing exemplary status.
    • 博学求之 - Means to seek knowledge broadly from many sources.
    • 吾道东矣 - Suggests that Vajrayana Buddhism (or another form of Buddhism) has spread to the east.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋王戎 - Wang Rong, a prominent figure during the Western Jin Dynasty.
    • 王澄 - Wang Cheng, a scholar and official during the Western Jin Dynasty.
    • 王右军 - Wang Xizhi, known as the 'Sage of Calligraphy,' who lived during the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 永和九年 - The year 353 CE, when the famous *Lanting* (Orchid Pavilion) gathering took place.
    • 鼠须笔蚕茧纸 - High-quality writing materials: mouse whisker brushes and silk cocoon paper.
    • 《兰亭记叙》 - The *Lanting* (Orchid Pavilion) inscription, a famous calligraphy work by Wang Xizhi.
    • 太宗 - Emperor Taizong of Tang, who reigned from 626 to 649 CE.
    • 汉张骞 - Zhang Qian, a Han Dynasty diplomat and explorer.
    • 大月氐 - The Great Yuezhi, an ancient nomadic people.
    • 涂林 - Likely a type of fruit, possibly a variant of pomegranate or another fruit.
    • 汉和忆 - He Yi, a figure from the Han Dynasty.
    • 畿令 - A local official responsible for maintaining order.
    • 害人者低头 - A method of distinguishing between two tigers, one of which was known to harm people.
    • 郑玄 - Zheng Xuan, a renowned scholar of the Eastern Han Dynasty.
    • 马融 - Ma Rong, another prominent scholar of the Eastern Han Dynasty.
    • 先达 - Earlier accomplished figures or predecessors who can be learned from.
    • 可为师表 - Can serve as a teacher and model.
    • 博学求之 - To seek knowledge broadly from many sources.
    • 吾道东矣 - Suggests that Vajrayana Buddhism (or another form of Buddhism) has spread to the east.

    晋王戎，字仲。性鄙吝。家有绿李，子熟时惠人，必钻破其核，恐他人种植之。
    　　王澄出为荆州刺史，送者盈路，见路傍树有一鹊巢，乃自解衣上树，探弄鹊雏，傍若无人。
    　　王右军，永和九年曲水会，用鼠须笔蚕茧纸为《兰亭记叙》，平生之札，最为得意。其后虽书数百本，无一得及者。太宗令御史萧翼密购得之，爵赏之外，别费亿万。太宗临崩，谓高宗曰：“以《兰亭》殉吾，孝也。”遂随梓宫入陵。
    　　汉张骞奉使大月氐，往返一亿三万里，得葡萄、涂林、安石榴，植之于中国。
    　　汉和忆为畿令，常有一虎害人，亿令设槛，得二虎。亿曰：“害人者低头。”一虎低头，亿杀之，其一虎放去。自是猛兽皆出境，吏人以为神君。
    　　后汉郑玄居山东，有疑，莫知所问，遂往入关诣马融。三年不得见。一日融大会，遂见之。登楼问其疑，数十段皆决。语毕，遂归。融谓门人曰：“玄既归，吾道东矣。”

    晋王戎，字仲。他生性吝啬。家中有绿李子，当果子熟了的时候分给他人，必定要钻破果核，担心别人会拿去种植。
    　　王澄出任荆州刺史时，送行的人挤满了道路，他看到路边的树上有一个喜鹊巢，便自己脱下衣服爬上树，玩弄喜鹊雏鸟，旁若无人。
    　　王右军在永和九年（353年）的曲水宴会上，用鼠须笔和蚕茧纸书写了《兰亭记叙》，这是他一生中最得意的作品。之后虽然写了数百本，没有一本能及得上原作。太宗命令御史萧翼秘密购得真迹，除了封赏之外，还花费了数亿。太宗临终时对高宗说：“将《兰亭》与我一同埋葬，这是孝道。”于是《兰亭》随太宗的棺椁一同入葬。
    　　汉朝的张骞奉命出使大月氏，往返行程达一亿三万里，带回了葡萄、涂林（一种水果）和安石榴，并在中国种植。
    　　汉朝的和忆担任畿令时，常有一只老虎伤害人，和忆下令设置陷阱，捕获了两只老虎。和忆说：“伤害人的那只会低下头。”其中一只老虎低下了头，和忆杀了它，另一只老虎被放走。从此之后，猛兽都离开了边境，官吏和百姓认为和忆是神明。
    　　后汉的郑玄住在山东，遇到疑难问题，不知道向谁请教，于是前往关中拜访马融。三年未能见到马融。一天马融举行大会，郑玄终于得以相见。郑玄登楼询问自己的疑惑，马融解答了数十个问题。说完后，郑玄便回家了。马融对门人说：“郑玄既然已经归来，我的学问已经传到了东方。” [52]

    晋索报兄之仇，手杀四十人。
    　　鲁公仪休为相，归见其妻织，乃焚机而出，谓其妻曰：“吾为相食禄，今尔夺百姓之利，使民安归哉”
    　　韩康伯隐药肆，卖价无二。有二女子买药，不识康伯，乃酬酢之，康伯不移，女子曰：“君何若康伯无二价也！”康伯乃逃去，不知所在。
    　　高丽国王侍婢立王左右，一旦，有气自天而下，大如鸡子，入其口。十月孕一男，名曰“东明”，善射。王恐为国害，欲杀之。东明走，弯弓射水，鱼鳖浮出而为梁，以渡东明。
    　　魏武帝尝居铜雀台，及终，令妓乐登台望西陵而歌舞。
    　　郑子华之弟子臧，好聚鹬毛为冠，郑伯闻而恶甚，使盗诱杀之。君子曰：“服之不衷，身之灾，以其非法服也。”
    　　晋大医司马程据上武帝雉头裘。诏曰：“此裘非当服，损费功用。”遂命火，于殿前焚之。 [53]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [53]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 晋索报兄之仇 - Elliptical phrase; Jìn Suǒ Mǐ avenged his brother's death by personally killing forty people.
    • 手杀四十人 - Literally means 'killed forty people with his hands,' emphasizing personal and direct action.
    • 归见其妻织，乃焚机而出 - Ambiguous phrase; on returning home, he saw his wife weaving, and then burned the loom and left.
    • 吾为相食禄，今尔夺百姓之利，使民安归哉 - Elliptical phrase; I receive a salary as chancellor, but now you are taking away the people's benefits, so where will the people return to?
    • 隐药肆，卖价无二 - Elliptical phrase; He lived incognito in a pharmacy, selling goods at a consistent price.
    • 君何若康伯无二价也 - Elliptical phrase; How can you be like Kang Bo with an unchanged price?
    • 东明走，弯弓射水，鱼鳖浮出而为梁，以渡东明 - Elliptical phrase; Dongming ran away, drew his bow and shot at the water, causing fish and turtles to float out and form a bridge, allowing Dongming to cross.
    • 魏武帝尝居铜雀台，及终，令妓乐登台望西陵而歌舞 - Elliptical phrase; Emperor Wei Wu once stayed at the Copper Sparrow Tower, and upon his death, he ordered musicians and dancers to climb the tower and look toward the Western Tomb while dancing and singing.
    • 郑子华之弟子臧，好聚鹬毛为冠，郑伯闻而恶甚，使盗诱杀之 - Elliptical phrase; Zeng Zha, a disciple of Zheng Zihua, liked to collect heron feathers to make hats. Zheng Bo heard about this and detested it greatly, so he sent a thief to lure and kill him.
    • 晋大医司马程据上武帝雉头裘，诏曰，此裘非当服，损费功用，遂命火，于殿前焚之 - Elliptical phrase; The prominent physician Sima Chengju presented a pheasant head fur coat to Emperor Wu, who issued an edict saying, 'This fur coat is not appropriate to wear and wastes usefulness, so I order it to be burned in front of the palace.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋索 - Historical figure from the Jin dynasty, known for avenging his brother's death.
    • 公仪休 - Historical figure from the Spring and Autumn period, known for his integrity and self-discipline.
    • 韩康伯 - Historical figure known for his honesty and consistency in business.
    • 东明 - Legendary figure in Korean mythology, known for his escape from the king and his miraculous journey.
    • 魏武帝 - Cao Cao, a prominent warlord and statesman of the late Eastern Han dynasty.
    • 郑子华 - Historical figure, and his disciple is **弟子臧**.
    • 司马程据 - Historical figure, and **武帝** is the emperor of the Jin dynasty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋索 - Historical figure from the Jin dynasty.
    • 公仪休 - Historical figure from the Spring and Autumn period.
    • 韩康伯 - Historical figure known for his honesty and consistency in business.
    • 东明 - Legendary figure in Korean mythology.
    • 魏武帝 - Cao Cao, a prominent warlord and statesman of the late Eastern Han dynasty.
    • 郑子华 - Historical figure.
    • 司马程据 - Historical figure, and **武帝** is the emperor of the Jin dynasty.
    • 铜雀台 - A famous tower.
    • 西陵 - Western Tomb.
    • 雉头裘 - Pheasant head fur coat.

    Jìn Suǒ Mǐ avenged his brother's death by personally killing forty people.
    When Lǔ Gōngyí Huī served as chancellor, upon returning home, he saw his wife weaving. He then burned the loom and left, telling his wife, "I receive a salary as chancellor, but now you are taking away the people's benefits. Where will the people return to?"
    Hán Kāngbó lived incognito in a pharmacy, selling goods at a consistent price. Two women came to buy medicine but did not recognize him, so they haggled with him. Kangbó remained firm, and the women said, "How can you be like Kangbó with an unchanged price?" Kangbó then fled and was never seen again.
    In Gāolí, the king's servant girl stood by the king's side. One day, a cloud descended from the sky, large as an egg, and entered her mouth. She conceived a son after ten months and named him "Dōngmíng," who was skilled in archery. The king feared he would be a threat to the kingdom and wanted to kill him. Dōngmíng fled, drew his bow, and shot at the water. Fish and turtles floated out and formed a bridge, allowing Dōngmíng to cross.
    Emperor Wèi Wǔ once stayed at the Copper Sparrow Tower. Upon his death, he ordered musicians and dancers to climb the tower and look toward the Western Tomb while dancing and singing.
    Zhèng Zihua's disciple, Zēng Zhā, liked to collect heron feathers to make hats. Zhèng Bó heard about this and detested it greatly, so he sent a thief to lure and kill him. A gentleman said, "Wearing something inappropriate is a disaster for oneself, as it is not legal or proper attire."
    The prominent physician Sima Chengju presented a pheasant head fur coat to Emperor Wǔ. The emperor issued an edict saying, "This fur coat is not appropriate to wear and wastes usefulness." He then ordered it to be burned in front of the palace. [53]

    陶潜在家，每酒熟，即以头上葛巾漉酒，毕，复裹之。
    　　晋羊秀，字稚舒。家富豪，秋冬月造酒，令人抱瓮，须臾，易之，有顷便可熟。
    　　汉文帝俭约，常集谏书囊而为帐。所幸姬慎夫人，衣不曳地。
    　　晋庚衮，字叔褒。父在常戒衮以酒。及父殁，日饮不止。因责曰：“余废先人之戒，何以训人”乃携挺于墓前，自杖三十。
    　　晋明帝十余岁，未为太子，元帝坐之膝上，问曰：“日与长安孰近”答曰：“日近。”复问之：“何言日近”答曰：“举头见日，不见长安。”帝异之，明日对群臣，复问之，答曰：“日远，长安近。”元帝甚惊，问曰：“何以与昨日之对有异”复答曰：“只闻人从长安来，不闻人从日边来。”帝愈奇之，立为太子。
    　　管辂年七八岁时，与邻里小儿戏，画地为日月星辰之状，言动不常。父母禁之，答曰：“家鸡、野鹄尚知天时，况人乎哉！” [54]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [54]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 每酒熟 - Whenever the wine is ready, a habitual expression.
    • 以頭上葛巾漉酒 - Use the hemp cap on his head to filter the wine, a literal but idiomatic description of a unique practice.
    • 畢，復裹之 - Afterwards, he puts it back on his head, indicating a repeated action.
    • 常集諫書囊而為帳 - He often collected admonitions and used them to make a screen or curtain, a metaphor for frugality and resourcefulness.
    • 孰近 - Which is closer, a comparative question.
    • 舉頭見日，不見長安 - When you look up, you see the sun, but not长安, a literal yet idiomatic response.
    • 畫地為日月星辰之狀 - Drew the shapes of the sun, moon, and stars on the ground, a vivid description of a child's imaginative play.
    • 言動不常 - His words and actions were unusual, indicating a precocious or eccentric nature.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 陶潛 - Tao Qian (陶潜), a famous poet and recluse from the Eastern Jin dynasty, known for his simplicity and love of nature.
    • 漢文帝 - Emperor Wen of Han (汉文帝), a ruler of the Han dynasty known for his frugality and benevolence.
    • 晉庚衮 - A historical figure from the Jin dynasty, known for his filial piety and struggles with alcoholism.
    • 晉明帝 - Emperor Ming of Jin (晋明帝), a ruler of the Jin dynasty, known for his intelligence and early wisdom.
    • 管辂 - Guan Lu (管辂), a renowned astrologer and scholar from the Three Kingdoms period, known for his precocious intellect.
    • 葛巾 - A hemp cap, often associated with simplicity and rusticity.
    • 諫書 - Admonitions or advice, often given by ministers to the emperor.
    • 太子 - Crown prince, the heir to the throne.
    • 長安 - The ancient capital of China, a symbol of imperial power and grandeur.

    Proper Nouns

    • 陶潛 - Tao Qian (陶潜), a famous poet and recluse.
    • 漢文帝 - Emperor Wen of Han (汉文帝).
    • 晉庚衮 - A historical figure from the Jin dynasty.
    • 晉明帝 - Emperor Ming of Jin (晋明帝).
    • 管辂 - Guan Lu (管辂), a renowned astrologer and scholar.
    • 葛巾 - A hemp cap.
    • 諫書 - Admonitions or advice.
    • 太子 - Crown prince.
    • 長安 - The ancient capital of China.

    Tao Qian, at home, would always filter his wine with the hemp cap on his head whenever it was ready, and after finishing, he would put it back on.
    During the Jin dynasty, Yang Xiù, courtesy name Zhi Shū, was a wealthy man. In the autumn and winter months, he had his servants carry large jars of wine, and within moments, they would replace them with new jars. In a short while, the wine would be ready.
    Emperor Wen of Han was frugal. He often collected admonitions and used them to make a screen or curtain. His favored concubine, Shen Fu Ren, did not let her robes touch the ground.
    During the Jin dynasty, Geng Gǔn, courtesy name Shū Bāo, was frequently warned by his father about drinking. After his father's death, he drank every day without stopping. He then scolded himself, saying, "I have abandoned my father's warnings. How can I teach others?" He took a stick to his grave and beat himself thirty times.
    When Emperor Ming of Jin was over ten years old, before he became crown prince, Emperor Yuan sat him on his knee and asked, "Which is closer, the sun or Chang'an?" He replied, "The sun is closer." The emperor asked again, "Why do you say the sun is closer?" He replied, "When you look up, you see the sun, but not Chang'an." The emperor was amazed and the next day asked him again in front of his ministers. He replied, "The sun is far, but Chang'an is closer." Emperor Yuan was greatly surprised and asked, "Why is your answer different from yesterday?" He replied, "I have only heard of people coming from Chang'an, but not from the side of the sun." The emperor was even more amazed and immediately made him crown prince.
    When Guan Lù was seven or eight years old, he played with the children in his neighborhood, drawing the shapes of the sun, moon, and stars on the ground, and his words and actions were unusual. His parents tried to stop him, but he replied, "Even chickens and wild geese know the seasons, how much more so humans!" [54]

    舜父瞽瞍纳后妻谗言，尝笞舜。舜见小杖则受，大杖则走，故能保身于孝道。
    　　黄霸为颍川太守，召吏。方食于野亭，鸟攫食。霸见吏曰：“汝为鸟攫食耶”吏惊，以为霸尽知其行止，后不敢为非。
    　　秦败，豪杰之士争取金玉，唯任氏子独为仓窖贮粟。后谷石万钱，于是金玉宝货尽归任氏。
    　　何邵字敬祖。日供口食，计二万钱，而兼四方珍味，虽三日帝厨之膳，不及之也。
    　　《吕氏春秋》曰：以隋侯之珠弹千仞之雀，人笑其用重求所轻也。
    　　唐初张公艺九世同居，高宗东封过其家，问之：“何以致然”公艺执笔，唯书百余“忍”字，余无他言。遂旌表其门。
    　　汉高祖既入关，诸将劫珠玉宝货，唯萧何独收秦格式律令。卒为汉名相，功居第一。
    　　阮修，字宣子。居贫，年四十未有室。王敦等敛钱为婚，皆名士也。时慕之者求入一钱不得。 [55]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [55]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 小杖則受，大杖則走 - Idiom meaning to accept minor punishment but avoid severe punishment, implying wisdom in self-preservation.
    • 唯書百餘‘忍’字 - Writing over a hundred 'forbearance' characters, symbolizing the virtue of patience and tolerance.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 瞽瞍 - Emperor Shun's father, known for his blindness and often used as a symbol of moral education.
    • 黄霸 - A famous official of the Han dynasty known for his integrity and administrative skills.
    • 秦 - The Qin dynasty, marking the end of the First Emperor's rule.
    • 任氏子 - Ren Shi's son, known for his foresight and wisdom.
    • 何邵 - A prominent official and scholar of the Western Jin dynasty.
    • 隋侯之珠 - A pearl from the Suo State, symbolizing great value.
    • 张公艺 - Zhang Gongyi, known for his family's longevity and harmony.
    • 汉高祖 - Emperor Gaozu of Han, the founder of the Han dynasty.
    • 萧何 - A key advisor to Emperor Gaozu, known for his administrative reforms.
    • 阮修 - A scholar and official of the Western Jin dynasty, known for his poverty and integrity.
    • 王敦 - A prominent official of the Western Jin dynasty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 瞽瞍 - Emperor Shun's father.
    • 黄霸 - A famous official of the Han dynasty.
    • 秦 - The Qin dynasty.
    • 任氏子 - Ren Shi's son.
    • 何邵 - A prominent official and scholar of the Western Jin dynasty.
    • 隋侯之珠 - A pearl from the Suo State.
    • 张公艺 - Zhang Gongyi.
    • 汉高祖 - Emperor Gaozu of Han.
    • 萧何 - A key advisor to Emperor Gaozu.
    • 阮修 - A scholar and official of the Western Jin dynasty.
    • 王敦 - A prominent official of the Western Jin dynasty.

    Emperor Shun's father, Gushu, listened to the slanderous words of his new wife and often beat Shun. Shun would accept minor punishment but avoid severe punishment, thus preserving his life through filial piety.
    Huang Ba served as the governor of Yingchuan and summoned officials. While eating in a rural pavilion, birds snatched his food. Huang Ba saw the officials and said, "Are you being snatched by birds?" The officials were startled, thinking Huang Ba knew everything about their actions, and later dared not act improperly.
    After the fall of Qin, brave men seized gold and jade, but only Ren Shi's son stored grain in granaries. Later, grain cost ten thousand coins per stone, and all gold, jade, and precious goods ended up with Ren Shi.
    He Shao, courtesy name Jingzu, had a daily food supply costing two thousand coins, and he also enjoyed delicacies from all directions. Even three days of imperial kitchen meals could not match his.
    The Lüshi Chunqiu says: "Using a pearl from the Suo State to shoot at a sparrow thousands of feet high, people laugh at its misuse of value for something trivial."
    In early Tang, Zhang Gongyi lived with nine generations under one roof. Emperor Gaozong passed by his home during his eastern tour and asked him, "Why is this so?" Zhang Gongyi wrote over a hundred "forbearance" characters and said nothing else. His door was honored and commended.
    After Emperor Gaozu of Han entered Guanzhong, generals seized jewels and precious goods, but only Xiao He collected the legal codes and regulations of Qin. He became a famous prime minister of Han and was ranked first in merit.
    Ruan Xiu, courtesy name Xuanzi, lived in poverty and was not married at forty. Wang Dun and others collected money for his marriage, and all were famous scholars. Those who admired him could not even contribute a single coin. [55]

    隋刘君良累代义居，兄弟四人同气。大业末，天下饥馑，其妻欲劝分居，乃窃取庭树中鸟雏置诸窠中，令群鸟斗竞。举家怪之。其妻曰：“今天下大乱，战争之秋，禽鸟尚不相容，况人乎”君良知其计，中夜遂搅妻发，大呼曰：“此乃破家贼！”召诸兄弟，哭以告之，而弃其妻，居虽三院，而共一厨。
    　　唐中宗为天后废于房陵，仰天而叹，因抛一石于云中，心祝之曰：“我为帝，即此石不落。”遂为树枝阁之。至今犹存。又有人渡水拾薪，得一古镜，进之。中宗照面，其影中有人语曰：“即作天子，即作天子。未浃旬，践居帝位。
    　　荀奉倩与妻情厚，冬月，妇病热，奉倩出，露坐，候体冷，即入熨之。甚为世所饥。
    　　玄宗幸蜀，至利州吉伯渡，有一白鱼来御舟而过。
    　　河间王孝恭，才智识略时出于众。初受诏征蒲公┙，上有一器，倏然变成血，满坐惊畏，左右不测。孝恭自省无负神祗，此变应是公┙。时人服其先见。 [56]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [56]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 累代義居 - Living together for generations out of filial piety, emphasizing long-term familial harmony.
    • 大業末 - The end of Emperor Yang of Sui's reign, marking a period of political and social turmoil.
    • 天下饑馑 - Widespread famine across the empire.
    • 其妻欲勸分居 - His wife wanted to persuade them to separate, indicating a desire to break up the family unit.
    • 竊取庭樹中鳥雛置諸窠中 - A metaphorical act where the wife secretly takes bird chicks from a tree and places them in different nests, causing the birds to fight.
    • 舉家怪之 - The whole family found it strange, indicating their confusion over the wife's actions.
    • 破家賊 - The traitor who will ruin the family, used by Liu Junliang to describe his wife's intentions.
    • 三院，而共一廚 - Three separate houses, but sharing one kitchen, indicating a continued sense of unity despite separation.
    • 天下大亂，戰爭之秋 - A time of great chaos and conflict.
    • 禽鳥尚不相容，況人乎 - Even birds cannot coexist during times of turmoil, let alone humans.
    • 中夜遂攪妻發 - At midnight, he pulled out his wife's hair, a dramatic gesture to reveal her treachery.
    • 中宗為天后廢於房陵 - Emperor Zhongzong was deposed by Empress Wu Zetian and exiled to Fangling.
    • 仰天而嘆 - Looked up to heaven and sighed, indicating a moment of reflection and despair.
    • 抛一石於雲中 - Threw a stone into the sky, a symbolic act.
    • 心祝之曰 - Whispered to himself, indicating a private vow or wish.
    • 即此石不落 - If this stone does not fall, a condition tied to his future ascension to the throne.
    • 至今猶存 - Still exists today, indicating the enduring nature of the event.
    • 有人渡水拾薪，得一古鏡 - Someone who crossed the river to gather firewood found an ancient mirror, a seemingly mundane event with significant consequences.
    • 進之 - Presented it, indicating the act of giving the mirror to the emperor.
    • 其影中有人語曰 - There was a voice in the reflection saying, a supernatural or symbolic occurrence.
    • 即作天子，即作天子 - If you become emperor, if you become emperor, a repeated affirmation of his destiny.
    • 未浃旬，践居帝位 - Within a few days, he ascended to the imperial throne, indicating a rapid rise to power.
    • 荀奉倩與妻情厚 - Xun Fengqian had a deep affection for his wife, indicating a strong marital bond.
    • 冬月，婦病熱 - In winter, his wife was ill with a fever, a specific time and condition.
    • 奉倩出，露坐，候體冷，即入熨之 - Xun Fengqian went out, sat in the open air, waited until his wife's body cooled, and then went inside to warm her, a detailed description of his care.
    • 甚為世所饑 - It was greatly admired by the world, indicating the high regard for his actions.
    • 玄宗幸蜀，至利州吉伯渡 - Emperor Xuanzong visited Shu and arrived at Jibudu in Lizhou, a specific historical event.
    • 有一白魚來御舟而過 - A white fish swam past the imperial boat, a symbolic or auspicious event.
    • 河間王孝恭，才智識略時出於眾 - Prince Hejian Xianggong was exceptionally talented and wise, indicating his superior abilities.
    • 初受詔征蒲公┙ - When he first received the order to campaign against Pu Gongyu, a specific historical context.
    • 上有一器，倏然變成血 - There was a vessel on the table that suddenly turned into blood, a supernatural or ominous event.
    • 滿坐驚畏 - Everyone in the room was shocked and fearful, indicating the reaction to the event.
    • 左右不測 - No one knew what it meant, indicating the mystery surrounding the event.
    • 孝恭自省無負神祗 - Xianggong reflected that he had not wronged any gods or spirits, indicating his self-assurance.
    • 此變應是公┙ - This change must be related to Pu Gongyu, indicating his conclusion about the cause of the event.
    • 時人服其先見 - People at the time admired his foresight, indicating the recognition of his insight.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 隋劉君良 - Liu Junliang, a historical figure from the Sui Dynasty known for his filial piety and family harmony.
    • 大業末 - The end of Emperor Yang of Sui's reign, marked by political instability and social unrest.
    • 天下饑馑 - A period of widespread famine that contributed to the downfall of the Sui Dynasty.
    • 中宗 - Emperor Zhongzong of Tang, who was deposed and later restored to the throne.
    • 天后 - Empress Wu Zetian, the only female emperor in Chinese history.
    • 房陵 - A place where Emperor Zhongzong was exiled.
    • 玄宗 - Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, known for his reign during the golden age of the Tang Dynasty.
    • 蜀 - Shu, a region in southwestern China.
    • 利州吉伯渡 - A specific location in Lizhou, where a significant event occurred.
    • 河間王孝恭 - Prince Hejian Xianggong, a prominent military leader during the Tang Dynasty.
    • 蒲公┙ - Pu Gongyu, a rebel leader during the early Tang Dynasty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 隋劉君良 - Liu Junliang, a historical figure from the Sui Dynasty.
    • 大業末 - The end of Emperor Yang of Sui's reign.
    • 天下饑馑 - Widespread famine across the empire.
    • 中宗 - Emperor Zhongzong of Tang.
    • 天后 - Empress Wu Zetian.
    • 房陵 - A place where Emperor Zhongzong was exiled.
    • 玄宗 - Emperor Xuanzong of Tang.
    • 蜀 - Shu, a region in southwestern China.
    • 利州吉伯渡 - A specific location in Lizhou.
    • 河間王孝恭 - Prince Hejian Xianggong.
    • 蒲公┙ - Pu Gongyu, a rebel leader during the early Tang Dynasty.

    Liu Junliang of the Sui Dynasty lived with his family for generations, and he and his three brothers were united in spirit. At the end of the Great Prosperity era, famine swept across the land. His wife wanted to persuade them to separate, so she secretly took bird chicks from a tree and placed them in different nests, causing the birds to fight. The whole family found this strange. His wife said, "Nowadays, the world is in chaos, and it is a time of war. Even birds cannot coexist, let alone humans." Liu Junliang realized her plan and, at midnight, pulled out her hair and shouted, "This is the traitor who will ruin our family!" He called his brothers and wept, revealing her treachery. They then abandoned her, living in three separate houses but sharing one kitchen.

    Emperor Zhongzong of Tang was deposed by Empress Wu Zetian and exiled to Fangling. He looked up to heaven and sighed, then threw a stone into the sky, whispering to himself, "If I become emperor, this stone will not fall." The stone was caught by a tree branch and remains there to this day. Another person, while crossing a river to gather firewood, found an ancient mirror and presented it to the emperor. When Zhongzong looked into the mirror, a voice in the reflection said, "If you become emperor, if you become emperor." Within a few days, he ascended to the imperial throne.

    Xun Fengqian had a deep affection for his wife. In winter, when she was ill with a fever, he went out, sat in the open air, and waited until her body cooled before entering to warm her. His actions were greatly admired by the world.

    Emperor Xuanzong of Tang visited Shu and arrived at Jibudu in Lizhou, where a white fish swam past the imperial boat.

    Prince Hejian Xianggong was exceptionally talented and wise. When he first received the order to campaign against Pu Gongyu, there was a vessel on the table that suddenly turned into blood, shocking everyone in the room. No one knew what it meant. Xianggong reflected that he had not wronged any gods or spirits and concluded that this change must be related to Pu Gongyu. People at the time admired his foresight. [56]

    太公封于齐，宿于逆旅，主人晨起，有一人谓曰：“客寝甚甘，殆非就国者也。”太公蹶起即路。俄有追者至，以其出关，遂止。
    　　齐桓公与管仲谋伐莒，国人知之。桓公谓管仲曰：“寡人与仲父言，国人知之，何也？”管仲曰：“意者左右有圣人乎今东都牙安在”桓公顾曰：“在此。”管仲曰：“子何以知之”牙曰：“君子有三色，是以知之。”仲曰：“何谓三色”曰：“欢欣众悦，钟鼓之色；愁ヘ哀忧，衰之色；猛厉忠实，兵革之色。”仲曰：“何以知其莒也”曰：“君东面南面指之，口张而不掩，舌举而不下，是以知其莒也。”
    　　朱敬则，亳州末城人。孝友忠鲠，举世莫比。门表阙者六所，古今无之。
    　　公仪休相鲁国，入园，见妻莳葵，因拔去，谓妻曰：“身为国相，与民争利，非理也。”乃逐其妻。 [57]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [57]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 宿于逆旅 - Staying at an inn; a common expression for temporary lodging.
    • 客寝甚甘 - The guest slept very soundly; '甘' (sweet) is used metaphorically to describe a pleasant sleep.
    • 殆非就国者也 - Probably not someone who is going to take office; '殆' (probably) and '就国' (take office) are key components.
    • 蹶起即路 - Jumped up and headed out immediately; '蹶起' (jumped up) and '即路' (headed out) are vivid expressions.
    • 俄有追者至 - Soon someone came to chase him; '俄' (soon) and '追者' (chaser) are important.
    • 以其出关 - Because he had left the pass; '以其' (because) and '出关' (left the pass) are key phrases.
    • 齐桓公与管仲谋伐莒 - Duke Huan of Qi and Guan Zhong planned to attack Ju; '谋伐' (planned to attack) is a key phrase.
    • 国人知之 - The people knew about it; '国人' (the people) and '知之' (knew about it) are important.
    • 意者左右有圣人乎 - Perhaps there is a sage among your attendants?; '意者' (perhaps) and '圣人' (sage) are key terms.
    • 东都牙安在 - Where is the Eastern Capital's general?; '东都' (Eastern Capital) and '牙' (general) are important.
    • 君子有三色 - A gentleman has three expressions; '三色' (three expressions) is a key phrase.
    • 欢欣众悦，钟鼓之色 - The expression of joy and celebration, like the sound of bells and drums; '欢欣众悦' (joy and celebration) and '钟鼓之色' (expression of bells and drums) are vivid metaphors.
    • 愁哀忧，衰之色 - The expression of sorrow and mourning, like the sound of mourning music; '愁哀忧' (sorrow and mourning) and '衰之色' (expression of mourning music) are vivid metaphors.
    • 猛厉忠实，兵革之色 - The expression of sternness and loyalty, like the sound of war; '猛厉忠实' (sternness and loyalty) and '兵革之色' (expression of war) are vivid metaphors.
    • 何以知其莒也 - How do you know it's Ju?; '知其莒' (know it's Ju) is a key phrase.
    • 君东面南面指之 - When the king pointed east and south; '东面南面' (east and south) and '指之' (pointed) are important.
    • 口张而不掩，舌举而不下 - His mouth was open and his tongue was raised; '口张而不掩' (mouth open) and '舌举而不下' (tongue raised) are vivid descriptions.
    • 朱敬则，亳州末城人 - Zhu Jingze, from Mocheng, Bo Prefecture; '亳州末城' (Mocheng, Bo Prefecture) is a specific place name.
    • 孝友忠鲠，举世莫比 - Filial piety, kindness, loyalty, and integrity, unmatched in the world; '孝友忠鲠' (filial piety, kindness, loyalty, and integrity) and '举世莫比' (unmatched in the world) are key phrases.
    • 门表阙者六所 - Six of his family's doorposts were inscribed with official titles; '门表阙者' (doorposts inscribed) and '六所' (six) are important.
    • 公仪休相鲁国 - Gong Yi Hui served as chancellor of the State of Lu; '相鲁国' (served as chancellor of the State of Lu) is a key phrase.
    • 见妻莳葵 - Saw his wife planting melons; '莳葵' (planting melons) is a vivid description.
    • 身为国相，与民争利，非理也 - As a chancellor, competing with the people for profit is not right; '身为国相' (as a chancellor) and '与民争利' (competing with the people for profit) are key phrases.
    • 逐其妻 - He dismissed his wife; '逐' (dismissed) is a key term.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 太公 - Refers to Jiang Ziya (姜子牙), a legendary figure and advisor to King Wen and King Wu of Zhou.
    • 齐桓公 - Refers to Duke Huan of Qi (齐桓公), a famous ruler of the State of Qi during the Spring and Autumn period.
    • 管仲 - Refers to Guan Zhong, a famous statesman and advisor to Duke Huan of Qi.
    • 莒 - Refers to the State of Ju, a vassal state during the Spring and Autumn period.
    • 朱敬则 - Refers to Zhu Jingze, a Tang dynasty official known for his integrity and filial piety.
    • 亳州末城 - Refers to Mocheng, a town in Bo Prefecture, which is now part of Anhui Province.
    • 公仪休 - Refers to Gong Yi Hui, a chancellor of the State of Lu during the Spring and Autumn period.

    Proper Nouns

    • 宿于逆旅 - Staying at an inn.
    • 客寝甚甘 - The guest slept very soundly.
    • 殆非就国者也 - Probably not someone who is going to take office.
    • 蹶起即路 - Jumped up and headed out immediately.
    • 俄有追者至 - Soon someone came to chase him.
    • 以其出关 - Because he had left the pass.
    • 齐桓公与管仲谋伐莒 - Duke Huan of Qi and Guan Zhong planned to attack Ju.
    • 国人知之 - The people knew about it.
    • 意者左右有圣人乎 - Perhaps there is a sage among your attendants?
    • 东都牙安在 - Where is the Eastern Capital's general?
    • 君子有三色 - A gentleman has three expressions.
    • 欢欣众悦，钟鼓之色 - The expression of joy and celebration, like the sound of bells and drums.
    • 愁哀忧，衰之色 - The expression of sorrow and mourning, like the sound of mourning music.
    • 猛厉忠实，兵革之色 - The expression of sternness and loyalty, like the sound of war.
    • 何以知其莒也 - How do you know it's Ju?
    • 君东面南面指之 - When the king pointed east and south.
    • 口张而不掩，舌举而不下 - His mouth was open and his tongue was raised.
    • 朱敬则，亳州末城人 - Zhu Jingze, from Mocheng, Bo Prefecture.
    • 孝友忠鲠，举世莫比 - Filial piety, kindness, loyalty, and integrity, unmatched in the world.
    • 门表阙者六所 - Six of his family's doorposts were inscribed with official titles.
    • 公仪休相鲁国 - Gong Yi Hui served as chancellor of the State of Lu.
    • 见妻莳葵 - Saw his wife planting melons.
    • 身为国相，与民争利，非理也 - As a chancellor, competing with the people for profit is not right.
    • 逐其妻 - He dismissed his wife.

    Jiang Ziya was enfeoffed to the State of Qi and stayed at an inn. One morning, the innkeeper got up and a man said to him, "The guest slept very soundly, probably not someone who is going to take office." Jiang Ziya jumped up and headed out immediately. Soon, someone came to chase him because he had left the pass, and they stopped him.

    Duke Huan of Qi and Guan Zhong planned to attack the State of Ju, and the people of Qi knew about it. Duke Huan said to Guan Zhong, "I spoke with my father, and the people know about it, why is that?" Guan Zhong replied, "Perhaps there is a sage among your attendants? Where is the general of the Eastern Capital?" Duke Huan looked around and said, "He is here." Guan Zhong asked, "How do you know it's Ju?" The general replied, "A gentleman has three expressions, so I know." Guan Zhong asked, "What are the three expressions?" The general said, "The expression of joy and celebration, like the sound of bells and drums; the expression of sorrow and mourning, like the sound of mourning music; the expression of sternness and loyalty, like the sound of war." Guan Zhong asked, "How do you know it's Ju?" The general said, "When the king pointed east and south, his mouth was open and his tongue was raised, so I know it's Ju."

    Zhu Jingze was from Mocheng, Bo Prefecture. He was filial, kind, loyal, and had integrity, unmatched in the world. Six of his family's doorposts were inscribed with official titles, a feat never seen before or since.

    Gong Yi Hui served as chancellor of the State of Lu. One day, he entered a garden and saw his wife planting melons. He pulled them out and told her, "As a chancellor, competing with the people for profit is not right." He then dismissed her. [57]

    晋文公出伐卫，公子仰而笑。公问曰：“何笑”公子曰：“臣笑臣邻人也。臣之邻人有送其妻适私家者，道逢桑妇而悦，与之言；然顾视其妻，亦有招之者矣。是以窃笑之。”公悟其言，乃止，引兵而还。未到，有伐其北鄙者。
    　　昔有人海上日与鸥鸟狎，引数百相从。其父曰：“吾闻鸥鸟从汝游，可与俱来。吾玩之。”明日，其人往，群鸥翔而不下，盖以机萌于心而物惧也。
    　　姚泓将妻子降于刘，斩之于建康市。凡百里之内，草皆焦而死。
    　　符坚委政于王猛，小大无疑。猛卒，其子皮谋反。坚让曰：“丞相临终，以十具牛为田，不闻与子求位。知子莫若父，何斯言之验也。”赦而不诛。
    　　唐仪凤中，中书舍人欧阳通起复判馆，每人朝，必徒跣至城门，然后着鞋。到直省之所，即席地籍藁，非公事不言，未尝启齿。归家必衣衰，号恸无时。国朝夺情，惟通得理。 [58]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [58]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 晋文公出伐卫 - Military campaign by Duke Wen of Jin against the state of Wei.
    • 公子仰而笑 - Reaction to something seen or heard.
    • 臣笑臣邻人也 - Idiomatic expression indicating mockery or amusement at someone else's situation.
    • 臣之邻人有送其妻适私家者 - Neighbor's wife was leaving for a different residence.
    • 道逢桑妇而悦，与之言 - Chance encounter and subsequent interaction.
    • 然顾视其妻，亦有招之者矣 - Both the neighbor and his wife were being pursued by others.
    • 是以窃笑之 - Reason for the marquis's laughter.
    • 公悟其言，乃止，引兵而还 - Duke's realization and decision to withdraw.
    • 未到，有伐其北鄙者 - Unexpected event during their return journey.
    • 昔有人海上日与鸥鸟狎 - Narrative about a man's relationship with birds.
    • 引数百相从 - Man's ability to attract a large number of birds.
    • 其父曰 - Introduction of a dialogue between the man and his father.
    • 吾闻鸥鸟从汝游，可与俱来。吾玩之 - Father's curiosity and desire to see the birds.
    • 明日，其人往，群鸥翔而不下 - Birds' reaction to the man's father.
    • 盖以机萌于心而物惧也 - Explanation of the birds' behavior.
    • 姚泓将妻子降于刘 - Historical event involving a surrender.
    • 斩之于建康市 - Execution of Yao Hong.
    • 凡百里之内，草皆焦而死 - Aftermath of the execution.
    • 符坚委政于王猛 - Historical figure delegating authority.
    • 小大无疑 - Trust placed in Wang Meng.
    • 猛卒，其子皮谋反 - Conflict after Wang Meng's death.
    • 坚让曰 - Dialogue between Fu Jian and Wang Meng's son.
    • 丞相临终，以十具牛为田，不闻与子求位 - Wang Meng's final wishes.
    • 知子莫若父，何斯言之验也 - Fu Jian's understanding of Wang Meng's intentions.
    • 赦而不诛 - Fu Jian's decision to show mercy.
    • 唐仪凤中，中书舍人欧阳通起复判馆 - Historical appointment.
    • 每人朝，必徒跣至城门，然后着鞋 - Ouyang Tong's ritualistic behavior.
    • 到直省之所，即席地籍藁，非公事不言，未尝启齿 - Ouyang Tong's strict adherence to protocol.
    • 归家必衣衰，号恸无时 - Ouyang Tong's grief and mourning practices.
    • 国朝夺情，惟通得理 - Court's recognition of Ouyang Tong's situation.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋文公 - Duke Wen of Jin, a prominent ruler during the Spring and Autumn period.
    • 卫 - The state of Wei, one of the major states during the Spring and Autumn period.
    • 姚泓 - Historical figure who surrendered to Liu Jiu.
    • 刘 - Liu Jiu, a historical figure who executed Yao Hong.
    • 建康市 - The market of Jiankang, a city in present-day Nanjing.
    • 符坚 - Fu Jian, a ruler of the Former Qin dynasty.
    • 王猛 - Wang Meng, a trusted advisor and chancellor of Fu Jian.
    • 唐仪凤中 - The Yifeng period of the Tang dynasty.
    • 中书舍人 - A high-ranking official in the Tang dynasty's central government.
    • 欧阳通 - Ouyang Tong, a historical figure known for his strict adherence to mourning rituals.
    • 夺情 - Practice where officials were allowed to continue their mourning duties despite official duties.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • Careful translation required to maintain context and flow in sentences describing military campaigns, reactions, narratives, and historical events.

    Duke Wen of Jin went out to attack Wei, and the marquis looked up and laughed. The duke asked, "Why are you laughing?" The marquis replied, "I am laughing at my neighbor. My neighbor had a wife who was going to her private home. On the way, he met a mulberry woman and was pleased with her, speaking to her; but when he looked back at his wife, he saw that she too was being called. That is why I secretly laughed at him." The duke understood his words and stopped, leading his troops back. Before they arrived, there was an attack on the northern border.

    Once upon a time, there was a man by the sea who daily played with seagulls, bringing hundreds of them along. His father said, "I heard that seagulls follow you, so you can bring them along. I want to play with them." The next day, the man went, and the seagulls flew around but did not land. Because the birds sensed his intention and were afraid.

    Yao Hong surrendered his wife and children to Liu Jiu, who executed him in the market of Jiankang. Within a hundred miles, the grass all withered and died.

    Fu Jian entrusted his government to Wang Meng, with no doubt in matters big or small. When Wang Meng died, his son Pi plotted a rebellion. Fu Jian said, "The Chancellor at his deathbed, left ten oxen for the fields, but did not hear him ask for a position for his son. No one knows a son better than his father; how true these words are." He pardoned him instead of executing him.

    During the Yifeng period of the Tang dynasty, the Shangshu Lang Ouyang Tong was reinstated as judge of the Hall. Every time he went to court, he would walk barefoot to the city gate, then put on his shoes. When he reached the office, he would sit on the ground and use straw as a mat, speaking only about official matters and never opening his mouth otherwise. When he returned home, he would always wear mourning clothes and wail without a fixed time. The court took pity on him and allowed him to continue his mourning duties. [58]

    汉宣帝足下有毛，所居常有光耀。
    　　晋颜含有孝行。兄几服药过多，死于家。含遂开棺，复生。母妻家人尽勤倦，含弃绝人事，侍兄疾十三年，曾无劳怠。
    　　宋燕相齐，见逐罢归，召门尉陈饶等二十三人，曰：“诸大夫有能与我赴诸侯乎”饶等皆伏而不对。燕曰：“悲乎哉！士大夫易得而难用。”陈饶对曰：“非士大夫易得而难用，君不能用也。君不能用，即有不平之心，是先诸己而责诸人。”燕曰：“先诸己而责诸人，其云何？”饶曰：“三斗之粟，不足于士，而君雁鹜有余食；园果梨栗，后宫妇女以相携掷，而士不得一尝；绫纨绮，丽靡于常服，而士大夫不得以为禄。财者，君之所轻；死者，士之所重。君不能捐其所轻而使士致其所重，譬若铅刀蓄之，干将用之，不亦难乎！”宋燕惭而避席，曰：“燕过矣。” [59]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [59]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 足下有毛，所居常有光耀 - Descriptive phrase indicating that the person has hair on their feet and emits a radiant glow where they live.
    • 孝行 - Refers to filial piety, a core virtue in Confucian ethics.
    • 開棺復生 - Miraculous event where someone is brought back to life after being in a coffin.
    • 棄絕人事 - Means to completely withdraw from social affairs, indicating a deep commitment to personal duty.
    • 赴諸侯 - Means to join another lord or ruler, often in the context of seeking new opportunities or patronage.
    • 伏而不對 - Means to remain silent and not respond, indicating reluctance or fear.
    • 易得而難用 - Means that something is easy to obtain but difficult to use effectively.
    • 先諸己而責諸人 - Means to first examine oneself before criticizing others, a principle of self-reflection.
    • 三斗之粟 - Specific quantity of grain, used here to illustrate a point about the value of resources.
    • 雁鹜有余食 - Refers to the abundance of food for geese and ducks, symbolizing the luxury of the ruler.
    • 園果梨栗 - Refers to fruits and nuts from the garden, symbolizing the ruler's wealth.
    • 麗靡於常服 - Means to wear luxurious clothing, indicating the ruler's extravagance.
    • 財者，君之所輕 - Means that wealth is something the ruler takes lightly, suggesting a lack of appreciation for it.
    • 死者，士之所重 - Means that death is something the scholar takes seriously, emphasizing the value of life and honor.
    • 鉛刀蓄之，干將用之 - Metaphor comparing a dull knife to a sharp one, suggesting that the ruler's lack of appreciation for resources makes it difficult for scholars to achieve their goals.
    • 燕過矣 - Means that the ruler has made a mistake, acknowledging the criticism.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 漢宣帝 - Refers to Emperor Xuan of Han, the eighth emperor of the Western Han dynasty.
    • 顏含 - Refers to Yan Han, a famous scholar and official during the Jin dynasty.
    • 宋燕 - Refers to Song Yan, a historical figure known for his political career and the events described in the text.
    • 門尉 - Refers to a gatekeeper or guard, a low-ranking official.
    • 諸侯 - Refers to feudal lords or rulers in the feudal system.
    • 士大夫 - Refers to scholars and officials, a class of educated and influential people.
    • 雁鹜 - Refers to geese and ducks, often used symbolically in classical literature.
    • 干將 - Refers to a legendary swordsmith, often used metaphorically to denote a skilled craftsman.

    Proper Nouns

    • 漢宣帝 - Emperor Xuan of Han.
    • 顏含 - Yan Han.
    • 宋燕 - Song Yan.
    • 門尉 - Gatekeeper or guard.
    • 諸侯 - Feudal lords or rulers.
    • 士大夫 - Scholars and officials.
